# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Consortium Blockchain with a Suitable Example. **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Consortium Blockchain** is a semi-decentralized, permissioned distributed ledger whose consensus process is controlled by a pre-selected group of trusted nodes, typically representing multiple organizations or institutions.

**Key Features:**
- **Federated Validation:** A group of pre-approved organizations (e.g., a consortium of 10 banks) act as validators rather than a single central authority.
- **Higher Throughput:** Since the validator set is limited and known, consensus can be reached much faster than in public chains.
- **Partial Decentralization:** Balances the trust assumptions of a private chain with the distributed governance of a public chain.
- **Identified Participants:** All members have verified real-world identities.

**Example:** **R3 Corda** — a distributed ledger platform designed for financial services, used by a consortium of banks for inter-bank settlements and trade finance. Another example is **Hyperledger Besu**, used in enterprise consortiums.

---

### Question 2: List Any Three Properties of a Valid Consensus Protocol. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three fundamental properties of a valid consensus protocol are:

1. **Agreement (Safety):** All honest (non-faulty) nodes in the network must agree on the same value or state. No two honest nodes should ever decide on conflicting values, ensuring a single, consistent chain.

2. **Validity:** The value that is ultimately agreed upon must be one of the values proposed by an honest participant in the network. This prevents a malicious node from injecting arbitrary or invalid data into the consensus outcome.

3. **Termination (Liveness):** Every honest node must eventually reach a decision and produce a result. The protocol must not stall indefinitely; new blocks must keep being added to the chain.

*(Additional property for bonus: Fault Tolerance — the protocol must remain correct even if up to f nodes behave maliciously, where n ≥ 3f + 1 for BFT.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often confuse **Consortium** with **Private** blockchain. Remember: Private = single organization, Consortium = group of organizations.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Writing only definitions without diagrams in consensus questions leads to a **2-mark deduction**. Always include at least one flow diagram.
> - **Pitfall 3:** For PoW, students forget to mention the **difficulty adjustment algorithm** (2016-block recalibration). This is a frequently asked 2-mark sub-question.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Confusing **CFT (n ≥ 2f + 1)** with **BFT (n ≥ 3f + 1)** bound — examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a): Explain the Different Types of Blockchain in Detail with Their Characteristics and Use Cases. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Public Blockchain (2 marks):**
A public blockchain is a permissionless, fully decentralized ledger where any participant can read, write, and validate transactions without prior authorization. It is secured by economic incentives (mining rewards, staking rewards) and cryptographic consensus (PoW, PoS).
- **Characteristics:** Open access, anonymity/pseudonymity, full transparency, high decentralization, low TPS, high energy consumption.
- **Use Cases:** Cryptocurrencies (Bitcoin, Ethereum), decentralized finance (DeFi), NFTs, public voting.
- **Example:** Bitcoin — anyone can download the Bitcoin client, become a node, and start transacting.

**2. Private Blockchain (2 marks):**
A private blockchain is a permissioned ledger controlled by a single organization that determines who can read, write, and validate. It offers high throughput and privacy at the cost of centralization.
- **Characteristics:** Restricted access, identity-bound, centralized governance, high TPS, low energy use, no native token typically.
- **Use Cases:** Internal audit trails, asset tracking within a corporation, internal record management.
- **Example:** Hyperledger Fabric (deployed within a single enterprise for supply chain tracking).

**3. Consortium Blockchain (1.5 marks):**
A consortium blockchain is a semi-decentralized, permissioned ledger governed by a group of pre-selected organizations. It balances the trust of a private chain with the distribution of a public chain.
- **Characteristics:** Federated validation, known participants, moderate decentralization, high TPS, legally binding governance.
- **Use Cases:** Inter-bank settlements, cross-border payments, healthcare data exchange between hospitals.
- **Example:** R3 Corda used by a consortium of banks for syndicated loan management.

**4. Hybrid Blockchain (1.5 marks):**
A hybrid blockchain combines a private chain (for sensitive business logic) with a public chain (for verifiable anchoring). It allows selective transparency.
- **Characteristics:** Dual-layer architecture, configurable privacy, cross-chain bridges, partial decentralization.
- **Use Cases:** Government identity systems, supply chain with public auditability, intellectual property registries.
- **Example:** Dragonchain — used by Disney for managing internal assets with public verifiability.

---

#### **Part (b): With a Neat Diagram, Explain the Proof of Work (PoW) Consensus Mechanism. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Proof of Work (PoW) is a consensus mechanism where miners compete to solve a computationally intensive cryptographic puzzle. The first miner to find a valid solution gets the right to append the next block to the chain and is rewarded with newly minted cryptocurrency and transaction fees.

**Working Steps (4 marks):**

1. **Transaction Collection:** Miners gather pending transactions from the mempool (memory pool) and assemble them into a candidate block.

2. **Merkle Root Construction:** All transaction hashes are pairwise hashed in a binary tree (Merkle tree) to produce a single Merkle root, which is included in the block header.

3. **Block Header Assembly:** The block header contains: previous block hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty target, and a variable field called the **nonce**.

4. **Hash Puzzle:** The miner computes:
   $$H(\text{Header} \Vert \text{Nonce})$$
   and checks if the resulting hash is less than the target value. The target is a 256-bit number derived from the current network difficulty.

5. **Nonce Iteration:** Since SHA-256 is a one-way function, miners must brute-force the nonce (and optionally extra nonce in coinbase). Average attempts in Bitcoin: ~$10^{20}$ hashes per block.

6. **Block Broadcast:** Upon finding a valid nonce, the miner broadcasts the block to the network. Other nodes verify the solution in **O(1)** time (single hash check) and accept the block.

7. **Reward Distribution:** The successful miner receives the **block subsidy** (currently 3.125 BTC post-2024 halving) plus transaction fees.

**Difficulty Adjustment (1 mark):**
Every 2016 blocks (~2 weeks), Bitcoin recalibrates the difficulty to maintain a 10-minute block time, using the formula:
$$D_{\text{new}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600}$$

**Diagram (1 mark):** (Refer to the PoW Mining Flow diagram in Section 4.3)

**Advantages:** Sybil-resistant, battle-tested (since 2009), provably secure.
**Disadvantages:** Energy-intensive, low throughput (~7 TPS), 51% attack theoretically possible with majority hashrate.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a): Define Consensus in Blockchain. Explain the Byzantine Generals Problem and How BFT Solves It. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition of Consensus (1 mark):**
Consensus in blockchain is the protocol by which distributed, mutually untrusting nodes agree on a single, consistent state of the ledger despite the presence of faulty or malicious actors. It ensures **safety** (no conflicting states) and **liveness** (the chain keeps progressing).

**The Byzantine Generals Problem (3 marks):**
Introduced by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease in **1982**, the Byzantine Generals Problem is a logical dilemma describing the difficulty of achieving consensus in a distributed system where some participants may be unreliable or malicious.

**Scenario:** Several divisions of the Byzantine army surround an enemy city. The generals must agree on a unified battle plan (attack or retreat) via messenger-based communication. Some generals may be **traitors** who send conflicting messages to different loyal generals, causing them to disagree on the action.

**In Blockchain Terms:**
- **Generals** = Nodes (validators/miners)
- **Messengers** = Network communication (which can be delayed, lost, or forged)
- **Traitors** = Malicious/Byzantine nodes
- **Consensus** = Agreement on the next valid block

**The Core Challenge:** With $f$ traitors, we need enough honest nodes to outvote and outlast the traitors' coordinated dishonesty.

**How BFT Solves It (3 marks):**

The **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)** bound establishes the minimum number of nodes required:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

This means even if up to **f** nodes are malicious and send conflicting messages, the remaining **n - f ≥ 2f + 1** honest nodes can still form a strict supermajority and reach consensus.

**Practical Implementation: PBFT (Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance)**
PBFT (Castro & Liskov, 1999) achieves BFT through a three-phase commit protocol:

1. **Pre-Prepare:** The primary (leader) proposes a block ordering.
2. **Prepare:** Replicas broadcast their agreement; the block is considered "prepared" when **2f + 1** replicas agree.
3. **Commit:** Replicas commit to the prepared block; the block is finalized when **2f + 1** commit messages are received.

**Application:** Hyperledger Fabric, Tendermint (used in Cosmos), and other enterprise blockchains use BFT-based consensus.

---

#### **Part (b): Compare Proof of Work (PoW), Proof of Stake (PoS), and Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) Consensus Mechanisms. [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Consensus mechanisms are the heart of any blockchain, ensuring all nodes agree on the ledger's state. The three mechanisms compared here represent fundamentally different philosophies: computational, economic, and identity-based.

**Proof of Work (PoW) — 2 marks:**
- **Principle:** Miners expend computational energy to solve a hash puzzle. The first to find a valid nonce wins the block reward.
- **Block Production:** Probabilistic, ~10 minutes in Bitcoin.
- **Security:** Economic — a 51% attack requires controlling more than half the global hashrate, costing billions of dollars in hardware and electricity.
- **Finality:** Probabilistic — blocks are considered final after ~6 confirmations (~60 minutes).
- **Energy:** Extremely high (estimated 0.5% of global electricity).
- **Example:** Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin (pre-merge Ethereum).

**Proof of Stake (PoS) — 2 marks:**
- **Principle:** Validators lock up ("stake") native tokens as collateral. A pseudo-random selection process picks a validator to produce the next block, weighted by stake size.
- **Block Production:** Deterministic slot-based, ~12 seconds in Ethereum.
- **Security:** Economic — attackers must acquire and risk losing 51% of the staked tokens. Misbehavior results in **slashing** (forfeiture of stake).
- **Finality:** Economic finality — reverting a finalized block requires burning at least 1/3 of the total staked ETH (~$30+ billion).
- **Energy:** Over 99.95% lower than PoW.
- **Example:** Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano (Ouroboros), Polkadot (Nominated PoS).

**Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) — 2 marks:**
- **Principle:** A pre-selected set of known validators reaches consensus via a three-phase voting protocol (Pre-Prepare, Prepare, Commit).
- **Block Production:** Immediate finality after the commit phase.
- **Security:** Cryptographic + Identity — validators are known and bound by legal agreements; the protocol tolerates up to $\lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ malicious nodes.
- **Finality:** Deterministic — once committed, the block is final and cannot be reverted without colluding 2/3+ of validators.
- **Scalability:** O($n^2$) message complexity — limited to small validator sets (typically <50 nodes).
- **Example:** Hyperledger Fabric, Zilliqa (modified), Tendermint Core.

**Comparative Summary (1 mark):**
- **PoW** is the most decentralized and battle-tested but energy-intensive and slow.
- **PoS** balances decentralization with energy efficiency and is the modern trend for public chains.
- **PBFT** offers instant finality and high throughput but sacrifices decentralization — best suited for permissioned enterprise use cases.

**Selection Criteria:**
- **Public chain + maximum security** → PoW (historically) / PoS (modern)
- **Public chain + sustainability** → PoS
- **Enterprise / Consortium** → PBFT / PoA
- **Hybrid** → Tendermint, HotStuff

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### **A. Types of Blockchain (4 types to remember)**
- ☐ **Public** → Permissionless, anonymous, low TPS, high decentralization (Bitcoin, Ethereum).
- ☐ **Private** → Single organization controls, high TPS, centralized (Hyperledger Fabric).
- ☐ **Consortium** → Group of organizations govern, semi-decentralized, used in banking (Corda, Quorum).
- ☐ **Hybrid** → Combines private + public with selective transparency (Dragonchain).
- ☐ Always remember the **trade-off triangle:** Decentralization ↔ Security ↔ Scalability.

### **B. Consensus — Definition & Properties**
- ☐ Consensus = Agreement + Validity + Termination + Fault Tolerance.
- ☐ Solves the **Byzantine Generals Problem** (Lamport et al., 1982).
- ☐ **BFT bound:** $n \geq 3f + 1$ (NOT $2f+1$, which is CFT).
- ☐ **FLP Impossibility:** Deterministic consensus impossible in pure asynchrony with one fault.

### **C. Consensus Types (Memorize the 5 most important)**
- ☐ **PoW** — Hashrate-based, energy-intensive, probabilistic finality, 51% attack protection via cost.
- ☐ **PoS** — Stake-based, slashing mechanism, economic finality, energy-efficient.
- ☐ **DPoS** — Delegated voting, very high TPS, partial centralization.
- ☐ **PBFT** — Three-phase voting (Pre-Prepare → Prepare → Commit), immediate finality, O($n^2$).
- ☐ **PoA** — Identity-based, suitable for permissioned chains, no mining.

### **D. Key Numerical Facts to Remember**
- ☐ Bitcoin block time = **10 minutes**, Ethereum = **12 seconds**.
- ☐ Bitcoin difficulty adjusts every **2016 blocks (~2 weeks)**, max **4x change**.
- ☐ PBFT requires **2f + 1 matching messages** in Prepare AND Commit phases.
- ☐ PoS Ethereum finality = **~12.8 minutes (2 epochs × 32 slots × 12s)**.
- ☐ Mining probability = $p = q/h$; Expected time = $(h/q) \times T_b$.

### **E. Common Exam Traps**
- ☐ Distinguish **Permissioned** vs **Permissionless**.
- ☐ **Probabilistic finality** (PoW) vs **Deterministic finality** (PBFT, Tendermint).
- ☐ Don't confuse **blockchain type** (Public/Private) with **consensus type** (PoW/PoS).
- ☐ Remember: A public chain CAN use PBFT (e.g., Stellar), and a private chain CAN use PoW (rarely).

### **F. KTU-2024 Specific High-Yield Topics**
- ☐ Comparative table of blockchain types (high probability of a 7-mark question).
- ☐ PoW mining flow with diagram (favourite 7-mark question).
- ☐ Byzantine fault tolerance derivation $n \geq 3f + 1$ (numerical problems possible).
- ☐ Difficulty adjustment formula (5-mark sub-question favourite).
- ☐ Differences between PoW, PoS, and PBFT (comparison question — 7 marks).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always draw at least **one labeled diagram** in any consensus question. A well-drawn flow diagram can fetch **1–2 extra marks** even if your textual answer is average. KTU examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decentralization - Decentralization Using Blockchain, Methods of Decentralization, Routes to Decentralization, Blockchain and Full Ecosystem Decentralization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Decentralization - Decentralization Using Blockchain, Methods of Decentralization, Routes to Decentralization, Blockchain and Full Ecosystem Decentralization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Decentralization?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> **Decentralization** is the architectural and governance paradigm in which authority, decision-making, control, and data storage are distributed across multiple autonomous nodes in a peer-to-peer (P2P) network rather than being concentrated in a single central authority, intermediary, or trusted third party. In the context of blockchain, decentralization is achieved through cryptographic protocols, distributed consensus algorithms, and economic incentive mechanisms that collectively eliminate single points of failure.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, decentralization is not just a *technical* state but a **multi-dimensional property** of a system that touches architecture (how nodes connect), politics (who controls the nodes), and logic (how a single state of the system is agreed upon).

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Foundational Dimensions of Decentralization (Web3 Foundation / KTU Module 1 Standard)**
> 1. **Architectural Decentralization** – How many physical computers is the system made up of? How many of those computers can fail independently?
> 2. **Political Decentralization** – How many individuals or organizations ultimately control those computers?
> 3. **Logical Decentralization** – Does the system present itself as a single coherent monolith, or as an amorphous swarm with no single discernible state?

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **village well** versus a **private water tanker**:

- **Centralized System (Water Tanker)**: One private company owns the tanker. They decide water quality, timing, pricing. If the owner decides to stop service or is bribed, **everyone suffers**. This is a **single point of failure**.

- **Decentralized System (Village Well)**: The well belongs to the village community. Any villager can draw water. A record-keeper (analogous to a blockchain ledger) maintains a transparent log. No single person can deny access or corrupt the records unilaterally, because the rules are encoded and visible to all.

> [!TIP]
> **Think of blockchain decentralization like a public bulletin board in a college campus.** Anyone can post a notice, but once posted, no one can erase or alter it. The board isn't owned by any single professor or department — the *rules of posting* (the protocol) govern everyone equally.

### 1.3 Decentralization Using Blockchain

Blockchain is the **first practical technology** that simultaneously achieves all three dimensions of decentralization at scale. It does so by combining:

| Component | Role in Decentralization |
|---|---|
| **Distributed Ledger** | Replicated state across all nodes |
| **Consensus Protocol** | Agreement without central authority |
| **Cryptographic Hashing** | Tamper-evidence without a notary |
| **Economic Incentives** | Aligns self-interest with network health |
| **P2P Network** | Removes need for central servers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Definition**: *Decentralization using blockchain* refers to the redistribution of trust and authority from centralized intermediaries (banks, governments, corporations) to a cryptographically secured, consensus-driven, peer-to-peer network where every participant maintains an identical copy of the shared ledger.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Centralization Problem (Why Decentralize?)

Traditional systems suffer from the **Centralization Trilemma** of:

1. **Single Point of Failure (SPOF)** — Crash one server, lose the entire service.
2. **Trust Dependency** — Users must *blindly* trust the intermediary.
3. **Censorship & Manipulation** — A central authority can block, reverse, or alter transactions.

Blockchain eliminates these by enforcing **trust-minimization**: instead of trusting a person, you trust mathematics and code.

### 2.2 Methods of Decentralization (KTU Module 1 Core Sub-topic)

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, **Methods of Decentralization** refers to the *technical and structural mechanisms* by which a system can distribute control. The four primary methods are:

#### Method 1: Decentralizing the Database (Storage Decentralization)
- Instead of one server hosting the database, every node in the network holds a **full replica**.
- New transactions are broadcast to all nodes (gossip protocol).
- Each node independently verifies and stores the transaction.
- **Result**: No single node "owns" the data; data loss at one node ≠ data loss globally.

#### Method 2: Decentralizing Computation (Compute Decentralization)
- Work (transaction validation, smart contract execution) is split across nodes.
- Examples: **Validator nodes** in Proof of Stake, **Miners** in Proof of Work.
- **Result**: No single entity processes all transactions; computational power is shared.

#### Method 3: Decentralizing Authority (Governance Decentralization)
- Decision-making about protocol upgrades, parameter changes, and dispute resolution is distributed.
- Mechanisms: **DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations)**, on-chain voting, off-chain signaling (e.g., EIPs in Ethereum).
- **Result**: No single CEO/board can unilaterally alter the rules.

#### Method 4: Decentralizing Identity (Identity Decentralization)
- Users own their identity via **self-sovereign identity (SSI)** and cryptographic key pairs (public/private keys).
- No central authority issues or revokes your identity.
- Examples: **DID (Decentralized Identifiers)**, ENS (Ethereum Name Service).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight**: When asked to "list methods of decentralization," students often only mention "distributed database." This is incomplete. A high-scoring answer must cover **at least four methods**: storage, computation, governance, and identity.

### 2.3 Routes to Decentralization (How to Get There)

While *methods* describe *what* is decentralized, **routes** describe *how* a system transitions from centralized to decentralized. The KTU module identifies **five canonical routes**:

| Route # | Route Name | Mechanism | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Mining / Forks** | Earn influence through computational work; soft/hard forks as governance events | Bitcoin, early Ethereum |
| 2 | **Trading** | Liquidity provision & token exchange decentralized exchanges | Uniswap, SushiSwap |
| 3 | **Voting** | Stake-weighted or coin-weighted on-chain governance | Tezos, MakerDAO, Dash |
| 4 | **Protocol Evolution** | Continuous development by distributed core teams | Bitcoin Core, Ethereum Foundation |
| 5 | **Delegation / Reputation** | Liquid democracy; delegate votes to experts | Steemit, Polkadot (NPoS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Routes vs Methods – Common KTU Confusion Alert**
> - **Methods** = *WHAT* is being decentralized (database, computation, authority, identity)
> - **Routes** = *HOW* decentralization is achieved/transitioned (mining, trading, voting, evolution, delegation)
>
> Examiners frequently test this distinction. Memorize it.

### 2.4 Blockchain and Full Ecosystem Decentralization

A **full ecosystem decentralization** is achieved only when *every layer* of the blockchain stack is decentralized. The KTU Module 1 framework breaks the ecosystem into **five layers**:

1. **Hardware Layer** — Internet, nodes, mining rigs (decentralized by geography)
2. **Data Layer** — The blockchain ledger itself (decentralized by replication)
3. **Network Layer** — P2P protocol (decentralized by mesh topology)
4. **Consensus Layer** — Agreement mechanism (decentralized by validator distribution)
5. **Application / Interface Layer** — dApps, wallets (decentralized by open-source development)

> [!TIP]
> **Full Ecosystem Decentralization = Decentralization at ALL five layers.** Most blockchains are only *partially* decentralized (e.g., mining pools concentrate consensus power, or core developers concentrate governance power).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are critical for numerical problems on decentralization metrics, Byzantine fault tolerance, and consensus probability.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Nakamoto Coefficient} &= \text{minimum number of entities} \\
&\quad \text{required to collude to attack the network}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sybil Resistance Score} &= \frac{\text{Cost to forge one identity}}{\text{Cost of one legitimate identity}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{honest majority}} &\geq 1 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $f$ = number of faulty/malicious nodes tolerated
- For **Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)**, the system remains safe if $f < \frac{n}{3}$ where $n$ is the total number of validators.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BFT Safety Threshold:} \quad n &\geq 3f + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PoW Hash Power Decentralization Index} &= 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $p_i$ is the fraction of total hash power held by mining pool $i$ (this is the **Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)** adapted for blockchain).

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Nakamoto Coefficient | $\min\{k \mid \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i \geq 0.5\}$ | Higher = more decentralized |
| HHI Index | $\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i^{2}$ | Lower = more decentralized |
| BFT Safety | $n \geq 3f+1$ | Fault tolerance bound |
| Gini Coefficient | $G = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \vert x_i - x_j \vert}{2n^{2}\bar{x}}$ | Token distribution inequality |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Byzantine Fault Tolerance Safety Threshold

The classic Byzantine Generals Problem asks: *how many traitors can a system of generals tolerate while still reaching consensus?*

**Step 1: State the Problem**
Suppose there are $n$ generals (validators) in total, of which $f$ are Byzantine (malicious/traitorous) and $n - f$ are loyal (honest).

**Step 2: Establish the Voting Requirement**
Each general sends a vote (transaction/prepared message). To overcome $f$ traitors, the loyal generals must be able to outvote them.

**Step 3: Apply the Quorum Intersection Argument**
For two quorums of loyal generals to share at least one honest member (required for consistency), we need:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 + Q_2 &\geq n + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

where $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ are quorum sizes.

**Step 4: Set Quorum Size**
To withstand $f$ faults, the quorum must include all honest nodes and still have margin:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 = Q_2 &= n - f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Substitute into the Intersection Equation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(n - f) + (n - f) &\geq n + 1 \\
2n - 2f &\geq n + 1 \\
n - 2f &\geq 1 \\
n &\geq 2f + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Adjust for Digital Signatures and Oral Messages (Lamport-Shostak-Pease Theorem)**
With cryptographic signatures, the bound tightens because traitors cannot forge loyal generals' votes. The general BFT bound becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &\geq 3f + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Solve for $f$ (Maximum Tolerable Faults)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{n - 1}{3} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style)**: If $n = 10$ validators, then $f_{\max} = \lfloor (10-1)/3 \rfloor = 3$. So **3 traitors** can be tolerated, and consensus still holds with **7 honest** nodes.

### 3.2 Derivation: Probability of Successful 51% Attack

Suppose an attacker controls fraction $q$ of the total hash power. The honest network controls $(1 - q)$.

**Step 1: Define Honest Chain Growth Rate**
The honest chain grows one block every $10$ minutes on average in Bitcoin. Let $t$ = number of blocks honest chain has lead.

**Step 2: Define Attacker's Catch-up Probability**
The probability that an attacker catches up from a deficit of $z$ blocks (binomial / Poisson approximation, Satoshi Nakamoto's 2008 derivation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{catch up}) &= 
\begin{cases}
1, & \text{if } q \geq 0.5 \\
\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^{k} e^{-\lambda}}{k!} \left(1 - \left(\frac{q}{1-q}\right)^{z-k}\right), & \text{if } q < 0.5
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\lambda = z \cdot \frac{q}{1-q}$.

**Step 3: Compute the Cumulative Probability**
For typical values, if attacker has $q = 0.1$ (10% hash power) and $z = 6$ confirmations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= 6 \cdot \frac{0.1}{0.9} = 0.667 \\
P(\text{catch up}) &\approx \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(0.667)^{k} e^{-0.667}}{k!} \left(1 - \left(\frac{0.1}{0.9}\right)^{6-k}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Numerical Evaluation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\text{catch up}) &\approx 0.0009 \\
&\approx 0.09\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation**: With just $10\%$ hash power and $6$ block confirmations, the attacker's probability of reversing a transaction is less than **0.1%**. This is why Bitcoin's 6-confirmation rule is considered secure even with imperfect decentralization.

### 3.3 Derivation: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) for Hash Power

Suppose 4 mining pools have hash power shares: $p_1 = 0.4$, $p_2 = 0.3$, $p_3 = 0.2$, $p_4 = 0.1$.

**Step 1: Recall HHI Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HHI} &= \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Each Squared Term**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1^{2} &= (0.4)^{2} = 0.16 \\
p_2^{2} &= (0.3)^{2} = 0.09 \\
p_3^{2} &= (0.2)^{2} = 0.04 \\
p_4^{2} &= (0.1)^{2} = 0.01
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Sum the Squared Shares**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HHI} &= 0.16 + 0.09 + 0.04 + 0.01 = 0.30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret Decentralization Index**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{\text{HHI}} &= 1 - \text{HHI} = 1 - 0.30 = 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note**: HHI ranges from $0$ (perfectly decentralized — infinitely many tiny pools) to $1$ (perfectly centralized — one pool has $100\%$). A blockchain is considered *healthy* if HHI $< 0.25$.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Methods × Routes × Ecosystem Layers

| Ecosystem Layer | Decentralization Method | Route to Achieve | Current State (e.g., Ethereum) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardware** | Geographic distribution of nodes | Mining / Staking | Mostly decentralized |
| **Data (Ledger)** | Full node replication | Forks / Open-source clients | Decentralized |
| **Network (P2P)** | Mesh topology | DevP2P, libp2p | Decentralized |
| **Consensus** | Validator distribution | Staking (PoS) | Moderately centralized (Lido, exchanges) |
| **Application** | Open-source dApps | Token launches, DAOs | Partially centralized (still many Web2 frontends) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Centralized vs Decentralized Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```
