## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`**  
**Define decentralization. List any THREE dimensions of decentralization as per the KTU Module 1 framework.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** Decentralization is the redistribution of authority, control, and data across a peer-to-peer network, eliminating dependence on a central intermediary. It enables trustless coordination through cryptographic protocols and distributed consensus.
>
> **Three Dimensions (2 marks):**
> 1. **Architectural Decentralization** – Number and independence of physical nodes in the system.
> 2. **Political Decentralization** – Number of individuals/organizations controlling those nodes.
> 3. **Logical Decentralization** – Whether the system appears as a single monolith or an amorphous swarm with no single state.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`**  
**Distinguish between the "methods" and "routes" of decentralization with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Methods (1 mark):** *What* is being decentralized. Examples: database decentralization (ledger replication), computation decentralization (mining), authority decentralization (DAO governance).
>
> **Routes (1 mark):** *How* decentralization is achieved or transitioned. Examples: mining as a route, voting as a route, trading as a route.
>
> **Distinction (1 mark):** Methods describe the *target property* of decentralization (e.g., distributed storage), while routes describe the *mechanism of transition* from centralized to decentralized state (e.g., using Proof of Work mining to earn influence).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the three dimensions of decentralization with suitable real-world examples. Discuss why blockchain achieves all three simultaneously. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With neat diagrams, explain the five layers of full ecosystem decentralization. For each layer, identify the decentralization method and a route used to achieve it. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**The Three Dimensions (4 marks):**

> **Architectural Decentralization** refers to how the system's physical infrastructure is distributed. A blockchain like Bitcoin has thousands of nodes spread across the globe. Even if 50% of the nodes fail simultaneously, the network continues to operate because no single node holds exclusive data. **Real-world example**: The internet itself is architecturally decentralized — multiple routers, servers, and ISPs collaborate without a central backbone.

> **Political Decentralization** refers to how many distinct individuals or organizations ultimately control the system. Bitcoin is politically decentralized because no single government, CEO, or company controls it. Miners, developers, and node operators exist in different jurisdictions with competing interests. **Real-world example**: Linux operating system — developed by thousands of contributors worldwide, not owned by any single corporation.

> **Logical Decentralization** is the most subtle dimension. It asks whether the system *presents itself* as a single coherent unit or as an amorphous swarm. A blockchain is logically decentralized: even though it has thousands of nodes, the ledger appears as one unified state, yet no central coordinator enforces this unity. **Real-world example**: Wikipedia is logically centralized (one canonical document) but politically/architecturally decentralized (anyone can edit).

**Why Blockchain Achieves All Three (3 marks):**

Blockchain uniquely combines all three dimensions because:

1. **P2P Networking** ensures architectural decentralization — anyone can run a node.
2. **Open-source protocols and community governance** ensure political decentralization — no single owner.
3. **Consensus algorithms (PoW, PoS, BFT)** ensure logical decentralization — all nodes converge to one canonical state without a central coordinator.

Cryptographic primitives (hashing, digital signatures) provide tamper-evidence, while economic incentives (block rewards, transaction fees) align individual self-interest with network integrity.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**The Five Layers (3.5 marks):**

| Layer | Description | Method Used | Route to Achieve |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Layer 0: Hardware** | Physical nodes, internet, mining rigs | Geographic distribution | Mining (PoW) / Staking (PoS) |
| **Layer 1: Data** | The blockchain ledger, transactions, blocks | Full replication across nodes | Gossip protocol, forks |
| **Layer 2: Network** | P2P communication protocol | Mesh topology | Open-source client software |
| **Layer 3: Consensus** | Agreement on ledger state | Validator distribution | Voting, delegation, staking |
| **Layer 4: Application** | dApps, smart contracts, wallets | Open-source development | DAOs, token launches |

**Explanation of Each Layer (3.5 marks):**

- **Layer 0 (Hardware)**: The physical foundation — nodes spread globally. Mining pools and validators use hardware in different geographies. *Decentralization Route*: Mining incentives encourage geographic spread.
- **Layer 1 (Data)**: The blockchain ledger is replicated on every full node. *Route*: Forks (hard/soft) enable community-driven evolution of the data layer.
- **Layer 2 (Network)**: P2P protocols like libp2p and DevP2P allow peer discovery and message relay. *Route*: Open-source development by distributed teams.
- **Layer 3 (Consensus)**: PoS or PoW mechanisms distribute validation. *Route*: On-chain voting and delegation (e.g., Polkadot's NPoS).
- **Layer 4 (Application)**: dApps built on top. *Route*: DAO governance for protocol changes.

A **full ecosystem decentralization** is achieved only when *all five layers* are simultaneously decentralized.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Explain the Byzantine Generals Problem and derive the condition for Byzantine Fault Tolerance ($n \geq 3f + 1$) in a decentralized consensus system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the FIVE routes to decentralization with examples. Explain how each route contributes to one or more layers of the ecosystem decentralization framework. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**The Byzantine Generals Problem (3 marks):**

The Byzantine Generals Problem, formalized by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease in 1982, describes a situation where multiple divisions of the Byzantine army surround an enemy city. The generals must agree on a common plan of action (attack or retreat) via messenger communication. However, some generals may be traitors who send conflicting messages.

The challenge: *How can loyal generals reach agreement despite traitors actively spreading misinformation?*

In blockchain terms:
- **Generals** = Validators / Miners
- **Loyal generals** = Honest nodes
- **Traitors** = Malicious or faulty nodes
- **Common plan** = Agreement on the next block

**Derivation of $n \geq 3f + 1$ (4 marks):**

Let $n$ = total validators, $f$ = number of Byzantine (malicious) nodes.

**Step 1:** For consensus to be possible, we need two quorums $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ of loyal generals such that their intersection contains at least one honest member:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 + Q_2 &\geq n + f + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The quorum size must include all honest nodes plus enough buffer to override traitors:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 = Q_2 &= n - f
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(n - f) &\geq n + f + 1 \\
2n - 2f &\geq n + f + 1 \\
n &\geq 3f + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Therefore, the maximum tolerable Byzantine nodes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{\max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{n - 1}{3} \right\rfloor
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** With $n = 10$ validators, $f_{\max} = 3$. The system tolerates up to **3 traitors** and remains safe.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**The Five Routes to Decentralization (5 marks):**

| Route | Description | Example | Layer(s) Served |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mining / Forks** | Earn influence via computational work or fork decisions | Bitcoin, Ethereum | Layer 0, 3, 4 |
| **Trading** | Decentralized exchange and liquidity provision | Uniswap, SushiSwap | Layer 4 |
| **Voting** | Stake-weighted governance | MakerDAO, Tezos | Layer 3, 4 |
| **Protocol Evolution** | Open-source development by distributed teams | Bitcoin Core, Ethereum | All layers |
| **Delegation** | Liquid democracy; delegate voting power | Polkadot (NPoS), Steemit | Layer 3, 4 |

**Contribution to Ecosystem Layers (2 marks):**

- **Mining** decentralizes the hardware layer (Layer 0) and consensus layer (Layer 3) by distributing computational work globally.
- **Trading** decentralizes the application layer (Layer 4) by enabling peer-to-peer asset exchange without intermediaries.
- **Voting** decentralizes governance (Layer 4 / DAO layer) by giving every stakeholder a voice.
- **Protocol Evolution** ensures continuous decentralization across all layers through community-driven upgrades.
- **Delegation** balances efficiency and decentralization by allowing token holders to delegate to expert validators, enhancing Layer 3.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Methods with Routes**: Examiners frequently give partial credit (1 mark instead of 2) when students blur the distinction. Memorize: *Methods = WHAT, Routes = HOW*.
> 2. **Listing only "distributed database"**: When asked for decentralization methods, students often forget to mention *governance* and *identity* decentralization. Cover all four.
> 3. **Skipping the derivation in BFT questions**: KTU 2024 Scheme expects **step-by-step derivation** of $n \geq 3f + 1$. A one-line answer without derivation loses 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting "full ecosystem"**: When asked about blockchain decentralization, do not stop at consensus. Mention **all five layers** explicitly.
> 5. **Confusing logical and architectural decentralization**: Logical decentralization is about the *appearance* of the system (monolith vs swarm), not the *physical* distribution. Examiners test this distinction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Decentralization** = distribution of authority, control, and data across a P2P network with no central intermediary.
- **Three dimensions**: Architectural, Political, Logical.
- **Four methods**: Database, Computation, Authority, Identity.
- **Five routes**: Mining, Trading, Voting, Protocol Evolution, Delegation.
- **Five ecosystem layers**: Hardware, Data, Network, Consensus, Application.
- **Full ecosystem decentralization** = decentralization at ALL five layers simultaneously.
- **BFT safety threshold**: $n \geq 3f + 1$, so $f_{\max} = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$.
- **Nakamoto Coefficient** = minimum entities required to collude for a $51\%$ attack (higher = more decentralized).
- **HHI Index**: $\sum p_i^2$; lower HHI = better decentralization (healthy if $< 0.25$).
- **Real-world analogy**: Village well (decentralized) vs. private water tanker (centralized).
- **Blockchain uniquely achieves all three dimensions** of decentralization through cryptography + consensus + economic incentives.
- **Examiner's mantra**: Always distinguish *what* is decentralized (methods) from *how* it is achieved (routes).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Consensus Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Consensus Algorithms # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Consensus Algorithm (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A **consensus algorithm** is a fault-tolerant mechanism used in distributed blockchain networks to achieve agreement on a single, immutable state of the shared ledger among all participating nodes, despite the presence of malicious actors, network latency, or node failures. It defines the rules by which trust is established in a **trustless environment** without requiring a central authority. Mathematically, a consensus protocol can be modeled as a function: $$C : \{N_1, N_2, \ldots, N_n\} \longrightarrow S_{agreed}$$ where $N_i$ represents the participating nodes and $S_{agreed}$ is the single globally accepted state (block) appended to the chain. ### 1.2 Classification Taxonomy (KTU Module 2 Core) Consensus algorithms are broadly classified into two families: | Family | Fault Model | Examples | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | **Crash Fault Tolerant (CFT)** | Tolerates node crashes only | Raft, Paxos | Permissioned chains | | **Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT)** | Tolerates malicious/arbitrary behaviour | PBFT, PoW, PoS | Permissionless public chains | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Class Election Problem" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine **100 students** in a school hall deciding the class monitor. There is no teacher, and some students might lie or stay silent. How do they all agree on the same name on the same ballot paper? A **consensus algorithm** is exactly this rule-book: > - **Proof of Work (PoW)** → Every student solves a tough puzzle (the loudest valid voice wins). > - **Proof of Stake (PoS)** → Students with the most attendance tokens get to vote proportionally. > - **PBFT** → A small pre-selected council votes in rounds until $\geq \frac{2}{3}$ agree. This is the essence of the **Byzantine Generals Problem** formulated by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982) — a foundational theoretical pillar of blockchain consensus. ### 1.4 The Byzantine Generals Problem (Theoretical Root) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Byzantine Generals Theorem:** A consensus is achievable among $n$ generals only if the number of **traitorous (Byzantine) generals** $f$ satisfies: > $$n \geq 3f + 1$$ > This means with **3f+1** nodes, the network can tolerate up to **f** malicious nodes and still reach agreement. This is the mathematical bedrock behind PBFT and modern BFT protocols. --- ## 1.5 Core Properties Every Consensus Must Guarantee (KTU CO Mapping) 1. **Safety** — All honest nodes agree on the *same* value (no forks that violate finality). 2. **Liveness** — The network keeps producing blocks / reaching decisions; it does not stall. 3. **Termination** — Every non-faulty node eventually decides on a value. 4. **Integrity** — No node decides on two different values. 5. **Fault Tolerance** — Works despite $\leq f$ faulty nodes (Byzantine or crash). 6. **Decentralization** — No single point of authority or trust. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Major Consensus Algorithms — Operational Breakdown ### 2.1.1 Proof of Work (PoW) — Used in Bitcoin, Ethereum (pre-Merge) **Operational Logic (Step-wise):** - Miners compete to find a **nonce** $n$ such that the block hash is below a target threshold. - Hash function used: **SHA-256** (Bitcoin) / **Ethash** (Ethereum). - The network automatically adjusts difficulty every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) in Bitcoin. - The miner who finds a valid nonce broadcasts the block; honest nodes verify and append it. - **Probabilistic Finality**: A block is considered final after **6 confirmations** (~60 minutes in Bitcoin). ### 2.1.2 Proof of Stake (PoS) — Used in Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano **Operational Logic (Step-wise):** - Validators **lock up (stake)** a quantity of the native cryptocurrency. - A pseudo-random selection algorithm picks a validator proportional to their stake weight. - Selected validator proposes the next block; others **attest** (vote). - Misbehaviour is punished via **slashing** — losing part of the staked amount. - **Economic Finality**: Achieved after $\frac{2}{3}$ of total stake attests (~12–15 minutes in Ethereum). ### 2.1.3 Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) — Used in EOS, Tron, BNB Chain **Operational Logic (Step-wise):** - Token holders **vote** to elect a fixed number of delegates (typically 21–101). - Delegates take turns producing blocks in a deterministic round-robin. - If a delegate misses their slot, they are voted out. - Achieves high throughput (thousands of TPS) at the cost of partial centralization. ### 2.1.4 Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) — Used in Hyperledger Fabric **Operational Logic (Step-wise):** - Operates in **three phases**: Pre-prepare, Prepare, Commit. - Requires **3f + 1** total nodes to tolerate **f** Byzantine nodes. - Provides **deterministic finality** — block is final as soon as commit phase ends. - Suitable only for permissioned networks due to $O(n^2)$ communication complexity. ### 2.1.5 Other Notable Algorithms | Algorithm | Core Idea | Key Feature | |---|---|---| | **Proof of Authority (PoA)** | Pre-approved validators sign blocks | Used in private chains (VeChain, Palm) | | **Proof of Burn (PoB)** | Miners burn coins to earn mining rights | Counterparty coin, Slimcoin | | **Proof of Elapsed Time (PoET)** | Random wait time lottery | Used in Hyperledger Sawtooth | | **Proof of Capacity (PoC)** | Hard-disk space = voting power | Burstcoin, Chia (uses Proof of Space + Time) | | **Raft** | Leader-based crash fault tolerance | Used in Quorum, etcd | --- ## 2.2 The CAP Theorem (Foundational Constraint) > [!NOTE] > **CAP Theorem (Brewer, 2000):** A distributed data store can simultaneously guarantee only **two** of the following three properties: > 1. **C**onsistency > 2. **A**vailability > 3. **P**artition Tolerance Since network partitions are inevitable in real distributed systems, blockchains typically choose **CP** (Consistency + Partition tolerance, e.g., Ethereum) or **AP** (Availability + Partition tolerance, e.g., eventual-consistency chains). ## 2.3 FLP Impossibility Result > [!IMPORTANT] > **FLP Impossibility (Fischer–Lynch–Paterson, 1985):** In an asynchronous network, no deterministic consensus protocol can guarantee all three properties — **Safety, Liveness, and Fault Tolerance** — in the presence of even a single faulty node. Blockchain consensus algorithms **circumvent** this by introducing: > - **Randomization** (e.g., PoW nonce search) > - **Synchrony assumptions** (partial synchrony model, used in PBFT) > - **Economic incentives / punishment** (slashing in PoS) --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination-Ready Formula Reference Table** (verify each symbol before use in numericals): | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H_{block} = H(H_{prev} \,\Vert\, nonce \,\Vert\, merkle_{root} \,\Vert\, t)$ | Block hash construction | PoW block structure | | 2 | $P_{mine}^{(i)} = \dfrac{H_i}{H_{net}}$ | Probability miner $i$ finds next block | Mining economics | | 3 | $H_{net} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} H_i$ | Total network hashrate | Security analysis | | 4 | $E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{net}}$ | Expected time to find next block | Difficulty tuning | | 5 | $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}}$ | Bitcoin difficulty adjustment | Every 2016 blocks | | 6 | $R_{block} = R_{base} + \sum_{j=1}^{k} f_j$ | Total block reward (subsidy + fees) | Block economics | | 7 | $S_{honest} = \dfrac{\text{Stake}_{honest}}{\text{Stake}_{total}}$ | Honest stake ratio | PoS safety threshold | | 8 | $n_{min} = 3f + 1$ | Minimum nodes to tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults | PBFT sizing | | 9 | $C_{PBFT} = O(n^2)$ | Communication complexity of PBFT | Scalability limit | | 10 | $E_{energy} \approx P_{net} \cdot T_{block} \cdot N_{blocks}$ | Energy spent per block | PoW environmental cost | | 11 | $F_{conf} \approx 1 - \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^z$ | Probability of successful double-spend attack after $z$ confirmations | Bitcoin security (Nakamoto, 2008) | | 12 | $P_{51\%} = \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^z$ | Attacker's double-spend success probability | Where $q$ = attacker hashrate fraction, $p$ = honest hashrate fraction, $p+q=1$ | > [!NOTE] > The double-spend probability formula **(12)** is a frequently-asked derivation in KTU university exams — students are advised to memorize the expression and the operational meaning of $p$, $q$, and $z$. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cryptocurrency Networks** — Securing $>\$2$ Trillion in market cap globally. - **Supply Chain Traceability** — IBM Food Trust, Maersk TradeLens. - **Cross-Border Payments** — Ripple (XRP), Stellar (XLM) use Federated Byzantine Agreement variants. - **Digital Identity** — Sovrin, Microsoft ION use permissioned consensus. - **Decentralized Finance (DeFi)** — All DeFi protocols rely on underlying chain consensus. - **IoT & Edge Computing** — Lightweight PoS / PoA variants for low-power devices. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Block Mining Time in PoW We want to derive the expected time $E[T_{block}]$ required to mine a block, given a network hashrate $H_{net}$ (hashes/sec) and difficulty $D$. ### Step 1 — Define Difficulty Numerically In Bitcoin, a valid hash $h$ must satisfy: $$h < \dfrac{2^{256}}{D}$$ Equivalently, the probability that any single random nonce produces a valid hash is: $$p = \dfrac{1}{D \cdot 2^{32}}$$ This comes from Bitcoin's compact representation where the target space is $D \cdot 2^{32}$ values out of the $2^{256}$ possible SHA-256 outputs. ### Step 2 — Recognize the Geometric Distribution The number of hashing attempts $X$ needed to find a valid block follows a **Geometric Distribution**: $$P(X = k) = (1 - p)^{k-1} \cdot p, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Geometric Mean Formula The expected number of attempts is: $$E[X] = \dfrac{1}{p}$$ ### Step 4 — Convert Attempts to Time Since the network performs $H_{net}$ hashes per second, the expected time per attempt is $\dfrac{1}{H_{net}}$ seconds. Therefore: $$E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{E[X]}{H_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{p \cdot H_{net}}$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute $p$ Substituting $p = \dfrac{1}{D \cdot 2^{32}}$ into the expression above: $$E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{D \cdot 2^{32}} \cdot H_{net}} = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{net}}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{net}}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bitcoin's Difficulty Adjustment Algorithm Bitcoin retargets difficulty every 2016 blocks to maintain an average block time of **10 minutes**. ### Step 1 — Define the Parameters Let: - $T_{actual}$ = actual time taken for the last 2016 blocks (in seconds) - $T_{target}$ = expected time for 2016 blocks = $2016 \times 600 = 1,209,600$ seconds - $D_{old}$ = previous difficulty - $D_{new}$ = new difficulty ### Step 2 — Set Up the Proportionality The new difficulty must adjust inversely proportional to the ratio of actual-to-target time: $$D_{new} \cdot T_{target} = D_{old} \cdot T_{actual}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve for New Difficulty Rearranging: $$D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}}$$ ### Step 4 — Apply the ±4× Capping Rule (Bitcoin Protocol) To prevent extreme volatility from sudden hashrate changes: $$D_{new} = \text{clip}\left(D_{old} \cdot \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}}, \, \dfrac{D_{old}}{4}, \, 4 \cdot D_{old}\right)$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{D_{new} = \min\!\left(4 \cdot D_{old}, \, \max\!\left(\dfrac{D_{old}}{4}, \, D_{old} \cdot \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{target}}\right)\right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Example (KTU Frequently Asked):** Suppose 2016 blocks were mined in 1,814,400 seconds (instead of 1,209,600 s). Then: > $$D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \dfrac{1{,}814{,}400}{1{,}209{,}600} = 1.5 \cdot D_{old}$$ > This means difficulty increases by **50%** — because blocks were mined too slowly (actual time > target). --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of Successful Double-Spend Attack (Nakamoto, 2008) Let: - $q$ = fraction of total network hashrate controlled by attacker - $p = 1 - q$ = honest hashrate fraction - $z$ = number of confirmations the victim waits ### Step 1 — Model the Race Between Two Chains The attacker secretly mines an alternative chain, while the honest network extends the public chain. This is a **random walk / binomial race problem**. ### Step 2 — Compute the Catch-Up Probability The probability that the attacker can ever catch up from a deficit of $z$ blocks is approximately: $$P_{catch} = \begin{cases} 1, & \text{if } q \geq p \\[6pt] \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^z, & \text{if } q < p \end{cases}$$ ### Step 3 — Compute Double-Spend Success Probability If the attacker can catch up, they successfully double-spend. Therefore: $$\boxed{P_{success} = \begin{cases} 1, & q \geq p \\[6pt] \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^z, & q < p \end{cases}}$$ ### Step 4 — Verification with Numerical Example Suppose $q = 0.1$ (10% hashrate) and $z = 6$ confirmations: $$P_{success} = \left(\dfrac{0.1}{0.9}\right)^{6} = (0.1111)^{6} \approx 1.88 \times 10^{-6}$$ This is why **6 confirmations** is considered secure in Bitcoin — the double-spend probability is roughly **0.000188%**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to compute this, always clearly state the assumption ($q < p$), plug in values, and convert the final answer to a percentage. Marks are awarded for stating the model and the conversion logic, not just the final arithmetic. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Block Reward & Total Supply Cap (Bitcoin) Bitcoin has a hard supply cap of **21 million BTC**. ### Step 1 — Define the Halving Schedule The block subsidy $R_{base}$ halves every **210,000 blocks** (~4 years). $$R_{base}(n) = \dfrac{50}{2^{\lfloor n / 210{,}000 \rfloor}} \quad \text{(in BTC)}$$ where $n$ is the current block height. ### Step 2 — Sum the Geometric Series The total BTC ever mined is: $$S_{total} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{50}{2^{k}}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve the Series Factoring out $210{,}000 \cdot 50$: $$S_{total} = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^k = 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 - 1/2} = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$ ### Final Result $$\boxed{S_{total}^{BTC} = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC (hard cap)}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Most Asked in KTU 14-Markers) | Parameter | PoW | PoS | DPoS | PBFT | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Node Selection** | Mining competition | Stake-weighted random | Delegate voting | Pre-approved | | **Energy Consumption** | **Very High** | Low | Low | Very Low | | **Throughput (TPS)** | ~7 (Bitcoin) | ~30 (Ethereum) | ~3000 (EOS) | ~1000 (Fabric) | | **Finality Type** | Probabilistic | Economic | Deterministic | Deterministic | | **Sybil Resistance** | Computational cost | Economic stake | Reputation | Permissioned | | **Byzantine Tolerance** | $\leq 50\%$ hashrate | $\leq 34\%$ stake | $\leq 34\%$ delegates | $f \leq \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ | | **Decentralization** | High | High | Medium | Low | | **Communication Cost** | Low | Low | Low | **$O(n^2)$ — High** | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Acknowledgment > [!NOTE] > All Mermaid diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric node identifiers** (no reserved keywords), and node labels contain **no markdown formatting** to ensure flawless rendering in markdown engines. --- ## 4.2 Diagram A — Proof of Work (PoW) Mining Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: New Transaction Pool] --> B[Collect Valid Transactions] B --> C[Construct Block Candidate] C --> D[Initialize Nonce n equals 0] D --> E[Compute SHA256 Header Hash] E --> F{Hash less than Target?} F -- No --> G[Increment Nonce n equals n plus 1] G --> E F -- Yes --> H[Broadcast Mined Block] H --> I[Other Nodes Verify Block] I --> J{Valid Proof?} J -- Yes --> K[Append to Local Chain] J -- No --> L[Reject Block] K --> M[Update UTXO Set] M --> N[Miner Receives Block Reward] L --> O[End] N --> O ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram B — Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) Three-Phase Protocol ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Client participant Primary as Primary Node P participant Replica1 as Replica R1 participant Replica2 as Replica R2 participant Replica3 as Replica R3 participant Replica4 as Replica R4 Client->>Primary: Request Operation Primary->>Replica1: Pre-Prepare Message Primary->>Replica2: Pre-Prepare Message Primary->>Replica3: Pre-Prepare Message Primary->>Replica4: Pre-Prepare Message Replica1->>Primary: Prepare Message Replica2->>Primary: Prepare Message Replica3->>Primary: Prepare Message Replica4->>Primary: Prepare Message Note over Primary,Replica4: All Nodes Collect 2f Prepare Messages Replica1->>Primary: Commit Message Replica2->>Primary: Commit Message Replica3->>Primary: Commit Message Replica4->>Primary: Commit Message Note over Primary,Replica4: All Nodes Collect 2f plus 1 Commit Messages Primary->>Client: Reply with Result ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation (KTU Exam Style):** With **n = 4** total nodes, this PBFT network tolerates $f = 1$ Byzantine node. The system requires $2f + 1 = 3$ matching replies in the Prepare phase and $2f + 1 = 3$ matching replies in the Commit phase before responding to the client. --- ## 4.4 Diagram C — Proof of Stake (PoS) Validator Selection & Slashing Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Validator Pool with Locked Stake] --> B[Compute Random Selection via RANDAO plus VDF] B --> C[Selected Proposer Creates Block] C --> D[Block Broadcast to All Validators] D --> E[Validators Attest to Block] E --> F{2/3 Stake Attests?} F -- No --> G[Block Discarded] F -- Yes --> H[Block Finalized on Chain] H --> I{Validator Misbehaved?} I -- Yes --> J[Slashing: Burn Stake] I -- No --> K[Reward Distribute Attesters and Proposer] J --> L[End] K --> L G --> L ``` --- ## 4.5 Diagram D — Comparative Consensus Algorithm Selection Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Choose Consensus Algorithm] --> B{Network Type?} B -- Public Permissionless --> C{Energy Budget?} B -- Private Permissioned --> D{Fault Tolerance Needed?} C -- High Budget OK --> E[Use Proof of Work] C -- Low Budget --> F{Stake Token Available?} F -- Yes --> G[Use Proof of Stake] F -- No --> H[Use Proof of Burn] D -- Byzantine Faults --> I{Finality Speed Priority?} D -- Crash Faults Only --> J[Use Raft or Paxos] I -- Speed Critical --> K[Use DPoS] I -- Strong Consistency --> L[Use PBFT] E --> M[Deploy Public Chain] G --> M H --> M K --> N[Deploy High TPS Chain] L --> O[Deploy Enterprise Chain] J --> O ``` --- ## 4.6 Diagram E — Cap Theorem Trade-Off in Blockchains ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Distributed Blockchain Network] --> B[CAP Theorem Constraint] B --> C[Consistency plus Partition Tolerance] B --> D[Availability plus Partition Tolerance] B --> E[Consistency plus Availability - Sacrifices P] C --> C1[Example: Ethereum, Bitcoin
Strong Consistency] D --> D1[Example: Some Sidechains
High Availability] E --> E1[Practically Unachievable with Partitions] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **Q: Define the Byzantine Generals Problem. State the minimum number of generals required to reach consensus if 2 generals are traitors.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — allocation below):** The Byzantine Generals Problem, formulated by Lamport, Shostak, and Pease in 1982, describes the difficulty of reaching consensus in a distributed system where some participants may act maliciously or fail arbitrarily. **(1 mark)** The generals must agree on a unified battle plan (common state) despite some generals sending false information. **(1 mark)** Using the formula $n \geq 3f + 1$, with $f = 2$ traitors: $$n \geq 3(2) + 1 = 7$$ Therefore, a minimum of **7 generals** are required to tolerate 2 traitors and still reach consensus. **(1 mark)** --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]` **Q: Differentiate between Proof of Work and Proof of Stake consensus mechanisms.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) | |---|---|---| | Resource Used | Computational power (electricity) | Economic stake (locked coins) | | Sybil Resistance | Cost of hardware and electricity | Cost of acquiring stake | | Block Finality | Probabilistic (after 6+ confirmations) | Economic (after 2/3 attestation) | | Energy Consumption | **Very High** | Low | | Example | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Ethereum (post-Merge), Cardano | *(1 mark for valid resource distinction; 1 mark for finality/sybil mechanism; 1 mark for energy/example difference.)* --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified 2024 Scheme]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the working of the Proof of Work consensus algorithm with a neat diagram. Discuss its advantages and limitations.** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** Proof of Work is a consensus mechanism where miners compete to solve a computationally intensive cryptographic puzzle. The first miner to find a valid solution earns the right to append the next block and receives a block reward. **Working Steps (3 marks):** 1. Miners collect pending transactions from the mempool and form a candidate block. 2. The block header contains: previous block hash, Merkle root, timestamp, difficulty target, and a **nonce** (number used once). 3. The miner repeatedly changes the nonce and computes SHA-256 of the block header. 4. A valid hash must be **numerically less than** the network target (i.e., starts with a specific number of leading zeros). 5. The miner who finds such a hash broadcasts the block; other nodes verify the proof by computing one hash and checking the target. 6. Upon verification, the block is appended, and the miner receives the block subsidy + transaction fees. **Advantages (1 mark):** - High Sybil resistance — attacking requires enormous hashrate investment. - Proven security model with **11+ years** of uptime on Bitcoin. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Extremely **energy-intensive** (~150 TWh/year for Bitcoin). - Throughput limited to **~7 TPS** in Bitcoin. - Risk of **51% attack** if a single entity gains majority hashrate. *(See Diagram A in Section 4 for the visual flow.)* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **With a neat diagram, explain the three phases of the Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) consensus algorithm. Why is PBFT unsuitable for public blockchains?** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** PBFT is a deterministic Byzantine fault tolerant consensus algorithm designed by Castro and Liskov (1999) for asynchronous distributed systems. It requires $n \geq 3f + 1$ total nodes to tolerate $f$ Byzantine faults. **Three Phases (4 marks):** **Phase 1 — Pre-Prepare:** - The **primary (leader) node** receives a client request and assigns it a sequence number. - The primary broadcasts a `` message to all backup nodes, where $v$ = view number, $n$ = sequence number, $d$ = digest, $m$ = message. **Phase 2 — Prepare:** - Each backup node verifies the pre-prepare message and broadcasts a `` to all other nodes. - A node enters the **prepared state** when it has received $2f$ matching prepare messages from different replicas. - This phase guarantees **agreement on the order** of requests across all honest nodes. **Phase 3 — Commit:** - Each node broadcasts a `` message. - A node executes the request when it has received $2f + 1$ matching commit messages from different replicas. - The result is sent back to the client. *(See Diagram B in Section 4.)* **Why Unsuitable for Public Blockchains (2 marks):** 1. **Communication Complexity:** PBFT requires $O(n^2)$ message exchanges per consensus round — infeasible for thousands of public nodes. 2. **Known Node Set:** PBFT assumes a fixed, known set of validators — incompatible with open, permissionless networks where nodes can join/leave freely. 3. **Sybil Vulnerability:** Without a Sybil resistance mechanism (like PoW/PoS), a public PBFT network can be trivially Sybil-attacked by an attacker creating thousands of fake identities. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Paper]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the Proof of Stake consensus mechanism in detail. Discuss the concept of slashing and economic finality.** **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** Proof of Stake is a consensus mechanism where validators are chosen to create new blocks based on the quantity of cryptocurrency they have **staked (locked)** as collateral, rather than via computational work. **Working (3 marks):** 1. Validators lock a minimum amount of the native token (e.g., 32 ETH in Ethereum). 2. A pseudo-random selection algorithm (using **RANDAO** + **Verifiable Delay Function** in Ethereum) selects a proposer proportional to their stake. 3. The selected validator creates a new block and broadcasts it. 4. Other validators **attest** to the block's validity. 5. When validators representing $\geq \frac{2}{3}$ of total stake attest, the block is **finalized**. **Slashing (2 marks):** Slashing is the punitive mechanism that destroys a portion of a validator's stake if they: - Propose or attest to **two conflicting blocks** for the same slot (equivocation). - Violate the **Casper FFG finality rules**. - Are inactive for extended periods (light penalty). Slashed stake is permanently burned, creating a strong economic disincentive against misbehaviour. **Economic Finality (1 mark):** A block is considered economically final when reverting it would require an attacker to burn at least **$\frac{1}{3}$ of the total staked ETH** (currently worth billions of USD), making reversal economically irrational. *(See Diagram C in Section 4.)* --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **A Bitcoin-like network has a total hashrate of $100 \times 10^{18}$ hashes/sec. If the current difficulty is $D = 1.2 \times 10^{13}$, calculate:** **(i) The expected block mining time.** **(ii) The probability that an attacker with 12% hashrate can double-spend after 6 confirmations.** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $H_{net} = 100 \times 10^{18} = 10^{20}$ hashes/sec - $D = 1.2 \times 10^{13}$ - $q = 0.12$, $p = 0.88$, $z = 6$ **Part (i) — Expected Block Mining Time (3 marks):** Using the formula: $$E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{net}}$$ Substituting: $$E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{13} \cdot 4.295 \times 10^{9}}{10^{20}}$$ $$E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{5.154 \times 10^{22}}{10^{20}} = 515.4 \text{ seconds}$$ **Verification using alternative path:** $$E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{13} \times 2^{32}}{10^{20}} = \dfrac{5.154 \times 10^{22}}{10^{20}} \approx 515.4 \text{ s} \approx 8.59 \text{ minutes}$$ *(Note: The actual Bitcoin difficulty target gives ~10 minutes; the slight variation here is due to the chosen parameters.)* **Part (ii) — Double-Spend Probability (4 marks):** Since $q < p$, use: $$P_{success} = \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^z = \left(\dfrac{0.12}{0.88}\right)^{6}$$ Step-wise calculation: $$\dfrac{0.12}{0.88} = 0.1364$$ $$P_{success} = (0.1364)^{6}$$ Taking logarithms: $$\log_{10}(P_{success}) = 6 \cdot \log_{10}(0.1364) = 6 \cdot (-0.8650) = -5.190$$ $$P_{success} = 10^{-5.190} \approx 6.46 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{P_{success} \approx 6.46 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.000646\%}$$ **Interpretation (extra credit in KTU valuation):** The attacker has only a **0.000646%** chance of successfully double-spending after 6 confirmations — this is why waiting for 6 confirmations is considered secure in Bitcoin. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Read Carefully):** > 1. **Formula Substitution Errors** — In derivation questions (such as the double-spend probability), students often write $\left(\dfrac{p}{q}\right)^z$ instead of $\left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^z$. **Always double-check the ratio direction.** > 2. **Mixing Finality Types** — Many students confuse **probabilistic finality** (PoW) with **deterministic finality** (PBFT) and **economic finality** (PoS). These are three distinct concepts — examiners **specifically test this distinction**. > 3. **Forgetting the FLP Impossibility Assumption** — When explaining why consensus is hard, students must mention the asynchronous network assumption. > 4. **Confusing $\mathbf{51\%}$ thresholds** — PoW needs $>50\%$ hashrate, PoS needs $>66\%$ stake, PBFT needs $>33\%$ Byzantine nodes. Students frequently interchange these. > 5. **Skipping the Mathematical Justification for $\mathbf{3f + 1}$** — In PBFT, students must explain *why* the bound is $3f+1$ and not $2f+1$. Hint: it arises from needing $2f+1$ consistent replies to override $f$ malicious messages in both Prepare and Commit phases. > 6. **Missing Energy Comparison in PoW vs PoS** — KTU examiners specifically award 1 mark for mentioning the environmental impact difference. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Consensus Algorithm Definition** → Fault-tolerant agreement protocol in distributed systems. - **Byzantine Generals Theorem** → $n \geq 3f + 1$ (Lamport, Shostak, Pease 1982). - **FLP Impossibility** → No deterministic consensus can guarantee Safety + Liveness + Fault Tolerance in asynchronous networks; blockchain sidesteps this via randomization/synchrony/economics. - **CAP Theorem** → Choose any 2 of {Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance}; blockchains typically pick **CP**. - **PoW Formula** → $E[T_{block}] = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_{net}}$; difficulty retargets every 2016 blocks in Bitcoin. - **Bitcoin Block Reward** → Starts at 50 BTC, halves every 210,000 blocks; **21 million hard cap**. - **Double-Spend Probability** → $P = \left(\dfrac{q}{p}\right)^z$ for $q < p$; equals 1 if $q \geq p$. - **PoS Slashing** → Economic punishment for double-signing / violating Casper FFG rules. - **Economic Finality** → Achieved when reverting a block costs $\geq \frac{1}{3}$ of total staked value. - **DPoS** → Token holders vote for 21–101 delegates; high TPS, partial centralization. - **PBFT Phases** → Pre-Prepare → Prepare → Commit; needs $n = 3f+1$ nodes; provides **deterministic finality**; communication cost $O(n^2)$. - **PoW Tolerance** → Tolerates up to **49.9%** malicious hashrate. - **PoS / BFT Tolerance** → Tolerates up to **33.3%** malicious stake / Byzantine validators. - **51% Attack Cost** → In PoW: rent or acquire >50% hashrate (~$10–20 billion currently for Bitcoin). - **Finality Speed Order** → PoW (slow, probabilistic) < PoS (medium, economic) < DPoS ≈ PBFT (fast, deterministic). - **Permissioned Chains** → Hyperledger Fabric uses PBFT; deterministic finality. - **Permissionless Chains** → Bitcoin/Ethereum use PoW/PoS; economic / probabilistic finality. - **Energy Efficiency** → PoW: ~150 TWh/year (Bitcoin) vs. PoS: ~0.01 TWh/year (Ethereum). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Examination Mantra:** > *"Byzantine problem needs $3f+1$ nodes. PoW uses energy; PoS uses stake. PBFT needs three phases. Double-spend is $\left(q/p\right)^z$. Bitcoin caps at 21 million. Finality is probabilistic in PoW, economic in PoS, deterministic in PBFT."*

topics.title: Crash Fault-Tolerance (CFT) Algorithms - Paxos, Raft
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Crash Fault-Tolerance (CFT) Algorithms - Paxos, Raft

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Crash Fault-Tolerance (CFT)** is the ability of a distributed system to continue providing correct service (specifically, **safety** and **liveness**) when some subset of its nodes suffer *crash failures* — i.e., they simply halt and stop responding, without behaving maliciously or arbitrarily.

In the context of distributed consensus for blockchain and replicated state machines:

- **Crash Fault** (a.k.a. *Fail-Stop Fault*): A node stops executing, loses volatile memory, and never recovers within the consensus round. It never sends incorrect messages.
- **Byzantine Fault** (for contrast): A node behaves arbitrarily, including sending conflicting or malicious messages. CFT algorithms **cannot** tolerate Byzantine faults.

The two most influential CFT consensus algorithms are:

1. **Paxos** — Leslie Lamport (1989/1998). A family of protocols (Basic Paxos, Multi-Paxos, Cheap Paxos, Fast Paxos) for reaching agreement among unreliable processors.
2. **Raft** — Diego Ongaro & John Ousterhout (2014). Designed as an *understandable* alternative to Paxos; decomposes consensus into three sub-problems: **Leader Election, Log Replication, Safety**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 1 — Paxos as a "Parliamentary Vote"**
>
> Imagine **5 parliamentarians (acceptors)** in a dimly lit hall where lights flicker (crashes can happen). A **proposer** walks in with a proposal ("Law X: increase tax"). Before voting, she must collect *promise slips* from a majority (**3 out of 5**). If she gets them, she then asks the same majority to actually *accept* the law. Any new proposer who walks in later must first check — "Has someone already gotten a majority promise with a higher round number?" If yes, she must adopt that higher proposal. This is exactly **Basic Paxos**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy 2 — Raft as a "Captain's Logbook"**
>
> Imagine a **pirate ship** with a captain and many sailors. Only the **captain** (leader) may write new entries into the ship's official **logbook** (replicated log). Each entry is copied to a majority of sailors' personal logbooks. If the captain disappears (crashes), the sailors hold an **election** with randomly drawn timeouts — whoever times out first becomes a candidate and asks for votes. Crucially, each sailor only votes for a candidate whose logbook is *at least as up-to-date* as their own. This guarantees no committed entry is ever lost.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| **Fault Tolerance Threshold (f)** | Up to **f** simultaneous crash faults tolerated |
| **Minimum Cluster Size for CFT (n)** | **n = 2f + 1** |
| **Quorum / Majority Size (Q)** | **Q = ⌊n/2⌋ + 1 = f + 1** |
| **Quorum Intersection Property** | Any two majorities share **≥ 1** node |
| **Network Model** | Asynchronous (no bounded delay) |
| **Message Authenticity** | Assumed (no Byzantine adversaries) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key distinction for KTU exams:** CFT tolerates only **crash faults**, not arbitrary/BYZANTINE faults. Blockchain systems like *Hyperledger Fabric* use CFT consensus (Raft/Istanbul BFT variants), whereas *Bitcoin/Ethereum* use BFT consensus (PoW/PoS variants) to also tolerate malicious nodes.

### 1.4 The FLP Impossibility — Why CFT Algorithms Need Timeouts

In 1985, Fischer, Lynch, and Paterson proved that **no deterministic protocol** can guarantee consensus in a purely asynchronous system with even *one* crash failure.

> [!WARNING]
> The FLP result does **NOT** mean consensus is impossible in practice. It means pure asynchrony + determinism + 1 crash = *no guaranteed termination*. CFT protocols (Paxos, Raft) **circumvent FLP** by introducing either:
> - **Partial synchrony** (eventually bounded message delays — Raft's approach), or
> - **Randomization / timeouts** (Paxos uses leader election with timeouts).

This is a **favourite KTU question**: *"How does Paxos/Raft bypass the FLP impossibility?"*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Roles in Paxos

Paxos defines three logical roles (a single node can play multiple roles simultaneously):

1. **Proposer** — Issues proposals (numbered, monotonically increasing). Acts as the *client's representative*.
2. **Acceptor** — Forms the *quorum group*. Votes on proposals. The only role that provides safety.
3. **Learner** — Learns the chosen value once a proposal is *chosen*. Typically the replica that applies commands.

### 2.2 Basic Paxos — Phases of Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase 1 — Prepare / Promise (Quorum Formation)**
> - Proposer selects a proposal number **n** (globally unique, monotonically increasing).
> - Sends **PREPARE(n)** to a majority of acceptors.
> - Each acceptor, on receiving PREPARE(n):
>   - If **n** is *greater than* any previously seen prepare number, replies **PROMISE(n, n_prev, v_prev)** — promising never to accept any proposal numbered **< n**, and revealing the highest-numbered proposal it has already *accepted* (if any).
>   - Otherwise, ignores the request.
>
> **Phase 2 — Accept / Accepted (Value Selection)**
> - If the proposer receives PROMISE from a **majority** (Q = f+1 acceptors):
>   - It must select the value **v** of the highest-numbered accepted proposal among the promises (preserves safety — ensures no committed value is overwritten).
>   - If no acceptor has accepted anything yet, the proposer is free to choose its own **v**.
>   - Sends **ACCEPT(n, v)** to the same majority.
> - Each acceptor, on receiving ACCEPT(n, v):
>   - If **n ≥ highest prepare number seen**, it accepts and persists; replies **ACCEPTED(n, v)**.
>   - Otherwise, ignores.
>
> **Phase 3 — Learn (Replication Notification)**
> - Once the proposer receives ACCEPTED from a **majority**, the value **v** is **CHOSEN**.
> - The proposer (or any acceptor that noticed a majority) notifies all **learners**.

### 2.3 Multi-Paxos — The Production-Grade Optimization

> [!NOTE]
> Basic Paxos chooses only **one value per instance**. To run a replicated state machine, you need *infinitely many instances*. **Multi-Paxos** amortizes the cost of leader election across many instances:
>
> - Run Phase 1 **only once** (elect a *stable leader*).
> - The stable leader then issues only Phase 2 (ACCEPT) messages for every subsequent command.
> - Result: Steady-state throughput rises dramatically.

This is the basis of systems like **Google's Chubby lock service** and **Megastore** (Spanner's predecessor).

### 2.4 Raft — Decomposed into Three Sub-Problems

Raft is engineered for **understandability**. It factors consensus into three largely independent modules:

| Sub-problem | Purpose | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Leader Election** | Select one leader when no leader exists | Randomized election timeouts + RequestVote RPC |
| **Log Replication** | Propagate client commands to all replicas | AppendEntries RPC + commitIndex advancement |
| **Safety** | Guarantee that all nodes apply same command sequence to same log index | Voting restriction + commit rule |

#### 2.4.1 Raft Server States

A Raft server is always in exactly one of three states:

1. **Follower** — Passive. Responds to RPCs from leader/candidate. Issue heartbeats → becomes candidate after timeout.
2. **Candidate** — Used during elections. Increments term, votes for self, sends RequestVote RPCs.
3. **Leader** — Active. Handles all client requests, replicates log entries, sends periodic heartbeats (empty AppendEntries).

#### 2.4.2 Terms in Raft

A **term** is a monotonically increasing integer. Each term begins with an **election**. At most **one leader** per term. Terms act as a **logical clock** and are exchanged in every RPC.

> [!WARNING]
> If a candidate or leader discovers its term is *outdated* (i.e., a higher term exists), it immediately reverts to **Follower** state. This is a common source of confusion in KTU exams.

#### 2.4.3 Leader Election Algorithm

- Followers maintain a randomized **election timeout** (typically **150–300 ms**).
- If no heartbeat (AppendEntries) arrives within the timeout → follower increments current term, becomes **candidate**, votes for itself, and broadcasts **RequestVote** to all peers.
- A candidate wins the election if it receives votes from a **majority** of servers for the same term → becomes **Leader**.
- **Election restriction**: A server votes for a candidate only if the candidate's log is **at least as up-to-date** as the voter's own log. *"Up-to-date"* is defined by:
  1. Higher last log term wins.
  2. If equal term, longer log wins.

#### 2.4.4 Log Replication

- Client sends command to leader.
- Leader appends entry to its log (uncommitted), then issues **AppendEntries** RPCs in parallel to all followers.
- Entry is **committed** (and applied to state machine) once it has been replicated to a **majority** of nodes and the leader's current term includes this entry.
- Leader tracks **nextIndex[]** and **matchIndex[]** for each follower; on rejection, decrements nextIndex and retries until consensus.

#### 2.4.5 Safety Properties of Raft (the **5 Pillars**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five properties collectively guarantee **State Machine Safety** in Raft. KTU examiners love testing these:

1. **Election Safety**: At most one leader per term.
2. **Leader Append-Only**: A leader never overwrites or deletes entries in its own log; only appends.
3. **Log Matching**: If two logs contain an entry with the same (index, term), then all preceding entries are identical in both logs.
4. **Leader Completeness**: If a log entry is committed in term **T**, then every leader in any term **> T** will have that entry in its log.
5. **State Machine Safety**: If a server has applied a log entry at a given index to its state machine, no other server will ever apply a different entry at the same index.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Fault tolerance bound (CFT) | $f = \lfloor (n-1)/2 \rfloor$ | Crash faults only |
| Minimum cluster size | $n_{min} = 2f + 1$ | Odd preferred |
| Quorum / Majority size | $Q = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1 = f + 1$ | ≥ half + 1 |
| Quorum intersection | $\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq 1$ | For any two majorities |
| Paxos proposal number | $n = \langle \text{counter}, \text{node\_id} \rangle$ | Globally unique |
| Raft term advance | $\text{term}_{new} > \text{term}_{old}$ | Monotonic per node |
| Election timeout range | $T_{timeout} \in [150, 300]\text{ ms}$ | Raft default |
| Heartbeat interval | $T_{heartbeat} \ll T_{timeout}$ | Typically 50 ms |
| Commit condition | Entry replicated on $f+1$ nodes & in current term | Raft commit rule |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Google Spanner / Chubby**: Use Multi-Paxos for globally distributed transactions and lock services.
- **etcd** (Kubernetes' backing store): Uses Raft for cluster coordination.
- **Consul** (HashiCorp): Uses Raft for service discovery and configuration.
- **CockroachDB**: Raft-based replication of range replicas.
- **Hyperledger Fabric**: Pluggable CFT ordering service (Raft-based) for permissioned blockchains.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not use Paxos directly in Fabric?** Paxos' two-phase nature with no clear leader makes it harder to operate and debug. Raft provides equivalent safety with simpler operational semantics — a key engineering decision.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fault Tolerance Bound $n = 2f + 1$

> **Goal:** Prove that a cluster of **n** nodes, each of which may crash independently, can tolerate up to **f** simultaneous crash faults *iff* $n \geq 2f + 1$.

**Step 1 — Define the problem.**
We require that the system continues to make progress (liveness) and produce correct decisions (safety) as long as the number of crashed nodes $c \leq f$. The remaining alive nodes must still form a *decision-making quorum*.

**Step 2 — Define the quorum.**
Let $Q$ = size of any quorum. By definition, *any two quorums must intersect* in at least one node — otherwise two disjoint quorums could each decide conflicting values, violating safety. Equivalently:

$$
n < 2Q \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q > \frac{n}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \geq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 3 — Express quorum in terms of fault tolerance.**
We must be able to reach a quorum even after $f$ nodes crash. So the alive node count must be at least $Q$:

$$
n - f \geq Q \geq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the inequality.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n - f &\geq \left\lfloor \frac{n}{2} \right\rfloor + 1 \\
n - f &\geq \frac{n}{2} + 1 \quad \text{(using } \lfloor x \rfloor \geq x - 1 \text{, but tightest bound)} \\
n - \frac{n}{2} &\geq f + 1 \\
\frac{n}{2} &\geq f + 1 \\
n &\geq 2f + 2 \quad \text{(using ceiling for safety)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Tighten the bound.**
Since $n$ and $Q$ are integers, the tightest achievable bound is $n = 2f + 1$ (with $Q = f + 1$). This is the **minimum cluster size** for tolerating $f$ crash faults.

**Step 6 — Concrete illustration.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f = 1 &\Rightarrow n_{min} = 3, \quad Q = 2 \\
f = 2 &\Rightarrow n_{min} = 5, \quad Q = 3 \\
f = 3 &\Rightarrow n_{min} = 7, \quad Q = 4 \\
f = 4 &\Rightarrow n_{min} = 9, \quad Q = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Verify quorum intersection.**
For $n = 2f + 1$, two majorities each of size $f+1$ must share at least one node:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 + Q_2 &= (f+1) + (f+1) = 2f + 2 \\
&= n + 1 \\
&> n
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **Pigeonhole Principle**, any two subsets of an $n$-element set whose sizes sum to $> n$ must share at least one element. Hence $\vert Q_1 \cap Q_2 \vert \geq 1$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation of Raft's "Up-to-Date" Voting Rule

> **Goal:** Show that the Raft voting rule (compare last log term, then length) is sufficient to ensure **Leader Completeness**.

**Step 1 — Define up-to-date comparison.**
For two logs $A$ and $B$, $A$ is *more up-to-date* than $B$ iff:
- (a) Last term of $A$ > last term of $B$, OR
- (b) Last terms are equal AND $\vert A \vert > \vert B \vert$.

**Step 2 — Assume, for contradiction**, that a leader $L'$ is elected in term $T' > T$ but lacks an entry $e$ that was committed in term $T$ (committed means stored on a majority $Q_T$).

**Step 3 — Leader Completeness requires this to be impossible.** When $L'$ was elected, it received votes from a majority $Q_{T'}$. Since $Q_T$ and $Q_{T'}$ both have size $f+1$ and $Q_T + Q_{T'} = 2f + 2 > 2f + 1 = n$, they intersect. So there exists a voter $v \in Q_T \cap Q_{T'}$.

**Step 4 — $v$ must have entry $e$** (because $v \in Q_T$, the majority that committed $e$).

**Step 5 — When $v$ voted for $L'$, by the voting rule, $L'$'s log was *at least as up-to-date* as $v$'s log.** Since $v$'s log contains $e$, $L'$'s log must also contain $e$ (by Log Matching — if $L'$ had the entry at the same index it would have the same term, contradicting the choice; if its log were shorter, it would be less up-to-date).

**Step 6 — Contradiction reached.** $\therefore$ $L'$ must have $e$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Paxos vs Raft

| Dimension | Paxos (Multi-Paxos) | Raft |
|---|---|---|
| **Original Goal** | Correctness under any failure | Understandability + Correctness |
| **Leader Model** | Weak — any proposer can lead if elected | Strong — at most one leader per term |
| **Election Mechanism** | Implicit (round number, proposer id) | Explicit (term number + RequestVote RPC) |
| **Log Structure** | Per-instance value slots | Unified replicated log with (term, index) tuples |
| **Commit Rule** | Majority ACCEPTED messages | Majority replication *and* entry in current term |
| **Client Redirect** | Clients may need to retry on proposer failure | Client always redirected to current leader |
| **Log Compaction** | Snapshotting | Snapshotting (built-in) + InstallSnapshot RPC |
| **Membership Change** | Complex (Reconfiguration Paxos variants) | Joint Consensus (single-server changes) |
| **Byzantine Safety** | No (CFT only) | No (CFT only) |
| **Practical Users** | Google Spanner, Chubby | etcd, Consul, CockroachDB, TiKV, Fabric |
| **Implementation Difficulty** | High (many edge cases) | Moderate (designed for clarity) |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Paxos Choosing Value V

> **Scenario:** $n = 5$ acceptors, so $f = 2$, $Q = 3$. Proposer $P_1$ wants value $V$ chosen with proposal number $n = (5, P_1)$.

**Step 1:** $P_1$ sends PREPARE(5) to all 5 acceptors.

**Step 2:** Acceptors $A_1, A_2, A_3$ respond with PROMISE(5, ⊥, ⊥) — they have not accepted anything yet. ($A_4, A_5$ slow or crashed.)

**Step 3:** $P_1$ has 3 promises (majority). No accepted values reported. $P_1$ chooses its own value $V$.

**Step 4:** $P_1$ sends ACCEPT(5, V) to all 5.

**Step 5:** $A_1, A_2, A_3$ reply ACCEPTED(5, V). $P_1$ has 3 acceptances → **V is CHOSEN**.

**Step 6 (concurrency check):** Suppose $P_2$ simultaneously tried $n = (4, P_2)$. Its PREPARE(4) reaches $A_2, A_3$ but they already promised $n = 5$ → reject. $P_2$'s attempt fails. $P_2$ must retry with $n = 6$ and adopt any value $P_1$ might have already forced. Safety preserved.

### 3.5 Worked Example — Raft Leader Election

> **Scenario:** $n = 5$, current term $T = 3$, all nodes are Followers. Node $S_2$'s election timer fires first.

**Step 1:** $S_2$ increments term to $T = 4$, votes for itself, state = Candidate, sends RequestVote(term=4, lastLogIndex=7, lastLogTerm=3) to $S_1, S_3, S_4, S_5$.

**Step 2:** Each follower compares candidate's log to its own:
- $S_1$: last term=3, length=7 → equal term, equal length → **grant vote**.
- $S_3$: last term=3, length=7 → equal term, equal length → **grant vote**.
- $S_4$: last term=3, length=6 → **reject** (candidate longer, but voter shorter = candidate *more* up-to-date; candidate has equal term and longer length, so *voter's log is less up-to-date*; rule says voter votes only if candidate at least as up-to-date → candidate IS more up-to-date, voter **grants**? Actually rule: voter grants if candidate's log is **at least as up-to-date as voter's**. Yes, grant.).
- $S_5$: last term=3, length=8 → reject (voter has longer log with same term → voter is more up-to-date → candidate not as up-to-date → **reject**).

**Step 3:** $S_2$ receives 3 votes from $S_1, S_3, S_4$ (majority of 5). Becomes **Leader of term 4**.

**Step 4:** $S_2$ immediately sends heartbeats (AppendEntries with no entries, term=4) to assert leadership. All others revert to Followers.

> **Insight:** Even when two candidates split votes (e.g., 2-2-1), randomized timeouts ensure one candidate wins eventually. This is Raft's mechanism for bypassing FLP.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Crash Fault Tolerance (CFT) and Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT). Give one real-world blockchain example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | CFT | BFT |
|---|---|---|
| Failure Model | Nodes only *crash* (halt) | Nodes can behave *arbitrarily* (lie, equivocate) |
| Fault Threshold | Tolerates up to **f** crashes with **n = 2f + 1** | Tolerates up to **f** Byzantine with **n = 3f + 1** |
| Algorithms | Paxos, Raft | PBFT, Tendermint, HotStuff |
| Blockchain Example | **Hyperledger Fabric** (Raft orderer) | **Cosmos Hub / Tendermint** |

*(1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for fault tolerance formulas, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Explain how Raft bypasses the FLP impossibility result.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The FLP impossibility (1985) states that no deterministic protocol can guarantee consensus in a purely asynchronous system with even one crash failure. Raft bypasses FLP by assuming **partial synchrony** — it assumes message delays are bounded *eventually*, though not known in advance. Raft uses:
1. **Randomized election timeouts** (150–300 ms) to break symmetry among candidates (avoids perpetual split votes).
2. **Heartbeats** to detect leader liveness and trigger fresh elections on timeout.
3. **Term numbers** as a logical clock that monotonically increases, ensuring progress.
*(1 mark per point.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Describe the **Basic Paxos algorithm** in detail, including all three phases: Prepare/Promise, Accept/Accepted, and Learn. Explain the role of the proposal number and how safety is guaranteed when two proposers compete. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Raft consensus algorithm**, focusing on its three sub-problems: Leader Election, Log Replication, and Safety. Clearly state the **five safety properties** of Raft and prove why each is necessary. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q3(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Roles (1 mark)**
Paxos has three roles: **Proposer** (initiates proposals), **Acceptor** (votes, stores values), **Learner** (replicates the chosen value). A single node can play multiple roles.

**Step 2 — Phase 1: Prepare / Promise (2 marks)**
- Proposer picks a globally unique proposal number **n** (e.g., $\langle \text{counter}, \text{node\_id} \rangle$).
- Sends **PREPARE(n)** to a majority of acceptors (Q = ⌊n/2⌋ + 1).
- Each acceptor:
  - If **n > any previously seen prepare number** → reply **PROMISE(n, n_prev, v_prev)** and promise never to accept proposals numbered **< n**.
  - Else → ignore.

**Step 3 — Phase 2: Accept / Accepted (2 marks)**
- If majority of PROMISE responses received, proposer chooses value **v**:
  - If any acceptor revealed an already-accepted value with the highest number → proposer **must adopt** that value (preserves safety).
  - Otherwise, proposer uses its own proposed value.
- Sends **ACCEPT(n, v)** to the same majority.
- Each acceptor: if **n ≥ highest prepare number seen**, accepts; replies **ACCEPTED(n, v)**.

**Step 4 — Phase 3: Learn + Safety under contention (2 marks)**
- Value **v** is **chosen** when a majority replies ACCEPTED.
- Learners are notified.
- **Safety under contention**: Suppose $P_1$ uses $n_1$ and $P_2$ uses $n_2 > n_1$. If $P_2$ succeeds in Phase 1, acceptors that already promised $n_1$ will reject ACCEPT($n_1$). $P_1$ must retry with $n > n_2$ and discover $P_2$'s value. The *highest-numbered accepted value wins* rule prevents two distinct values from both being chosen.

---

#### Model Solution — Q3(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Three sub-problems (1 mark)**
Raft decomposes consensus into (a) Leader Election, (b) Log Replication, (c) Safety. Each is implemented as a separate module for understandability.

**Step 2 — Leader Election (2 marks)**
- Followers wait for AppendEntries heartbeats from leader.
- If heartbeat not received within randomized timeout (150–300 ms) → follower becomes candidate, increments term, votes for self, broadcasts RequestVote.
- Wins if majority of votes received. Loses if higher term discovered → reverts to Follower.

**Step 3 — Log Replication (2 marks)**
- Leader receives client command, appends to its log as new entry.
- Sends AppendEntries RPCs to all followers in parallel.
- Entry is committed once replicated on a majority *and* the entry is in the current term.
- Leader tracks nextIndex[] and matchIndex[] per follower; on rejection, decrements and retries.

**Step 4 — Five Safety Properties (2 marks)**
1. **Election Safety**: At most one leader per term — enforced by majority voting.
2. **Leader Append-Only**: Leader never overwrites/deletes log entries.
3. **Log Matching**: Same (index, term) → all prior entries identical — enforced by AppendEntries consistency check.
4. **Leader Completeness**: Every leader for term > T contains all entries committed in term T — enforced by up-to-date voting rule.
5. **State Machine Safety**: No two servers apply different commands at the same log index — consequence of properties 3 & 4.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(a)** Discuss the design philosophy of **Multi-Paxos**. How does it differ from Basic Paxos? Why is it the preferred choice for production systems like Google Spanner? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **partial synchrony** in distributed systems. Discuss how Raft uses this assumption to ensure liveness. Compare and contrast Raft's leader election with Paxos' round-number-based approach. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q4(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Basic Paxos limitation (1 mark)**
Basic Paxos chooses only **one value per instance**. Running an infinite sequence of commands (as a replicated state machine requires) means running infinite instances, each with two phases. This is unacceptably slow.

**Step 2 — Multi-Paxos design (3 marks)**
- **Stable Leader**: The first successful instance establishes a *distinguished proposer* (leader).
- **Phase 1 reuse**: Skip Prepare/Promise for subsequent instances when the leader is stable.
- **Steady state = one RTT per command**: Leader issues only ACCEPT; majority ACCEPTED → chosen.
- **Leader re-election**: If leader crashes, a new election re-runs Phase 1 to establish a new stable leader.

**Step 3 — Production advantages (3 marks)**
- **Spanner**: Uses Multi-Paxos per Paxos group; combines with TrueTime API for external consistency across datacentres.
- **Chubby lock service**: Uses Multi-Paxos for highly available coarse-grained locking.
- **Throughput**: Steady-state latency ~1 RTT vs ~2 RTT in Basic Paxos.
- **Pipelining**: Leader can pipeline multiple ACCEPTs, increasing throughput dramatically.

---

#### Model Solution — Q4(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Partial Synchrony (2 marks)**
A model where there exists an unknown **Global Stabilization Time (GST)** after which the network behaves synchronously (bounded delay, bounded clock skew). Before GST, the network may be arbitrarily asynchronous. This is the formal model DLS (Dwork, Lynch, Stockmeyer, 1988).

**Step 2 — Raft's use of partial synchrony (3 marks)**
- Raft assumes messages will eventually be delivered within some bound (after GST).
- Election timeouts are chosen to be >> typical RTT but << GST duration — so elections will *eventually* succeed.
- If election fails repeatedly (split votes), randomized timeouts ensure probabilistic success.
- Safety is unconditional (always holds); **liveness is conditional** (only guaranteed after GST).

**Step 3 — Comparison with Paxos (2 marks)**
| Aspect | Paxos | Raft |
|---|---|---|
| Leadership | Emergent, weakly defined | Explicit per term |
| Election Trigger | Implicit (round number retries) | Explicit (timeout + RequestVote) |
| Conflict Resolution | Higher proposal number wins | Higher term + up-to-date log wins |
| Randomness | None inherent (deterministic) | Randomized timeouts |

> Both use the same quorum intersection property and tolerate f = ⌊(n-1)/2⌋ crash faults.

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CFT with BFT fault tolerance bounds.** Students often write "$n = 3f + 1$" for Paxos/Raft. WRONG. CFT requires only $n = 2f + 1$. The $3f + 1$ bound is for *Byzantine* fault tolerance (PBFT).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the value-promotion rule in Paxos Phase 2.** When a proposer receives promises, it MUST adopt the highest-numbered already-accepted value, even if it proposed something different. Omitting this loses 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Mishandling Raft's "current term" commit rule.** A leader cannot directly commit entries from *previous* terms by counting replicas alone. It must replicate a *current-term* entry first, then all earlier entries become committed *indirectly*. This is a frequently tested nuance.
>
> 4. **Forgetting quorum intersection in derivations.** When proving fault tolerance, the pigeonhole argument $\vert Q_1 \vert + \vert Q_2 \vert = 2(f+1) > 2f+1 = n$ must be stated explicitly.
>
> 5. **Mixing up terms in Raft.** Terms are *per-election*, not global. A node discovering a higher term must step down — this is the basis of leader completeness.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **CFT** tolerates *crash* faults only; **BFT** tolerates *arbitrary* faults.
- [x] CFT requires **$n = 2f + 1$** nodes; BFT requires **$n = 3f + 1$** nodes.
- [x] **Quorum** = $f + 1 = \lfloor n/2 \rfloor + 1$. Any two quorums intersect (pigeonhole).
- [x] **Paxos** has 3 roles (Proposer, Acceptor, Learner) and 3 phases (Prepare/Promise, Accept/Accepted, Learn).
- [x] Proposal numbers must be **globally unique and monotonically increasing**; typically $\langle \text{counter}, \text{node\_id} \rangle$.
- [x] **Value promotion rule**: Proposer adopts highest-numbered already-accepted value if reported by acceptors.
- [x] **Multi-Paxos** reuses Phase 1 by electing a *stable leader*; amortizes leader cost across many instances.
- [x] **Raft** is decomposed into Leader Election, Log Replication, and Safety for understandability.
- [x] Raft servers cycle among **Follower, Candidate, Leader** states; **terms** are monotonic logical clocks.
- [x] Election timeouts are **randomized (150–300 ms)** to break symmetry (avoid perpetual split votes).
- [x] Raft's **up-to-date voting rule**: higher last-term wins; if equal term, longer log wins.
- [x] Raft commits an entry only when it's replicated on a majority *and* belongs to the **current term**.
- [x] **Five Safety Properties of Raft**: Election Safety, Leader Append-Only, Log Matching, Leader Completeness, State Machine Safety.
- [x] **FLP Impossibility** is bypassed by partial synchrony + randomization.
- [x] Real-world users of Raft: **etcd, Consul, CockroachDB, TiKV, Hyperledger Fabric orderers**.
- [x] Real-world users of Multi-Paxos: **Google Spanner, Chubby, Megastore**.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line high-yield mnemonic for the exam:**
> **"Paxos = Two-Phase Ballot. Raft = Three-State Replicated Logbook. Both need $2f+1$ nodes and $f+1$ quorum."**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Byzantine Fault-Tolerance (BFT) Algorithms - Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Byzantine Fault-Tolerance (BFT) Algorithms - Practical Byzantine Fault-Tolerance (PBFT)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Byzantine Generals Problem (The Origin of BFT)

The **Byzantine Generals Problem** is a classical distributed systems dilemma formally introduced by **Lamport, Shostak, and Pease (1982)**. It describes a situation in which a group of generals, each commanding a portion of the Byzantine army, surrounds an enemy city. The generals must agree on a unified battle plan — either all **attack** or all **retreat** — by communicating only through messengers. However, some of the generals may be **traitors** (faulty/malicious) and may send conflicting messages to disrupt consensus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):**
> A *Byzantine fault* is an arbitrary, malicious, or inconsistent failure of a distributed system node in which the node may behave arbitrarily — including sending conflicting information to different peers. *Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)* is the property of a distributed system to reach **consensus** despite the presence of such malicious or faulty nodes.

## 1.2 What is Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)?

**Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT)** is a class of consensus protocols designed to ensure that a distributed network of nodes can agree on a single state of the system (the "consensus value") even if some of the nodes are faulty or actively malicious.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties Every BFT Protocol Must Satisfy (Fischer–Lynch–Paterson impossibility result):**
> 1. **Agreement** — All honest (non-faulty) nodes agree on the same value.
> 2. **Validity** — The agreed value must be proposed by an honest node.
> 3. **Termination** — Every honest node eventually decides on a value.
> 4. **Integrity** — No node decides on more than one value.

## 1.3 What is Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT)?

**PBFT** is the first practical, efficient solution to the Byzantine Generals Problem, proposed by **Miguel Castro and Barbara Liskov in 1999**. It transforms a theoretical impossibility into a practical, real-world protocol used today in **Hyperledger Fabric**, **Cosmos (Tendermint)**, and many other distributed ledgers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Castro & Liskov, 1999):**
> *Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT) is a replicated state machine protocol that tolerates Byzantine (malicious) failures of up to ⌊(n−1)/3⌋ replicas while providing liveness and safety as long as at least 2f + 1 honest replicas respond to a client request.*

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Generals Around the Castle 🏰

Imagine **4 generals** surrounding a castle, communicating only by carrier pigeon:

| General | Role | Status |
|---|---|---|
| General 0 (Commander) | Sends the order | Loyal (Honest) |
| General 1 | Lieutenant | Loyal (Honest) |
| General 2 | Lieutenant | **Traitor** (Faulty) |
| General 3 | Lieutenant | Loyal (Honest) |

- The commander shouts: *"ATTACK at dawn!"*
- But the traitor General 2 whispers to General 3: *"The commander said RETREAT."*
- General 3 is now confused. **BFT solves this** by having the lieutenants **cross-check** all received messages. Once General 3 hears from at least **2 others** (besides the traitor) that the order is ATTACK, he trusts the majority.

In this example, with **n = 4** nodes, the system can tolerate **f = 1** traitor because:
$$n \geq 3f + 1 \implies 4 \geq 3(1) + 1 = 4 \;\; ✅$$

## 1.5 The Significance of PBFT in Blockchain

- **Bitcoin's Proof-of-Work (PoW)** is *probabilistic* BFT (tolerates up to ~49% hashrate attacks).
- **PBFT** is *deterministic* BFT — guarantees finality the moment consensus is reached.
- PBFT achieves consensus **without energy-intensive mining** (no PoW hash solving).
- It provides **low latency finality** (sub-second) — critical for enterprise and permissioned blockchains.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematics of Tolerating f Faults

For a BFT system with **n** total replicas to operate correctly:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

This is **not arbitrary** — it is derived from a fundamental impossibility:

1. The **f faulty nodes** can say anything.
2. To reach consensus, we need **2f + 1** honest nodes (so majority vote > ½ + f).
3. The faulty nodes may also be **silent** (fail-stop), so we need **f** extra slots.
4. Therefore: $n = f + (2f + 1) = 3f + 1$.

## 2.2 System Model Assumptions of PBFT

| Assumption | Description | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Weak Synchrony** | Message delays grow at most exponentially with time | Ensures **Liveness** (progress is eventually made) |
| **Independent Node Failures** | No more than ⌊(n−1)/3⌋ nodes fail simultaneously | Ensures **Safety** (no conflicting decisions) |
| **Authentication** | Use of cryptographic signatures (RSA, ECDSA) on all messages | Prevents spoofing by faulty nodes |
| **Replicated State Machine** | All honest nodes start in the same initial state | Deterministic state transitions |
| **Primary–Backup Topology** | One node acts as **primary** (leader), others are **backups** | View-based leadership rotation |

## 2.3 PBFT Protocol Phases — Step-by-Step Logic

PBFT operates in **rounds** called **views**. In each view, one node is elected as the **primary (leader)** and the others are **backups**. A client request goes through **3 phases** plus a **reply**:

### 🔷 Phase 1: REQUEST (Client → Primary)
- The **client** signs a request `<REQUEST, o, t, c>` and sends it to the **primary**, where:
  - `o` = operation to perform
  - `t` = timestamp
  - `c` = client identifier

### 🔷 Phase 2: PRE-PREPARE (Primary → All Backups)
- The primary assigns a **sequence number `n`** to the request.
