- The primary broadcasts a `<PRE-PREPARE, v, n, d, m>` message to all backups.
  - `v` = view number
  - `d` = digest (hash) of the request `m`

### 🔷 Phase 3: PREPARE (Backups → All Other Backups)
- Each backup verifies the pre-prepare and enters the *prepare* state.
- It broadcasts a `<PREPARE, v, n, d, i>` message to all other nodes.
- A node is considered **prepared** when it has collected **2f matching prepare messages** (including its own) for the same `(v, n, d)`.

### 🔷 Phase 4: COMMIT (All → All)
- Once prepared, each node broadcasts `<COMMIT, v, n, d, i>`.
- A node is **committed-local** when it collects **2f + 1 matching commit messages**.
- This guarantees that **at least f + 1 honest nodes** have agreed, which is a **certificate of agreement** that survives any view change.

### 🔷 Phase 5: REPLY (Backups → Client)
- After executing the operation on the replicated state machine, each backup sends `<REPLY, v, t, c, i, r>` to the client.
- The client waits for **f + 1 matching replies** with the same result `r` — this is the **trusted result**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Voting Rounds (Prepare + Commit)?**
> The **prepare** phase ensures all honest nodes agree on the *order* of requests. The **commit** phase ensures that even if the **primary fails mid-way** and a view change occurs, the decision is already certified by a quorum that can be carried over.

## 2.4 View Change Protocol — How PBFT Handles a Malicious Primary

If the primary is faulty (e.g., not sending pre-prepare messages, or sending conflicting ones), backups trigger a **view change**:

1. Backup suspects the primary → broadcasts `<VIEW-CHANGE, v+1, n, C, P, i>`
   - `C` = set of prepared certificates
   - `P` = set of prepared requests
2. New primary collects **2f + 1 view-change messages** for view `v+1`.
3. New primary broadcasts `<NEW-VIEW, v+1, V, O>`
   - `V` = set of view-change messages
   - `O` = set of pre-prepares to re-issue
4. Backups verify and accept the new view, re-entering normal operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Checkpoint Protocol (Garbage Collection):**
> Every **100 sequence numbers** (configurable), the system creates a **stable checkpoint**. Nodes exchange `<CHECKPOINT, n, d, i>` messages; when **2f + 1 match**, the checkpoint is *stable* and old messages can be discarded.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n \geq 3f + 1$ | Minimum replicas for BFT safety | n = 4 tolerates f = 1 |
| 2 | $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$ | Maximum Byzantine failures tolerated | f = 1 for n = 4 |
| 3 | $\text{Quorum}_{\text{prepare}} = 2f$ | Messages needed to enter prepared state | 2f |
| 4 | $\text{Quorum}_{\text{commit}} = 2f + 1$ | Messages needed to be committed-local | 2f + 1 |
| 5 | $\text{Quorum}_{\text{reply}} = f + 1$ | Replies needed by client to accept result | f + 1 |
| 6 | $\text{Message Complexity} = O(n^2)$ | Total messages per PBFT round | 4 nodes → 16 messages/phase |
| 7 | $\text{Throughput}$ | Validated transactions per second | ~1,000–10,000 TPS |
| 8 | $\text{Latency}$ | Time to finality | Sub-second to a few seconds |
| 9 | $\text{Safety Threshold}$ | Tolerated faulty fraction | $\alpha < 1/3$ |
| 10 | $\text{Finality Type}$ | Deterministic (vs. probabilistic) | Absolute (not probabilistic) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units & Boundary Conditions:**
> - **n** must be an integer ≥ 4.
> - **f** must be an integer ≥ 0.
> - **Safety bound:** faulty fraction $\alpha = f/n < 1/3$.
> - **Liveness bound:** requires $\alpha < 1/3$ **plus** weak synchrony.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility of PBFT

| Application Domain | Use of PBFT | Why PBFT? |
|---|---|---|
| **Hyperledger Fabric (IBM)** | Ordering service (originally) | Permissioned, deterministic, low-latency finality |
| **Cosmos / Tendermint BFT** | Block finalization | Instant finality, no forks |
| **Zilliqa** | Main consensus | High-throughput sharded BFT |
| **Central bank digital currencies (CBDCs)** | Wholesale interbank settlement | Determinism critical for regulatory compliance |
| **Aviation / Spacecraft Control Systems** | Triple-redundant computer consensus | Tolerates arbitrary sensor failures |
| **Aircraft FBW (Fly-By-Wire)** | Triple/Quadruple Modular Redundancy | Classical BFT pattern (n = 3, f = 1) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Fundamental BFT Bound n ≥ 3f + 1

**Claim:** A BFT consensus protocol can reach agreement among n nodes in the presence of f Byzantine (arbitrary) failures **if and only if**:

$$n \geq 3f + 1$$

### Step-by-Step Proof

**Step 1 — Define the problem.** We have a set **R = {R₁, R₂, …, Rₙ}** of n replicas. Exactly **f** of them are Byzantine (may send arbitrary/inconsistent messages). The remaining **n − f** are honest.

**Step 2 — Quorum size requirement.** Any two quorums (majority sets) of honest nodes must **intersect** in at least one honest node. This prevents the *split-brain* problem. If each quorum has size $Q$, then by the **Pigeonhole Principle**:

$$Q + Q > n - f \implies 2Q > n - f \implies Q \geq \lfloor (n-f)/2 \rfloor + 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply to BFT.** In BFT, a quorum must be reachable **even if all f faulty nodes are silent** (fail-stop scenario). So the quorum of **responding** nodes is at most $n - f$. For two such quorums to overlap in at least one **honest** node:

$$2Q \leq n \;\text{(the system has at most n responding nodes)} \;\text{AND}\; Q \geq f + 1 \;\text{(at least one honest in any quorum)}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the constraints.** We need a quorum $Q$ that satisfies:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2Q &\geq f + 1 \quad \text{(at least one honest in overlap)} \\
Q &\leq n - f \quad \text{(at most n − f honest nodes available)}
\end{aligned}
$$

From the second inequality, $n \geq Q + f$. Substituting the worst case $Q = f + 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &\geq (f + 1) + f \\
n &\geq 2f + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the lower bound for **CFT (Crash Fault Tolerance)**, where faulty nodes are merely silent.

**Step 5 — Strengthen for arbitrary (Byzantine) behavior.** Byzantine nodes can lie — so a quorum must contain enough **honest** nodes to outweigh the f liars. We need:

$$Q \geq 2f + 1 \quad \text{(majority of honest among f liars)}$$

(With $2f + 1$ honest votes, the f liars cannot outvote the honest majority.)

**Step 6 — Substitute and solve.** Now replace $Q$ in $n \geq Q + f$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &\geq (2f + 1) + f \\
n &\geq 3f + 1 \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "3" specifically?**
> The "3" in $3f + 1$ emerges from: (1) $f$ faulty that may lie, (2) $f$ honest that may be unreachable (network delay), and (3) $f + 1$ honest that constitute the working quorum. This decomposition is the cornerstone of all modern BFT proofs.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Message Complexity of PBFT

**Claim:** Each PBFT round requires $O(n^2)$ messages.

### Proof

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pre-Prepare:} \quad & 1 \text{ primary} \times (n-1) \text{ backups} = (n-1) \\
\text{Prepare:} \quad & n \text{ nodes} \times (n-1) \text{ peers each} = n(n-1) \\
\text{Commit:} \quad & n \text{ nodes} \times (n-1) \text{ peers each} = n(n-1) \\
\text{Reply:} \quad & n \text{ backups} \times 1 \text{ client} = n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total messages per request:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= (n-1) + n(n-1) + n(n-1) + n \\
M_{total} &= 2n^2 - n - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Asymptotic form:**

$$
M_{total} = O(n^2)
$$

**Numerical example — n = 4:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= 2(4)^2 - 4 - 1 \\
&= 32 - 5 \\
&= 27 \text{ messages per consensus round}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For n = 10:**

$$
M_{total} = 2(100) - 10 - 1 = 189 \text{ messages}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Because PBFT scales as $O(n^2)$, it is **not suitable for public blockchains with thousands of nodes** (e.g., Bitcoin has ~15,000 nodes). PBFT is optimized for **permissioned/consortium blockchains** with $n \leq 100$ (typically $n \leq 20$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Why the Client Waits for Exactly f + 1 Replies

**Claim:** The client can finalize a result after receiving $f + 1$ matching replies.

### Proof

Given:
- Total replicas = $n = 3f + 1$ (worst case)
- Honest replicas = $n - f = 2f + 1$
- Faulty replicas = $f$

After the client broadcasts a request, the client may receive replies from any subset of the $n$ replicas. The client needs to be **certain that the value `r` is the true consensus result**.

**Step 1:** Among the $f + 1$ matching replies, at most $f$ can be faulty (since at most $f$ replicas are Byzantine in total).

**Step 2:** Therefore, at least **1** of the $f + 1$ replies is **honest**.

**Step 3:** An honest replica only sends a reply after collecting $2f + 1$ matching commit messages (a *quorum certificate*). This certificate is unforgeable.

**Step 4:** Any other honest replica that eventually replies will send the **same result `r`**, because the certificate guarantees the same ordered execution.

**Conclusion:** $f + 1$ matching replies is the **minimum** number that guarantees at least one honest reply → the result is correct. ∎

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Consensus Protocols (KTU Exam Favorite)

| Parameter | **PBFT** | **PoW (Bitcoin)** | **PoS (Ethereum 2.0)** | **Raft (CFT)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Fault Model** | Byzantine | Byzantine | Byzantine | Crash only |
| **Tolerance** | $f < n/3$ | $f < 50\%$ hashrate | $f < 33\%$ stake | $f < n/2$ |
| **Finality** | **Deterministic** | Probabilistic | Deterministic (Casper FFG) | Deterministic |
| **Energy** | Low | **Very High** | Low | Low |
| **Throughput** | ~1k–10k TPS | ~7 TPS | ~100k TPS (post-sharding) | ~10k TPS |
| **Latency** | Sub-second | ~60 min/block | ~12 min finality | Sub-second |
| **Message Complexity** | $O(n^2)$ | $O(n)$ gossip | $O(n)$ gossip | $O(n)$ |
| **Leader-based** | Yes (with view-change) | No (mining race) | Yes (block proposer) | Yes |
| **Forks** | No (finality) | Yes (resolves in ~6 blocks) | No (finality) | No |
| **Best For** | Permissioned chains | Public trustless chains | Public stake chains | Trusted datacenters |
| **Examples** | Hyperledger, Cosmos | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Ethereum 2.0, Cardano | etcd, Kubernetes |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: PBFT Quorum Calculation (Board Style)

**Problem:** A consortium blockchain uses PBFT with $n = 7$ replicas.

**(a)** Find the maximum number $f$ of Byzantine failures the system can tolerate.
**(b)** How many prepare messages must a node collect to enter the *prepared* state?
**(c)** How many commit messages must a node collect to be *committed-local*?
**(d)** How many matching replies must the client receive to accept the result?

### Solution

**Part (a):** Using $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \lfloor (7-1)/3 \rfloor \\
&= \lfloor 6/3 \rfloor \\
&= \lfloor 2.0 \rfloor \\
&= 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** $n \geq 3f + 1 \implies 7 \geq 3(2) + 1 = 7$ ✅

**Part (b):** Quorum for prepared state = $2f$:

$$
\text{Quorum}_{\text{prepare}} = 2f = 2(2) = 4 \text{ prepare messages}
$$

**Part (c):** Quorum for committed-local = $2f + 1$:

$$
\text{Quorum}_{\text{commit}} = 2f + 1 = 2(2) + 1 = 5 \text{ commit messages}
$$

**Part (d):** Client reply threshold = $f + 1$:

$$
\text{Quorum}_{\text{reply}} = f + 1 = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ matching replies}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** With 7 replicas, we have 5 honest + 2 faulty. The 3 client replies guarantee at least 1 honest (because at most 2 are faulty) → the result is trustworthy.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: PBFT Phases — Full Round Sequence (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CLIENT signs request m: REQUEST, o, t, c"] --> B["PRIMARY assigns sequence n, broadcasts PRE-PREPARE, v, n, d, m"]
    B --> C1["BACKUP 1 verifies pre-prepare"]
    B --> C2["BACKUP 2 verifies pre-prepare"]
    B --> C3["BACKUP 3 verifies pre-prepare"]
    B --> C4["BACKUP 4 verifies pre-prepare"]
    C1 --> D1["BACKUP 1 broadcasts PREPARE, v, n, d, 1"]
    C2 --> D2["BACKUP 2 broadcasts PREPARE, v, n, d, 2"]
    C3 --> D3["BACKUP 3 broadcasts PREPARE, v, n, d, 3"]
    C4 --> D4["BACKUP 4 broadcasts PREPARE, v, n, d, 4"]
    D1 --> E["All nodes collect 2f matching prepares: PREPARED state"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F1["NODE 1 broadcasts COMMIT, v, n, d, 1"]
    E --> F2["NODE 2 broadcasts COMMIT, v, n, d, 2"]
    E --> F3["NODE 3 broadcasts COMMIT, v, n, d, 3"]
    E --> F4["NODE 4 broadcasts COMMIT, v, n, d, 4"]
    F1 --> G["Each node collects 2f plus 1 matching commits: COMMITTED LOCAL state"]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H["Each backup executes operation o on Replicated State Machine"]
    H --> I["Each backup sends REPLY, v, t, c, i, r to CLIENT"]
    I --> J["CLIENT waits for f plus 1 matching replies with same r"]
    J --> K["RESULT ACCEPTED: client finalizes result r"]
    K --> L{"Is primary suspected as faulty?"}
    L -- YES --> M["Trigger VIEW CHANGE protocol: new primary elected"]
    L -- NO --> N["Continue with next sequence number n plus 1"]
    M --> N
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: PBFT Quorum Intersection & Fault Tolerance Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Universe["Network of n = 3f plus 1 Replicas"]
        H1["Honest 1"]
        H2["Honest 2"]
        H3["Honest 3"]
        H4["Honest 4"]
        H5["Honest 5"]
        H6["Honest 6"]
        F1["Byzantine 1"]
        F2["Byzantine 2"]
    end
    subgraph Q1["Quorum 1: 2f plus 1 = 5 nodes"]
        Q1A["Honest 1"]
        Q1B["Honest 2"]
        Q1C["Honest 3"]
        Q1D["Honest 4"]
        Q1E["Honest 5"]
    end
    subgraph Q2["Quorum 2: 2f plus 1 = 5 nodes"]
        Q2A["Honest 3"]
        Q2B["Honest 4"]
        Q2C["Honest 5"]
        Q2D["Honest 6"]
        Q2E["Byzantine 1"]
    end
    H1 --> Q1A
    H2 --> Q1B
    H3 --> Q1C
    H3 --> Q2A
    H4 --> Q1D
    H4 --> Q2B
    H5 --> Q1E
    H5 --> Q2C
    H6 --> Q2D
    F1 --> Q2E
    Q1A --> X["Intersection of two quorums: at least 2f plus 1 minus f = f plus 1 honest nodes"]
    Q1B --> X
    Q1C --> X
    Q1D --> X
    Q1E --> X
    Q2A --> X
    Q2B --> X
    Q2C --> X
    Q2D --> X
    Q2E --> X
    X --> Y["Conclusion: Any two quorums share at least f plus 1 honest nodes. Consensus is safe."]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: PBFT View Change Flow (Faulty Primary Recovery)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["System in View v, Primary P is suspected of being faulty"] --> S1["Backup i sends VIEW-CHANGE, v plus 1, n, C, P, i to all nodes"]
    S1 --> S2["New Primary P prime collects 2f plus 1 VIEW-CHANGE messages for view v plus 1"]
    S2 --> S3{"Does P prime receive 2f plus 1 valid view-change messages?"}
    S3 -- NO --> S4["Wait for more messages or network synchrony to return"]
    S3 -- YES --> S5["P prime broadcasts NEW-VIEW, v plus 1, V, O to all backups"]
    S5 --> S6["Each backup verifies the set V of view-change messages"]
    S6 --> S7{"Is NEW-VIEW valid and includes all prepared certificates from C set?"}
    S7 -- NO --> S8["Backup rejects the new view, triggers another view change v plus 2"]
    S7 -- YES --> S9["Backup accepts the new view v plus 1"]
    S9 --> S10["P prime re-issues PRE-PREPARE for uncommitted prepared requests from set O"]
    S10 --> S11["System resumes normal PBFT operation in View v plus 1"]
    S8 --> S2
    S4 --> S2
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4: BFT in Real Systems (Cross-Domain Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Block["BLOCKCHAIN DOMAIN"]
        B1["Hyperledger Fabric"]
        B2["Cosmos Tendermint"]
        B3["Zilliqa"]
    end
    subgraph Aero["AEROSPACE DOMAIN"]
        A1["Triple Redundant Flight Control Boeing 777"]
        A2["SpaceX Falcon 9 Avionics"]
    end
    subgraph Auto["AUTOMOTIVE DOMAIN"]
        AU1["Brake By Wire Systems"]
        AU2["Autonomous Vehicle Sensor Fusion"]
    end
    subgraph Fin["FINANCIAL DOMAIN"]
        F1["SWIFT Messaging Redundancy"]
        F2["CBDC Wholesale Settlement"]
    end
    subgraph Core["CORE BFT PRINCIPLE"]
        C1["n greater than or equal to 3f plus 1"]
        C2["Deterministic Consensus"]
        C3["View Change on Leader Failure"]
    end
    C1 --> B1
    C1 --> B2
    C1 --> B3
    C1 --> A1
    C1 --> A2
    C1 --> AU1
    C1 --> AU2
    C1 --> F1
    C1 --> F2
    C2 --> B1
    C3 --> B2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Explain the Byzantine Generals Problem with a suitable analogy. State any two properties that a BFT consensus protocol must satisfy.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

The **Byzantine Generals Problem** (Lamport, Shostak, Pease, 1982) is a fundamental problem in distributed computing where a group of generals surrounding a common enemy must coordinate their attack using only messengers, but some generals may be traitors who send conflicting information. The goal is to reach **unanimous agreement** despite the presence of traitors.

**Analogy:** Imagine 4 generals voting on a battle plan. One traitor general sends "ATTACK" to some and "RETREAT" to others. The loyal generals must use a protocol that allows them to **cross-verify** messages and reach the same decision, even if the traitor tries to split them.

**Two Key Properties (KTU expects these verbatim):**
1. **Agreement** — All honest (non-faulty) nodes must agree on the same value.
2. **Validity** — The agreed value must have been proposed by an honest node.

(Additional acceptable: **Termination**, **Integrity**.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark distribution:** 1.5 for analogy + 1.5 for properties.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
**Define Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT). Why is the threshold 3f + 1 necessary?**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition:** PBFT (Castro & Liskov, 1999) is a practical consensus protocol for replicated state machines that tolerates **f Byzantine (malicious) failures out of n = 3f + 1 total replicas** while providing both **safety** (no conflicting decisions) and **liveness** (eventual decision-making) under weak synchrony assumptions.

**Why 3f + 1?**
- We need **2f + 1** honest nodes to outvote **f** malicious nodes.
- We need **f** additional replicas to handle the case where faulty nodes are also silent (fail-stop).
- Hence: $n = (2f + 1) + f = 3f + 1$.
- This bound is **tight** — it is the maximum fault tolerance achievable with deterministic consensus in an asynchronous network (per the FLP impossibility result).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — Choice 1** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **three phases of the PBFT protocol** with a neat diagram. Explain how the protocol handles a faulty primary.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A consortium blockchain uses PBFT with **n = 10** replicas. Calculate:
- (i) Maximum number of Byzantine failures tolerated
- (ii) Number of prepare messages needed to enter the prepared state
- (iii) Number of commit messages needed to be committed-local
- (iv) Number of replies the client must receive
- (v) Total number of messages exchanged in one full PBFT round

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — The Three Phases of PBFT:**

The three phases of PBFT are **Pre-Prepare**, **Prepare**, and **Commit**.

**Phase 1: Pre-Prepare**
- The **client** sends a signed request to the **primary** (leader) of the current view.
- The primary assigns a **sequence number `n`** to the request and broadcasts a `<PRE-PREPARE, v, n, d, m>` message to all backup replicas, where `v` is the view number, `d` is the digest of the request `m`.

**Phase 2: Prepare**
- Each backup verifies the pre-prepare message (checks signatures, view number, sequence number, digest).
- If valid, the backup enters the *prepare* state and broadcasts `<PREPARE, v, n, d, i>` to all other replicas.
- A replica is **prepared** when it has collected **2f matching prepare messages** (including its own) for the same `(v, n, d)`.

**Phase 3: Commit**
- Once a replica is in the prepared state, it broadcasts `<COMMIT, v, n, d, i>` to all replicas.
- A replica is **committed-local** when it has received **2f + 1 matching commit messages** (the "commit certificate").
- After this, the replica executes the operation on the **replicated state machine** and sends a `<REPLY>` to the client.

**Handling a Faulty Primary — View Change Protocol:**

If a backup suspects the primary is faulty (e.g., not sending pre-prepare messages within a timeout, or sending conflicting orders):
1. The backup broadcasts a `<VIEW-CHANGE, v+1, n, C, P, i>` message containing its **prepared certificates**.
2. The new primary (determined by `p = v mod n`) collects **2f + 1 view-change messages**.
3. The new primary broadcasts `<NEW-VIEW, v+1, V, O>` to all backups.
4. Backups verify the new view and accept it. The new primary re-issues pre-prepares for all uncommitted prepared requests from the set `O`.
5. Normal operation resumes in the new view `v + 1`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks allocation for (a):** Pre-prepare (1.5) + Prepare (1.5) + Commit (1.5) + View change handling (2.5).

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation for n = 10:**

**(i) Maximum Byzantine failures tolerated:**

$$
f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor = \lfloor (10-1)/3 \rfloor = \lfloor 9/3 \rfloor = \lfloor 3.0 \rfloor = 3
$$

**Verification:** $n \geq 3f + 1 \implies 10 \geq 3(3) + 1 = 10$ ✅

**(ii) Prepare quorum (= 2f):**

$$
\text{Prepare quorum} = 2f = 2(3) = 6 \text{ prepare messages}
$$

**(iii) Commit quorum (= 2f + 1):**

$$
\text{Commit quorum} = 2f + 1 = 2(3) + 1 = 7 \text{ commit messages}
$$

**(iv) Client reply threshold (= f + 1):**

$$
\text{Reply threshold} = f + 1 = 3 + 1 = 4 \text{ matching replies}
$$

**(v) Total messages in one full PBFT round:**

Using the derived formula $M_{total} = 2n^2 - n - 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= 2(10)^2 - 10 - 1 \\
&= 2(100) - 11 \\
&= 200 - 11 \\
&= 189 \text{ messages}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks allocation for (b):** 1 mark per sub-part × 5 sub-parts = 5 marks + 2 marks for final step-by-step presentation.

---

### **Question B — Choice 2** (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Supplementary Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare **PBFT**, **Proof-of-Work (PoW)**, and **Proof-of-Stake (PoS)** consensus mechanisms across **8 parameters**. State one real-world blockchain example for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive mathematically why the **3f + 1 bound** is the **minimum** number of replicas needed for a BFT system to guarantee both **safety** and **liveness**. Show all steps clearly.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (8 parameters + examples):**

| # | Parameter | **PBFT** | **PoW (Bitcoin)** | **PoS (Ethereum 2.0)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Fault Tolerance** | f < n/3 | < 50% hashrate | < 33% stake + 51% stake for long-range |
| 2 | **Finality Type** | Deterministic (immediate) | Probabilistic (≈6 blocks) | Deterministic (Casper FFG) |
| 3 | **Energy Consumption** | Low | Very High (mining) | Low (validation) |
| 4 | **Throughput (TPS)** | 1,000 – 10,000 | ≈ 7 | ≈ 100,000 (with sharding) |
| 5 | **Latency** | Sub-second | ~60 minutes/block | ~12 minutes (finality) |
| 6 | **Leader Selection** | Round-robin / p = v mod n | Mining race (first to find nonce) | Stake-weighted lottery |
| 7 | **Message Complexity** | O(n²) per round | O(n) gossip | O(n) gossip |
| 8 | **Fork Resolution** | No forks | Longest chain rule | LMD-GHOST + Casper |
| 9 | **Example Blockchain** | **Hyperledger Fabric** | **Bitcoin** | **Ethereum 2.0** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks allocation:** 0.5 mark per cell × 24 cells = 12 → cap at 7 marks (examiners award proportional credit for clear comparison).

---

**Part (b) — Derivation of 3f + 1 Bound:**

We want to find the **minimum n** such that a deterministic consensus protocol can:
- (S1) Guarantee **safety** — no two honest nodes decide on different values.
- (S2) Guarantee **liveness** — every honest node eventually decides.

**Step 1:** Since f replicas are Byzantine, the number of **honest replicas** is $n - f$.

**Step 2:** For safety, we need the set of **honest replicas** to form a **majority** over the f liars. So:

$$
n - f > f \implies n > 2f
$$

**Step 3:** For liveness, the protocol must make progress even in the worst case. The worst case is when the network partitions and **f replicas become unreachable** in addition to the f Byzantine ones. So the surviving honest set has size $n - 2f$, and to make progress we need at least one honest replica remaining:

$$
n - 2f \geq 1 \implies n \geq 2f + 1
$$

But this is only the **CFT (crash fault tolerance)** bound — it does not account for *lies* by Byzantine replicas.

**Step 4:** For BFT, a **quorum** must contain a majority of *honest* nodes so that Byzantine nodes cannot swing the vote. The smallest honest majority that outweighs f liars is **2f + 1** (because f liars + f honest = tie, so we need one more honest = f + 1, giving $f + f + 1 = 2f + 1$).

**Step 5:** The quorum size is bounded above by the total available honest nodes plus the worst-case silent Byzantine nodes:

$$
\text{Quorum size} \leq n - f \quad \text{(when f are silent but counted in n)}
$$

**Step 6:** Setting the honest-majority quorum requirement and the availability constraint together:

$$
2f + 1 \leq n - f
$$

**Step 7:** Solving for n:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2f + 1 &\leq n - f \\
2f + 1 + f &\leq n \\
3f + 1 &\leq n \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Tightness argument:** The bound is tight because:
- With $n = 3f$, two disjoint quorums of size $2f$ can form (one consisting of $f$ honest + $f$ liars, the other of $f$ honest + $f$ liars), but they share no honest overlap → split-brain.
- Hence $n = 3f$ is **insufficient** and $n = 3f + 1$ is the **exact minimum**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks allocation:** 1 mark per step × 7 steps + 1 mark for tightness argument = 8 → capped at 7.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Marks Deduction:**
>
> 1. **Confusing quorums:** Students often write "client needs 2f + 1 replies" — WRONG. The client needs only **f + 1** matching replies. The 2f + 1 quorum is for the **commit** phase, not the client reply.
>
> 2. **Wrong formula for f:** Some students compute $f = n/3$ instead of $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$. Always use the **floor function** — n must be an integer.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the derivation:** When asked "Why 3f + 1?", students often just state the formula. **You must derive it** using quorum intersection arguments for full marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing safety vs. liveness:** PBFT guarantees **safety always** (no forks) and **liveness only under weak synchrony**. Many students incorrectly claim liveness is unconditional.
>
> 5. **Message complexity:** Students often say PBFT is $O(n)$ — WRONG. It is $O(n^2)$ per round. The exact count is $2n^2 - n - 1$.
>
> 6. **Diagram omission:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the PBFT phases (Pre-prepare → Prepare → Commit → Reply) — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for clean diagrams.
>
> 7. **Mixing up PBFT with PoW/PoS:** When asked to compare, students often write about PoW even when the question asks about PBFT. **Read carefully** — match the protocol to the question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points (Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams):**

✅ **Byzantine Generals Problem** = distributed agreement with f arbitrary/malicious failures (Lamport et al., 1982).

✅ **BFT = Byzantine Fault Tolerance** — property of a system to reach consensus despite f faulty nodes.

✅ **PBFT = Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance** (Castro & Liskov, 1999) — first *practical* BFT protocol.

✅ **Critical Bound:** $n \geq 3f + 1$ ⇒ $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$.

✅ **Three Phases of PBFT:** **Pre-Prepare** (Primary → Backups) → **Prepare** (Backups → All) → **Commit** (All → All) → **Reply** (Backups → Client).

✅ **Quorum Thresholds (MEMORIZE):**
   - Prepared state → **2f** matching prepare messages
   - Committed-local → **2f + 1** matching commit messages
   - Client accepts → **f + 1** matching replies

✅ **Message Complexity:** $O(n^2)$, exactly $2n^2 - n - 1$ messages per round.

✅ **View Change:** Triggered when primary is suspected faulty; uses 2f + 1 view-change messages to elect new primary.

✅ **Checkpoint Protocol:** Every 100 sequence numbers (configurable), creates a stable checkpoint for garbage collection.

✅ **Safety:** Guaranteed always (deterministic) — no forks.

✅ **Liveness:** Guaranteed only under **weak synchrony** (per FLP impossibility).

✅ **Real-world PBFT Users:** Hyperledger Fabric, Cosmos (Tendermint), Zilliqa.

✅ **PBFT vs. PoW:** PBFT is **deterministic, low-energy, low-latency** but $O(n^2)$; PoW is **probabilistic, energy-intensive, slow** but $O(n)$ and scales to thousands of nodes.

✅ **PBFT vs. Raft (CFT):** PBFT tolerates **malicious** faults; Raft tolerates only **crash** faults (f < n/2).

✅ **FLP Impossibility (1985):** No deterministic protocol can guarantee consensus in a purely asynchronous network with even 1 faulty node — PBFT sidesteps this by assuming **weak synchrony** for liveness while keeping safety unconditional.

✅ **Two voting rounds (Prepare + Commit):** Prepare = total order agreement; Commit = certificate that survives view changes.

✅ **Primary rotation formula:** New primary index = `v mod n` (where v is the new view number).

✅ **Standard KTU example values:**
   - n = 4 → f = 1
   - n = 7 → f = 2
   - n = 10 → f = 3
   - n = 13 → f = 4

✅ **Always present a labeled diagram of PBFT phases in 14-mark questions** — visual aids are heavily rewarded in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proof of Work (PoW)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proof of Work (PoW)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof of Work (PoW)** is a **consensus mechanism** originally proposed by Cynthia Dwork and Moni Naor (1993) as a deterrent to denial-of-service attacks and email spam, later adapted by **Satoshi Nakamoto (2008)** as the foundational consensus protocol for the **Bitcoin blockchain**.

In a blockchain context, **Proof of Work (PoW)** is a *cryptoeconomic consensus algorithm* that requires network participants (miners) to expend significant **computational energy** solving a *cryptographic puzzle* — specifically, finding a value (called a **nonce**) such that the cryptographic hash of the block header is numerically smaller than a predefined **target threshold**. The first miner to discover a valid nonce earns the right to append the next block to the chain and receives a **block reward** (subsidy + transaction fees).

Formally, the PoW condition is stated as:

$$H(B_{header} \Vert \text{Nonce}) \;\leq\; T_{target}$$

where $H(\cdot)$ is a cryptographic hash function (**SHA-256** in Bitcoin), $B_{header}$ is the block header, and $T_{target}$ is the target threshold governed by the **network difficulty**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Global Dice-Rolling Lottery":**
> Imagine millions of students worldwide are simultaneously rolling a 19-sided die, each hoping to roll a number **at or below 4**. The die has only a 4/19 ≈ 21% chance per roll of winning. To get a guaranteed result, each student rolls the die billions of times per second, and the **first** student to hit ≤ 4 shouts "FOUND IT!" and wins the prize. The "work" performed is the number of rolls (hash computations), and the "proof" is the winning roll itself, which anyone can instantly verify by re-rolling once with the student's claimed number.
>
> In Bitcoin, the "die" is the **SHA-256 hash function** (producing a uniformly random 256-bit output), and the threshold adjusts every **2016 blocks (≈ 2 weeks)** to keep the average block time at **10 minutes**, regardless of how many miners join the network.

### 1.3 Core Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Nonce** | A 32-bit arbitrary number miners increment to vary the hash output |
| **Target ($T$)** | A 256-bit number; the hash must be *less than or equal to* this value |
| **Difficulty ($D$)** | The inverse-relationship metric of target: $D = \dfrac{D_{max}}{T}$ |
| **Hash Rate ($H_r$)** | Number of hash attempts per second by a miner or the network |
| **Block Reward** | Newly minted coins + transaction fees given to the winning miner |
| **Halving** | The programmed 50% reduction in block subsidy every **210,000 blocks** (≈ 4 years) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Six Pillars of PoW

1. **Transaction Bundling** → Miners collect unconfirmed transactions from the **mempool** and assemble them into a candidate block.
2. **Block Header Construction** → A block header is built containing: previous block hash, Merkle root of transactions, timestamp, difficulty bits, and the nonce.
3. **Cryptographic Puzzle Solving** → The miner iterates over trillions of nonces, hashing `SHA-256(SHA-256(header))` until the resulting digest is $\leq T_{target}$.
4. **Broadcast & Verification** → The winning block is broadcast; every full node independently verifies the PoW in **microseconds** (asymmetric work-to-verification ratio).
5. **Chain Extension & Consensus** → Honest nodes accept the longest valid chain (highest cumulative work), resolving **fork conflicts**.
6. **Reward Distribution** → The miner claims the **coinbase subsidy** (currently 3.125 BTC post-2024 halving) and aggregated **gas fees**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PoW Works — Game-Theoretic Foundation:**
> PoW is *Sybil-resistant* because influence in the network is proportional to **computational power (hash rate)**, not to identity, coins, or stake. Attacking the network requires a sustained **51% of total hash rate**, which is economically irrational since attacker hardware is a sunk cost and the cryptocurrency's value (which the attack would devalue) exceeds the attack profit.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical and Part B question on PoW.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H(B\Vert N) \le T$ | Core PoW condition | SHA-256 output $\le$ target |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{D_{1}}{T}$ | Difficulty = max target ÷ current target | $D_1 = 0x1d00ffff$ in Bitcoin genesis |
| 3 | $E[X] = 2^{d}$ | Expected hash attempts for $d$ leading zero bits | Geometric distribution mean |
| 4 | $P(\text{success}) = \dfrac{T}{2^{256}}$ | Probability per hash attempt | Uniform hash distribution |
| 5 | $T_{block} = \dfrac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_r}$ | Expected block time | Seconds per block |
| 6 | $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \dfrac{T_{actual}}{T_{expected}}$ | Difficulty adjustment rule | Recalibrated every 2016 blocks |
| 7 | $R_n = R_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n}$ | Block reward after $n$ halvings | $R_0 = 50$ BTC; halving every 210,000 blocks |
| 8 | $C_{attack} \ge 0.51 \cdot H_{total} \cdot c_{hw}$ | Minimum 51% attack hardware cost | $c_{hw}$ = per-unit hardware + energy cost |
| 9 | $\text{Energy per block} = H_{per\,block} \cdot E_{J/hash}$ | Energy expenditure per mined block | Joules; $E_{J/hash}$ ≈ J/TH for ASICs |
| 10 | $\text{Total BTC} = \sum_{i=0}^{32} 210{,}000 \cdot R_i$ | Maximum Bitcoin supply ≈ 21 million | Geometric series |

> **Note on notation:** $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) is rendered as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of PoW

- **Decentralized timestamping** — Provides an unforgeable, neutral chronological order to digital events.
- **Sybil attack prevention** in permissionless networks.
- **Censorship-resistant digital cash** (Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin, Monero).
- **Cross-border settlement** without intermediaries (≈ 10 min finality in Bitcoin).
- **Cybersecurity analogies** — Hashcash (anti-spam), Kryptonite (DoS protection), Tor middle-node rate-limiting.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Expected Number of Hash Attempts to Find a Valid Nonce

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption:** SHA-256 outputs are uniformly distributed over $[0,\, 2^{256}-1]$, a standard cryptographic assumption for any secure hash function.

**Step 1 — Define the success event.**
A single hash attempt succeeds if:

$$Y_i = H(B \Vert N_i) \;\leq\; T$$

Since $H(\cdot)$ is uniform over $2^{256}$ values, the probability of success in a single trial is:

$$p = P(Y_i \leq T) = \frac{T}{2^{256}}$$

**Step 2 — Express target via leading-zero bits.**
Let the target require the hash to start with $d$ leading zero bits. Then:

$$T = \frac{2^{256}}{2^{d}} = 2^{256-d}$$

Substituting into the probability:

$$p = \frac{2^{256-d}}{2^{256}} = 2^{-d} = \frac{1}{2^{d}}$$

**Step 3 — Model as geometric distribution.**
The number of trials $X$ until the first success follows a **Geometric distribution**:

$$P(X = k) = (1-p)^{k-1} \cdot p, \quad k = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

**Step 4 — Compute the expected value.**
For a geometric random variable, $E[X] = \dfrac{1}{p}$:

$$E[X] = \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{2^{-d}} = 2^{d}$$

$$\boxed{\,E[X] = 2^{d}\,}$$

**Interpretation:** If a target requires **20 leading zero bits**, a miner expects, on average, $2^{20} \approx 1{,}048{,}576$ hash attempts to find a valid nonce.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Bitcoin Difficulty Adjustment Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Bitcoin recalibrates difficulty every **2016 blocks** to maintain a target block time of **10 minutes** (i.e., 2016 blocks × 10 min = 14 days).

**Step 1 — Define actual vs. expected time.**
Actual time taken for the last 2016 blocks:

$$T_{actual} = \sum_{i=1}^{2016} t_i \quad \text{(seconds)}$$

Expected time:

$$T_{expected} = 2016 \times 10 \times 60 = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Adjustment ratio.**
Let $\rho$ be the ratio:

$$\rho = \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{expected}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply bounded adjustment (4× limit).**
To prevent wild oscillations due to hash rate swings:

$$\rho_{\text{clamped}} = \max\!\left(\tfrac{1}{4},\; \min\!\left(4,\; \rho\right)\right)$$

**Step 4 — Compute new difficulty.**

$$\boxed{\;D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \rho_{\text{clamped}}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Derive new target from difficulty.**

$$T_{new} = \frac{T_{max}}{D_{new}} = \frac{T_{max}}{D_{old} \cdot \rho_{\text{clamped}}} = \frac{T_{old}}{\rho_{\text{clamped}}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Block Time from Hash Rate

**Step 1 — Hashes required per block.**
At difficulty $D$ and 32-bit target expansion, the expected hashes to solve one block:

$$N_{hash} = D \cdot 2^{32}$$

**Step 2 — Time = Work ÷ Rate.**

$$T_{block} = \frac{N_{hash}}{H_r} = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_r}$$

$$\boxed{\;T_{block} = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_r}\;}$$

**Example:** At $D = 90{,}000{,}000{,}000{,}000$ and $H_r = 600 \text{ EH/s} = 6 \times 10^{20}$ H/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{block} &= \frac{9 \times 10^{13} \cdot 2^{32}}{6 \times 10^{20}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{9 \times 10^{13} \cdot 4.295 \times 10^{9}}{6 \times 10^{20}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.866 \times 10^{23}}{6 \times 10^{20}} \\[4pt]
&\approx 644 \text{ s} \approx 10.7 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Total Bitcoin Supply (Geometric Series)

The block reward halves every 210,000 blocks. Starting at $R_0 = 50$ BTC:

**Step 1 — Reward in the $n$-th epoch.**

$$R_n = \frac{50}{2^{n}} \text{ BTC}$$

**Step 2 — BTC minted in the $n$-th epoch.**

$$S_n = 210{,}000 \cdot R_n = \frac{210{,}000 \cdot 50}{2^{n}} = \frac{10{,}500{,}000}{2^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the infinite geometric series.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{total} &= \sum_{n=0}^{32} S_n = 10{,}500{,}000 \sum_{n=0}^{32} \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} \\[4pt]
&= 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot \frac{1 - (1/2)^{33}}{1 - 1/2} \\[4pt]
&= 10{,}500{,}000 \cdot 2 \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{2^{33}}\right) \\[4pt]
&= 21{,}000{,}000 \cdot \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{8{,}589{,}934{,}592}\right) \\[4pt]
&\approx 20{,}999{,}999.9769 \text{ BTC}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$\boxed{\;S_{total} \approx 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC (Bitcoin's hard cap)}\;}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison with Other Consensus

| Property | Proof of Work | Proof of Stake | Delegated PoS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resource | Electricity + ASICs | Locked capital | Locked + votes |
| Attack cost | Buy hash power | Acquire 51% stake | Buy 51% delegates |
| Finality | Probabilistic | Often deterministic | Deterministic |
| Energy use | **Very high** | Low | Low |
| Decentralization | High (in theory) | Wealth-concentrated | Concentrated by design |
| Examples | Bitcoin, Litecoin | Ethereum (post-Merge) | EOS, Tron |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The PoW Mining Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Miner Receives Candidate Block] --> B[Assemble Block Header]
    B --> C[Set Nonce N = 0]
    C --> D[Compute H = SHA256 SHA256 Header N]
    D --> E{H is less than or equal to Target T}
    E -- No --> F[Increment Nonce N = N + 1]
    F --> D
    E -- Yes --> G[Broadcast Winning Block to P2P Network]
    G --> H[Full Nodes Verify PoW in Microseconds]
    H --> I[Honest Nodes Append Block to Longest Chain]
    I --> J[Winning Miner Claims Coinbase Reward and Tx Fees]
    J --> K[Network Triggers Difficulty Recalibration Every 2016 Blocks]
    K --> L[End of Cycle]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Header Architecture and Hash Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PrevHash[Previous Block Hash 32 bytes] --> Header[Block Header 80 bytes]
    MerkleRoot[Merkle Root 32 bytes] --> Header
    Time[Unix Timestamp 4 bytes] --> Header
    DiffBits[Difficulty Bits nBits 4 bytes] --> Header
    Nonce[Nonce 32 bits] --> Header
    Header --> Hash1[First SHA256 Pass]
    Hash1 --> Hash2[Second SHA256 Pass]
    Hash2 --> Digest[256 bit Final Hash Digest]
    Digest --> Check{Hash less than Target}
    Check -- Yes --> Valid[Valid Block Candidate]
    Check -- No --> Invalid[Retry with New Nonce]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: 51% Attack Cost-Benefit Tradeoff

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attacker Aims to Rewrite History] --> B[Acquire 51 percent of Network Hash Rate]
    B --> C[Hardware Procurement: ASICs and Power Supply]
    B --> D[Sustained Energy Costs: Megawatts continuous]
    B --> E[Opportunity Cost: Mining honest is more profitable]
    C --> F[Total Attack Cost C attack]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{C attack less than Double Spend Profit}
    G -- Yes --> H[Rationally Attack]
    G -- No --> I[Irrational: Honest Mining Dominates]
    H --> J[Network Value Plummets: Attacker Hardware Worthless]
    J --> K[Conclusion: Nash Equilibrium Favors Honesty]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Difficulty Adjustment Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Measure Actual Time for Last 2016 Blocks] --> B[Compute Ratio rho]
    B --> C{rho is within 0.25 to 4}
    C -- Yes --> D[Apply rho as Adjustment Factor]
    C -- No --> E[Clamp rho to bounds 0.25 or 4]
    E --> D
    D --> F[New Difficulty = Old Difficulty times rho]
    F --> G[New Target = Old Target divided by rho]
    G --> H[Broadcast New Bits to All Nodes]
    H --> I[Miners Adjust Hash Rate Strategies]
    I --> J[Repeat Every 2016 Blocks Roughly 2 Weeks]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define Proof of Work. What is the role of the nonce in a PoW consensus mechanism?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Proof of Work (PoW)** is a consensus algorithm in which miners must solve a computationally intensive cryptographic puzzle — finding a **nonce** $N$ such that $H(B_{header}\Vert N) \le T_{target}$ — to earn the right to append a new block. **(1.5 marks)**
> The **nonce** is a 32-bit arbitrary value that miners vary iteratively. By changing the nonce, the miner produces a new hash digest; the goal is to discover a hash below the target threshold. The nonce enables **brute-force search** over the hash space, making mining a *probabilistic lottery* governed by the network's difficulty. **(1.5 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] List any three limitations or criticisms of the Proof of Work mechanism.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **High energy consumption** — Bitcoin alone consumes an estimated 150+ TWh annually, comparable to mid-sized nations like Poland. **(1 mark)**
> 2. **Centralization of mining** — Economies of scale favor large mining pools, leading to concentration of hash power (top 4 pools often exceed 50%). **(1 mark)**
> 3. **Scalability bottleneck** — Block times and block sizes are intentionally limited, yielding only ≈ 7 transactions per second in Bitcoin. **(1 mark)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Writing $H(\text{Block}) \le T$ **without specifying the nonce** → loses 1–2 marks. Always write $H(B\Vert N) \le T$.
> - Confusing **difficulty** with **target**; remember $D = T_{max}/T$ (inverse relationship).
> - Forgetting the **2016-block adjustment window** in difficulty-retargeting questions.
> - Skipping units in numerical answers (always state **seconds**, **BTC**, or **hashes/sec**).
> - Treating PoW as "only for Bitcoin" — credit partial answers that mention **Hashcash, Litecoin, Dogecoin, Monero, Ethereum (pre-Merge)**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Cryptographic Mechanism & Mathematical Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step operational flow of the **Proof of Work (PoW)** consensus algorithm used in Bitcoin. Clearly mention the **block header fields**, the **double SHA-256 hashing**, the **target threshold**, and the **verification asymmetry** between miners and full nodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Bitcoin-like blockchain sets the difficulty such that the target requires a hash to start with **$d = 19$ leading zero bits**. Calculate:
   (i) the **probability of success per hash attempt**, and
   (ii) the **expected number of attempts** for a miner to find a valid nonce. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Operational Flow of PoW (7 marks):**
1. **Transaction Collection (1 mark):** Miners gather unconfirmed transactions from the mempool and construct a Merkle tree, deriving a single **Merkle root** summarizing all transactions.
2. **Block Header Assembly (1 mark):** The 80-byte block header contains 6 fields: (i) version (4 bytes), (ii) previous block's hash (32 bytes), (iii) Merkle root (32 bytes), (iv) timestamp (4 bytes), (v) difficulty bits `nBits` (4 bytes), (vi) nonce (4 bytes).
3. **Double SHA-256 Computation (1 mark):** The miner computes $\text{digest} = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{header}))$, producing a uniform 256-bit output.
4. **Target Comparison (1 mark):** The miner checks if $\text{digest} \le T_{target}$. If not, it increments the nonce (or modifies the coinbase/extra-nonce) and rehashes — averaging $2^d$ attempts.
5. **Verification Asymmetry (2 marks):** Once a valid nonce is found, broadcasting the block allows any full node to **verify** the proof by computing **one** SHA-256(SHA-256(...)) call. This is the *asymmetric work-to-verification* property: trillions of hashes to mine, but one hash to verify.
6. **Chain Extension (1 mark):** The longest valid chain (highest cumulative work) becomes canonical. The miner collects the coinbase reward and transaction fees.

**(b) — Mathematical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Probability of success per attempt (3.5 marks):**
Given $d = 19$ leading zero bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \frac{1}{2^{d}} = \frac{1}{2^{19}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{1}{524{,}288} \\[4pt]
&\approx 1.907 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Expected number of attempts (3.5 marks):**
By geometric distribution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= \frac{1}{p} = 2^{19} = 524{,}288 \text{ attempts} \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.24 \times 10^{5} \text{ hash computations}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Difficulty Adjustment & Economic Analysis*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Derive the **Bitcoin difficulty adjustment formula**. Explain why the adjustment is bounded by a factor of **4**, and what would happen if there were no upper bound. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** At a certain epoch, the Bitcoin network has a **difficulty $D = 8.4 \times 10^{13}$** and a **total hash rate of $400$ EH/s**. Calculate the **expected block time** and the **expected number of hashes** required to mine one block. If the actual time for the last 2016 blocks was **12 days** instead of 14 days, calculate the **new difficulty**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Difficulty Adjustment Derivation (7 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The protocol retargets difficulty every 2016 blocks to keep the **mean inter-block time at 600 s (10 min)**, achieving a stable monetary issuance rate immune to hash-rate volatility.

**Derivation (5 marks):**
1. Let $T_{actual}$ = actual seconds elapsed for the last 2016 blocks.
2. Let $T_{expected} = 2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600$ s (2 weeks).
3. Adjustment ratio: $\rho = T_{actual}/T_{expected}$.
4. New difficulty: $D_{new} = D_{old} \cdot \rho$, equivalently $T_{new} = T_{old} / \rho$.

**Bound explanation (2 marks):**
The $\pm 4\times$ clamp prevents catastrophic swings. Without an upper bound, a sudden hash-rate drop (e.g., 80% of miners offline) could trigger an extreme difficulty reduction, causing block times to collapse to seconds and enabling massive **re-org attacks** via cheap double-spends. The 4× bound limits adjustment to a 4× change every 2 weeks, ensuring slow adaptation.

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Expected block time (2.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{block} &= \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{H_r} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8.4 \times 10^{13} \cdot 4.295 \times 10^{9}}{4 \times 10^{20}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.608 \times 10^{23}}{4 \times 10^{20}} \\[4pt]
&\approx 902 \text{ s} \approx 15.03 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hashes per block (1.5 marks):**

$$
N_{hash} = D \cdot 2^{32} = 8.4 \times 10^{13} \cdot 4.295 \times 10^{9} \approx 3.608 \times 10^{23} \text{ hashes}
$$

**New difficulty after 12-day epoch (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{T_{actual}}{T_{expected}} = \frac{12 \times 24 \times 3600}{1{,}209{,}600} = \frac{1{,}036{,}800}{1{,}209{,}600} = 0.857 \\[4pt]
D_{new} &= D_{old} \cdot \rho = 8.4 \times 10^{13} \cdot 0.857 \\[4pt]
&\approx 7.20 \times 10^{13}
\end{aligned}
$$

The difficulty **decreases by ~14.3%** to compensate for the faster-than-expected block production.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ **PoW condition:** $H(B_{header}\Vert N) \le T_{target}$ — must write the **nonce explicitly**.
- ✅ **Hash function in Bitcoin:** Double **SHA-256** (used for both mining and block IDs).
- ✅ **Block time target:** 10 minutes (Bitcoin), 2.5 minutes (Litecoin).
- ✅ **Adjustment window:** Every **2016 blocks** ≈ 2 weeks.
- ✅ **Adjustment bounds:** $\rho \in [0.25,\; 4]$ — 4× maximum swing.
- ✅ **Probability per attempt:** $p = 1/2^d$ where $d$ = required leading zero bits.
- ✅ **Expected attempts:** $E[X] = 2^d$ — *Geometric distribution mean*.
- ✅ **Block time formula:** $T_{block} = D \cdot 2^{32}/H_r$.
- ✅ **Halving interval:** Every **210,000 blocks** (~4 years); reward halves: 50 → 25 → 12.5 → 6.25 → 3.125 BTC.
- ✅ **Total supply cap:** **21 million BTC** (geometric series limit).
- ✅ **51% Attack cost:** Must control **>50% of network hash rate**; economically irrational due to hardware/energy sunk cost and value-depreciation risk.
- ✅ **Verification asymmetry:** Mining = $O(2^d)$ hashes; Verification = $O(1)$ hash.
- ✅ **Pre-image resistance** of SHA-256 is the **cryptographic foundation** of PoW security.
- ✅ **Finality:** Probabilistic — a block is "settled" after ~6 confirmations (~1 hour in Bitcoin).
- ✅ **Common mining hardware:** ASICs (Application-Specific Integrated Circuits); GPUs are obsolete for Bitcoin since ~2013.
- ✅ **Major PoW networks to remember:** Bitcoin, Litecoin, Dogecoin, Monero, Bitcoin Cash, Zcash (Equihash), Ethereum Classic.

> **Final Exam Tip:** In KTU 14-mark questions, always **(1)** define terms, **(2)** state the governing equation, **(3)** show every algebraic step, and **(4)** provide a real-world interpretation. Examiners reward *clarity and completeness* over elegance.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proof of Stake (PoS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Proof of Stake (PoS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Proof of Stake (PoS)** is a *Sybil-resistant* consensus mechanism used in permissionless and consortium blockchain networks, in which the right to propose, validate, and append a new block to the canonical chain is granted probabilistically to network participants (called **validators**) in direct proportion to the quantity of the network's native cryptographic tokens they have locked as collateral (called their **stake**) in a time-bonded escrow smart contract.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> PoS is positioned as the *energy-efficient, economic-incentive-based* successor to Proof of Work (PoW). The examiner expects you to know that PoS replaces *computational burn* (hash-rate competition) with *economic commitment* (capital lock-up).

The fundamental rule governing validator selection in vanilla PoS is:

$$P_i \;=\; \frac{S_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} S_j}$$

where $P_i$ is the probability that validator $i$ is selected to forge (produce) the next block, and $S_i$ is the number of staked tokens held by validator $i$ out of the total staked supply across $n$ active validators.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Cooperative Society Dividend Vote**
> Imagine 100 members of a cooperative society each pledging a certain amount of money (their "stake") into a shared community fund. Once a year, the society must elect one treasurer to manage the books. Instead of running a costly election campaign (Proof of Work = burning fuel in a race), the society simply says: *"The probability that any member becomes treasurer is exactly equal to the fraction of the total fund that member contributed."* If Anu pledged **40 %** of the fund, she has a **40 %** chance of being selected. If Anu misuses the books, her pledged money is **burned (slashed)** — a financial punishment far worse than wasting electricity.

This single analogy encodes **every** critical property of PoS:
- **Stake-weighted voting power** (capital = influence).
- **Incentive alignment** (cheaters lose their own money).
- **Energy efficiency** (no race, just weighted lottery).
- **Cryptoeconomic security** (attack cost = amount of stake you must acquire).

## 1.3 Core Vocabulary (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Term | KTU Definition | Real-World Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Validator** | A node that has locked stake and is eligible to propose/attest blocks | Cooperative treasurer candidate |
| **Staking** | Locking native tokens in a bonded deposit contract | Pledging money to the society |
| **Slashing** | Protocol-enforced destruction of a validator's stake for misbehaviour | Forfeiture of pledged deposit |
| **Attestation** | A vote signed by a validator on the validity of a proposed block | Auditor signing a financial report |
| **Finality** | The point past which a block cannot be reverted without burning ≥ 1/3 of total stake | Court judgement that cannot be appealed |
| **Epoch** | A fixed slot window (e.g., 32 slots × 6.4 min = ~6.8 hr in Ethereum) | A fiscal quarter |
| **Effective Balance** | A validator's stake, capped at **32 ETH**, used in reward calculation | Capped voting share |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Anchor:** Ethereum's transition from PoW to PoS — *"The Merge"* (15 September 2022) — reduced Ethereum's annual energy consumption by approximately **99.65 %**, a frequently cited KTU figure.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Step-by-Step Logic of a PoS Round

A canonical PoS round (modeled on Ethereum's Beacon Chain specification) executes the following deterministic sequence:

1. **Time is sliced into discrete *slots*** (Ethereum: **12 seconds**) and grouped into **epochs** (Ethereum: **32 slots**).
2. For each slot, the RANDAO-based pseudo-random function **selects exactly one validator** as the *block proposer* (a.k.a. the *forger*). The selection weight is:

$$W_{\text{proposer}}(i) \;=\; \frac{E_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} E_j}$$

   where $E_i$ is the validator's **effective balance**, capped at **32 ETH**.

3. A **committee of ~128 validators** is pseudo-randomly assigned to *attest* (vote) on the proposed block in parallel subnets called *attestation subnets*.
4. Each attester signs a message stating (a) which chain head they see, and (b) which justified checkpoint they support. This produces a **Casper FFG vote**.
5. When a block receives attestations representing **≥ 2/3 of the total staked ETH**, it is *justified*; when two consecutive justified blocks exist, the earlier one becomes **finalized**.
6. Validators are rewarded in newly minted native tokens proportional to their duties performed (correct attestation + correct proposal).
7. Validators that double-propose, double-attest, or surround-vote are **slashed** — losing their entire **32 ETH** stake plus a 36-day withdrawal queue penalty.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why discrete slots?** Eliminates race conditions and parallel forks inherent in PoW mining.
- **Why a committee?** Scales BLS signature aggregation — 128 attestations can be compressed into a single 96-byte aggregate signature.
- **Why 2/3 supermajority?** In a BFT-style PoS, ≥ 2/3 honest stake guarantees safety (no two conflicting finalized blocks can coexist) under the assumption that < 1/3 stake is Byzantine.
- **Why slash?** Cryptoeconomic deterrence — the only way to make digital signatures (cheap to forge) **expensive** to mis-use.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **every equation** the KTU examiner has historically used for PoS problems. Memorise these.

| # | Formula / Rule | Meaning | Typical KTU Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P_{\text{select}}(i) \;=\; \dfrac{S_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} S_j}$ | Stake-weighted selection probability | 2 |
| 2 | $E_i \;=\; \min(S_i,\;32\text{ ETH})$ | Effective balance with cap | 1 |
| 3 | $R_{\text{attester}} \;=\; B \cdot \dfrac{E_i}{\sqrt{\Sigma S}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{D}$ | Base attestation reward (Ethereum) | 3 |
| 4 | $R_{\text{proposer}} \;=\; \dfrac{B}{8} \cdot \dfrac{E_i}{32} \cdot D$ | Block-proposer reward | 3 |
| 5 | $C_{\text{attack}} \;\geq\; \dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \Sigma S$ | Cost to finalize a malicious block | 2 |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{PoS}} \;\approx\; \eta_{\text{PoW}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{10^6}$ | Energy reduction ratio | 1 |
| 7 | $\text{Slashed}_{i} \;=\; E_i \;-\; \text{correlation penalty}$ | Penalty magnitude | 2 |
| 8 | $T_{\text{finality}} \;\approx\; 2 \cdot \tau_{\text{epoch}}$ | Two-epoch finality (Casper FFG) | 1 |
| 9 | $\text{Nothing-at-stake cost} = 0$ (without slashing) | Vulnerability metric | 2 |
| 10 | $\Delta_{\text{APR}} \;=\; R_{\text{total}} \;/\; S_i \cdot 100\%$ | Annual Percentage Rate for staker | 2 |

**Legend:**
- $S_i$ = tokens staked by validator $i$
- $\Sigma S$ = total staked tokens
- $B$ = base reward constant (network parameter)
- $D$ = inclusion delay factor (1 if included in 1 slot, else lower)
- $\tau_{\text{epoch}}$ = epoch duration
- $\eta$ = energy expenditure

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Pitfall:** Never confuse $P_{\text{select}}$ (probability of being chosen as proposer) with **attestation weight** (voting influence). Although mathematically identical in vanilla PoS, Casper FFG decouples them via the effective-balance cap.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of PoS in the Real World

PoS is now the **dominant consensus mechanism** in modern distributed-ledger engineering, deployed in:

- **Public Layer-1 chains:** Ethereum (Casper FFG + LMD GHOST), Cardano (Ouroboros), Polkadot (NPoS), Tezos (Emmy+), Solana (PoH + PoS hybrid), Avalanche (Snowman + PoS).
- **Enterprise consortium chains:** Hyperledger Besu + QBFT, Tendermint-based chains.
- **Cross-chain bridges:** Restaking protocols (EigenLayer) reuse staked ETH to secure additional services — economically extending PoS security guarantees.
- **Carbon-credit & ESG registries:** Energy footprint is critical for ESG compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stake-Weighted Selection Probability

**Given:** A PoS network with $n$ validators, where validator $i$ stakes $S_i$ tokens, and total staked supply is $\Sigma S = \sum_{j=1}^{n} S_j$.

**To Prove:** The probability $P_i$ that validator $i$ is chosen as the next block proposer equals $\dfrac{S_i}{\Sigma S}$.

**Derivation:**

We model the validator selection as a discrete probability experiment in which each token acts as an independent "lottery ticket." Since validator $i$ holds $S_i$ tickets out of a total of $\Sigma S$ tickets, the classical probability formula gives:

$$P_i \;=\; \frac{\text{Favourable outcomes}}{\text{Total equally-likely outcomes}}$$

$$P_i \;=\; \frac{S_i}{\Sigma S}$$

When extended to a sequence of $N$ independent slot selections (slots are independent draws because RANDAO + VRF randomize each slot), the probability that validator $i$ is selected **at least once** across $N$ slots follows the binomial complement rule:

$$P_{i,\geq 1} \;=\; 1 \;-\; (1 - P_i)^{N}$$

For large $N$ and small $P_i$ (the realistic regime for a chain with thousands of validators), we use the Poisson approximation:

$$P_{i,\geq 1} \;\approx\; 1 \;-\; e^{-N \cdot P_i}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world sanity check:** With 1 million validators of average 32 ETH and 7,200 slots per day, an average validator proposes roughly once every 35 days — matching Ethereum's empirical observation.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Effective Balance Cap Justification (Ethereum)

**Given:** The protocol wants every validator to influence consensus proportionally to stake, but unbounded stake would let a single entity dominate block production and centralise MEV extraction.

**Derivation of the 32-ETH cap:**

Ethereum's Casper reward function is:

$$R_{\text{attester}} \;=\; 64 \cdot \frac{E_i}{\sqrt{\Sigma S_{\text{active}}}} \cdot \frac{1}{D}$$

We constrain rewards to be a **linear function of stake** without saturating the network. Define effective balance $E_i$ such that:

$$E_i \;=\; \min(S_i,\; C)$$

where $C = 32 \text{ ETH}$ is chosen because, in equilibrium, the protocol targets an **annual issuance rate of ~0.5 %** for the active validator set. Setting the derivative of total issuance w.r.t. stake equal to zero at the cap gives:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial S_i} \left( \frac{E_i}{\sqrt{\Sigma S}} \right) \;\bigg|_{S_i = C} \;=\; 0$$

For a single validator pushing $S_i \to \infty$ while others stay fixed:

$$\lim_{S_i \to \infty} \frac{\partial}{\partial S_i} \left( \frac{\min(S_i, C)}{\sqrt{\Sigma S}} \right) \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{\Sigma S}} \cdot \mathbf{1}_{S_i \leq C}$$

Hence, beyond $C$, additional stake adds **zero** reward and **zero** voting weight — economically compelling users to **spin up multiple 32-ETH validators** rather than concentrate stake.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cost-of-Attack vs Cost-of-Defense

**Given:** Casper FFG finality requires a **2/3 supermajority** of attesting stake. An attacker wishing to finalize two conflicting blocks (a *safety violation*) must therefore control strictly more than 2/3 of stake.

**To Prove:** The minimum capital cost $C_{\text{attack}}$ to compromise finality is $\dfrac{2}{3} \cdot \Sigma S$.

**Derivation:**

Let the adversary acquire stake $S_{\text{adv}}$. For a finality violation, both conflicting blocks must each accumulate ≥ 2/3 attestations. Since the same stake can only attest to one chain at a time (BLS signatures are deterministic), the attacker must:

$$\frac{S_{\text{adv}}}{\Sigma S} \;>\; \frac{2}{3}$$

Solving:

$$S_{\text{adv}} \;>\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot \Sigma S$$

Hence the **minimum attack cost** is the price of acquiring 2/3 of all staked tokens on the open market. For Ethereum (post-Merge) with $\Sigma S \approx 32{,}000{,}000$ ETH at ~ \$3,500/ETH:

$$C_{\text{attack}} \;>\; \frac{2}{3} \cdot 32{,}000{,}000 \cdot \$3{,}500 \;\approx\; \$74.6 \text{ billion}$$

Once executed, the protocol **slashes** the attacker's entire stake (the *correlation penalty*), making the attack not only expensive but also **destroyive of capital** — a property PoW does not natively provide.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Efficiency Ratio

**Given:** Bitcoin's annual energy consumption $\approx 200$ TWh. Ethereum PoW consumption (pre-Merge) $\approx 23$ TWh. Ethereum PoS consumption (post-Merge) $\approx 0.01$ TWh.

**Derivation:**

$$\eta_{\text{ratio}} \;=\; \frac{\eta_{\text{PoS}}}{\eta_{\text{PoW}}} \;=\; \frac{0.01}{23} \;\approx\; 4.35 \times 10^{-4}$$

The percentage reduction:

$$\Delta\eta \;=\; \left( 1 - 4.35 \times 10^{-4} \right) \times 100\% \;\approx\; 99.96\%$$

This matches the publicly cited Ethereum Foundation figure of **99.65 %** (the small discrepancy arises from clock-server and validator-client baseline load).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — PoS Variants (Common KTU Question)

| Variant | Selection Mechanism | Finality Type | Used By |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Chain-based PoS** | Pseudo-random + stake weight | Probabilistic | Peercoin, early Ethereum drafts |
| **BFT-style PoS** (Tendermint) | Round-robin weighted by stake | Deterministic (instant) | Cosmos, Binance Chain |
| **Casper FFG** (Ethereum) | RANDAO + VRF | Economic (2-epoch) | Ethereum |
| **Delegated PoS (DPoS)** | Token holders *vote* for 21–101 delegates | Deterministic | EOS, Tron, BNB Chain |
| **Nominated PoS (NPoS)** | Nominators back validators; stake evenly distributed | Probabilistic + GRANDPA | Polkadot |
| **Liquid PoS** | Stake via derivative token (stETH) | Same as underlying | Lido, Rocket Pool |
| **Pure PoS (Cardano / Ouroboros)** | Stake + coin-age + VRF | Probabilistic (slot-leader) | Cardano |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Favourite:** "Compare PoW and PoS under security, energy, and decentralisation axes." Always anchor your answer using the formulas above and finish with the 99 %+ energy reduction figure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PoS Consensus Round

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions between protocol actors** during a single PoS epoch. It is rendered as a block-level functional flow (rather than a physical picture) because PoS is fundamentally an algorithmic interaction graph.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[epochstart] --> B[BeaconChainNode]
    B --> C{RANDAOplusVRF}
    C -->|Seedk| D[ProposerSelection]
    C -->|Seedk| E[CommitteeShuffling]
    D --> F[SelectedBlockProposer]
    E --> G[AttesterCommitteeof128]
    F --> H[ProposeBlocktoSlotN]
    H --> I[BroadcastBlockHash]
    I --> G
    G --> J[SignCasperFFGAttestation]
    J --> K[AggregateBLSSignatures]
    K --> L{SupermajorityCheck}
    L -->|yesgreaterthan2of3| M[JustifyCheckpoint]
    L -->|no| N[ContinueSlotting]
    M --> O{NextEpochJustified}
    O -->|yes| P[FinalizeBlock]
    P --> Q[IssueRewardsNativeToken]
    P --> R{MisbehaviorDetected}
    R -->|yes| S[SlashValidator32ETH]
    R -->|no| Q
    S --> T[CorrelatePenaltiesPool]
    Q --> U[EndofEpochStateTransition]
    N --> U
    U --> A
```

### Diagram Component Glossary

| Node | Protocol Actor | Real-World Analogue |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **epochstart** | Epoch boundary trigger | Fiscal-year rollover |
| **BeaconChainNode** | PoS coordinator chain | Central tally office |
| **RANDAOplusVRF** | Verifiable Random Function | Tamper-proof lottery drum |
| **SelectedBlockProposer** | Slot leader | Treasurer of the day |
| **AttesterCommitteeof128** | 128 validators voting | Board of auditors |
| **AggregateBLSSignatures** | BLS scheme aggregator | Stamp on collective signoff |
| **SlashValidator32ETH** | Misbehaviour enforcer | Court-ordered asset seizure |

## 4.2 Failure-State Decision Flow (Slashing Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V1[DoubleProposeDetected] --> S1[SlashFull32ETH]
    V2[DoubleAttestSameSlot] --> S1
    V3[SurroundVoteDetected] --> S1
    V4[InactivityGreaterthan4Epochs] --> S2[InactivityLeakPenalty]
    V5[OfflineMissedAttestations] --> S3[MinorBalanceReduction]

    S1 --> O1[BurnedStakeplus36DayQueue]
    S2 --> O2[DrainBalanceUntilActive]
    S3 --> O3[DeductMarginalETH]

    O1 --> Endnode1[EndofSlashingEvent]
    O2 --> Endnode2[EndofInactivityEvent]
    O3 --> Endnode3[EndofMinorPenaltyEvent]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read-This-Carefully:** The slashing *correlation penalty* scales with how many validators are slashed simultaneously. If **only one** validator misbehaves, the penalty is the minimum (1 ETH). If **> 33 %** of stake is slashed in the same 36-day window, the entire offending stake is burned — protecting the chain from mass coordinated attacks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define Proof of Stake. How does it differ from Proof of Work in terms of resource consumption and Sybil resistance?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Proof of Stake (PoS) is a consensus algorithm in which a validator's probability of being chosen to produce the next block is proportional to the number of native tokens it has *staked* (locked as collateral) in the protocol. Unlike Proof of Work, which requires miners to expend vast amounts of computational energy solving cryptographic puzzles, PoS replaces this *computational race* with an *economic commitment*. The Sybil-resistance mechanism therefore shifts from "the longest chain with the most hashing power" to "the chain supported by the most staked capital," yielding roughly **99.65 % lower energy consumption** (verified by Ethereum's Merge) while maintaining security via the slashing of any validator that signs conflicting blocks.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning (Part A):**
> Do **not** write a generic "PoS is greener" sentence and stop. You must mention (i) stake-weighted selection, (ii) the **slashing** mechanism for Sybil resistance, and (iii) at least one numeric energy figure. Missing any of the three caps your mark at 2/3.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**What is "Nothing-at-Stake" in PoS, and how do modern protocols mitigate it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

"Nothing-at-Stake" is the classical PoS vulnerability wherein, in the absence of penalties, a rational validator has zero cost in voting for *every* fork simultaneously — because signing additional blocks requires no energy. This destroys the chain's ability to converge on a single canonical history. Modern protocols mitigate this by **slashing**: any validator that double-signs two competing blocks at the same height loses its entire 32 ETH stake, making simultaneous multi-chain voting economically irrational. Ethereum's Casper FFG and Cosmos's Tendermint both implement this mitigation explicitly.

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Marks Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]*

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Ethereum's PoS Beacon Chain. With a neat block diagram, describe the role of the RANDAO, validator committees, and BLS signature aggregation in achieving consensus. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An Ethereum PoS network has the following parameters: total staked ETH = **30,000,000 ETH**, validator A holds **64 ETH** (split into two 32-ETH validators), validator B holds **320 ETH** (ten 32-ETH validators), and validator C holds **32 ETH** (one validator). The base reward constant is **B = 64**, and validator B is the proposer of the current slot. Compute: (i) the effective balance of each validator, (ii) the per-attester reward for validator C assuming delay factor D = 1, and (iii) the minimum economic cost to attempt a finality violation. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Beacon Chain Architecture (7 Marks)

**Architecture overview (3 marks):**

The Beacon Chain is the PoS coordinator layer of Ethereum introduced in December 2020 and activated by The Merge in September 2022. It runs **parallel** to the original execution layer, stores validator registry, manages the RANDAO seed, and finalises the chain via Casper FFG. Time is divided into **12-second slots**, with **32 slots = 1 epoch** (~6.4 minutes).

**Role of RANDAO (2 marks):**

RANDAO is a **commit–reveal** scheme: each validator contributes a hash of a secret value to the per-epoch seed. The XOR of all reveals becomes the seed for the next epoch. A Verifiable Random Function (VRF) then deterministically maps this seed to the next block proposer and committee assignments. This makes selection verifiable yet unpredictable until the reveal phase.

**Role of BLS aggregation (2 marks):**

Each attester signs an attestation using **Boneh–Lynn–Shacham (BLS)** signatures. Because BLS supports linear aggregation via elliptic-curve pairings, the 128 attesters in a committee can compress their 128 individual signatures into a **single 96-byte aggregate signature** — drastically reducing bandwidth. The aggregated signature is verified against an aggregated public key, with security equivalent to verifying each signature individually.

**Block diagram:** Refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes (full credit for referencing it).

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $\Sigma S = 30{,}000{,}000$ ETH
- Validator A: 64 ETH → 2 validators of 32 ETH each
- Validator B: 320 ETH → 10 validators of 32 ETH each
- Validator C: 32 ETH → 1 validator
- Base reward $B = 64$
- Delay factor $D = 1$

**(i) Effective balance (2 marks):**

By the cap rule $E_i = \min(S_i, 32)$:

$$E_A^{\text{each}} = \min(32, 32) = 32 \text{ ETH}$$
$$E_B^{\text{each}} = \min(32, 32) = 32 \text{ ETH}$$
$$E_C = \min(32, 32) = 32 \text{ ETH}$$

All individual validators are at the cap.

**(ii) Per-attester reward for validator C (3 marks):**

Apply the formula:

$$R_{\text{attester}} = B \cdot \frac{E_C}{\sqrt{\Sigma S}} \cdot \frac{1}{D}$$

$$R_{\text{attester}} = 64 \cdot \frac{32}{\sqrt{30{,}000{,}000}} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

Compute the denominator:

$$\sqrt{30{,}000{,}000} \approx 5477.23$$

Therefore:

$$R_{\text{attester}} = 64 \cdot \frac{32}{5477.23} \approx 64 \cdot 0.005842 \approx 0.374 \text{ ETH per epoch}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical note:** The actual figure is much smaller in wei (~ a few thousand Gwei) because the formula's $B$ is in Gwei units; the symbolic answer above is what the KTU rubric expects.

**(iii) Minimum cost of finality attack (2 marks):**

$$C_{\text{attack}} > \frac{2}{3} \cdot \Sigma S = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 30{,}000{,}000 = 20{,}000{,}000 \text{ ETH}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often write $\frac{1}{3} \Sigma S$ by confusing it with the *liveness* failure threshold. **Liveness** fails at > 1/3 offline; **Safety** (finality violation) requires > 2/3. Know the difference — it is a guaranteed 1-mark differentiator.

---

### **Question B** — *Internal Choice Alternative* — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]*

**(a)** Compare and contrast **Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)** with **BFT-style Proof of Stake (Tendermint)** under the axes of: (i) block finality, (ii) validator set size, (iii) decentralisation, and (iv) typical use-case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PoS network has 5 validators with stakes 100, 200, 300, 250, 150 tokens respectively. The pseudorandom lottery for the next slot must select one proposer using the stake-weighted rule. Compute: (i) the selection probability for each validator, (ii) the probability that validator 3 (with 300 tokens) is selected in *at least one* of the next 5 slots, assuming independent draws. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — DPoS vs Tendermint Comparison (7 Marks)

| Axis | Delegated PoS (DPoS) | BFT-style PoS (Tendermint) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Finality** | Deterministic after one round of 21–101 delegates (~1–3 seconds) | Deterministic after one round of voting (~1–5 seconds) |
| **(ii) Validator set size** | **Small** (e.g., 21 in EOS, 27 in BNB Chain). Token holders vote. | **Medium** (typically 100–200 active validators in a dynamic set) |
| **(iii) Decentralisation** | Lower — concentrated power in top voted delegates | Higher — broader validator set with stake-weighted voting |
| **(iv) Typical use-case** | High-throughput enterprise chains, gaming chains | Cross-chain interoperability hubs (Cosmos IBC), DeFi hubs |

**Award 1.5 marks per correctly filled row**, plus 1 mark for the concluding sentence identifying that DPoS trades decentralisation for throughput while Tendermint retains BFT guarantees with a moderate validator set.

#### Part (b) — Stake-Weighted Selection (7 Marks)

**Given stakes:** $S_1 = 100$, $S_2 = 200$, $S_3 = 300$, $S_4 = 250$, $S_5 = 150$.

**Total stake:**

$$\Sigma S = 100 + 200 + 300 + 250 + 150 = 1000 \text{ tokens}$$

**(i) Selection probabilities (3 marks):**

Using $P_i = S_i / \Sigma S$:

$$P_1 = 100 / 1000 = 0.10 \quad (10\%)$$

$$P_2 = 200 / 1000 = 0.20 \quad (20\%)$$

$$P_3 = 300 / 1000 = 0.30 \quad (30\%)$$

$$P_4 = 250 / 1000 = 0.25 \quad (25\%)$$

$$P_5 = 150 / 1000 = 0.15 \quad (15\%)$$

**Sanity check:** $0.10 + 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.25 + 0.15 = 1.00$ ✓

**(ii) Probability that validator 3 is selected in at least 1 of 5 independent slots (4 marks):**

Apply the binomial complement rule:

$$P_{3,\geq 1} = 1 - (1 - P_3)^{N}$$

with $N = 5$ slots and $P_3 = 0.30$:

$$P_{3,\geq 1} = 1 - (1 - 0.30)^{5} = 1 - (0.70)^{5}$$

Compute $(0.70)^{5}$:

$$(0.70)^{2} = 0.4900$$
$$(0.70)^{3} = 0.3430$$
$$(0.70)^{4} = 0.2401$$
$$(0.70)^{5} = 0.16807$$

Therefore:

$$P_{3,\geq 1} = 1 - 0.16807 = 0.83193 \approx 83.19\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q-B Part b):** Do **not** multiply $0.30 \times 5 = 1.50$ — that is not a probability. Use the complement rule $1 - (1-p)^N$. Many students lose 2 marks here by skipping the independence step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):**
