- [x] **PoS definition:** Stake-weighted selection + slashing-based Sybil resistance. **Energy reduction ≈ 99.65 %.**
- [x] **Probability formula:** $P_i = S_i / \Sigma S$ — committed to memory.
- [x] **Effective-balance cap:** 32 ETH (Ethereum) — prevents plutocratic domination.
- [x] **Casper FFG:** Finality requires ≥ **2/3** supermajority of stake; attack cost > $\frac{2}{3} \Sigma S$.
- [x] **Nothing-at-stake problem:** Solved by **slashing**, not by protocol design alone.
- [x] **BLS aggregation:** 128 attesters → 96-byte single aggregate signature.
- [x] **RANDAO + VRF:** Pseudo-random, verifiable validator selection per slot.
- [x] **Variants to name-drop:** Chain-PoS, BFT-PoS (Tendermint), DPoS, NPoS (Polkadot), Casper FFG, Ouroboros.
- [x] **Three key rewards:** Attester, Proposer, Sync-committee — all in native token.
- [x] **Three slashing triggers:** Double-proposal, double-attestation, surround-voting.
- [x] **Liveness vs Safety:** Liveness breaks at > 1/3 offline; Safety (finality) breaks at > 2/3 colluding.
- [x] **Time scale:** 12 s slot, 32 slots/epoch (~6.4 min), 2 epochs to finality (~12.8 min).
- [x] **Industry anchor:** Ethereum Merge (Sep 2022) — the canonical real-world PoS deployment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of PoS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Types of PoS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Proof of Stake (PoS)** is a family of consensus algorithms where block validators are selected based on the number of native cryptocurrency tokens they "stake" (lock up) as collateral, rather than computational power. **Types of PoS** refers to the various cryptographic-economic variants that have evolved from the original PoS concept to address scalability, decentralization, and security trade-offs. The taxonomy of PoS variants covered under the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 includes: | # | Variant | Full Form | Year Introduced | |---|---------|-----------|-----------------| | 1 | PoS | Proof of Stake | 2011 (Bitcointalk forum) | | 2 | DPoS | Delegated Proof of Stake | 2014 (Daniel Larimer, Bitshares) | | 3 | LPoS | Leased / Liquid Proof of Stake | 2016 (Waves), 2018 (Tezos) | | 4 | NPoS | Nominated Proof of Stake | 2019 (Polkadot, Web3 Foundation) | | 5 | BPoS | Bonded Proof of Stake | 2019 (Cosmos / Tendermint) | | 6 | Casper FFG | Friendly Finality Gadget | 2020 (Ethereum 2.0 Beacon Chain) | | 7 | PoA | Proof of Authority | 2017 (Parity, VeChain, Kovan) | | 8 | PoH | Proof of History | 2019 (Solana) | | 9 | HPoS | Hybrid Proof of Stake | 2018 (Decred) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module 2 Focus Areas:** The university syllabus emphasizes **Pure PoS, DPoS, LPoS, NPoS, BPoS, and Casper FFG** as the core six variants. PoA and PoH are treated as advanced supplementary topics. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **village council election** instead of a **physical wrestling match** (which is how Proof of Work works): - In a wrestling match (PoW), the strongest fighter wins and gets to decide the village's next law. This wastes energy. - In a council election (PoS), anyone who **owns a share** of village land can become a candidate. The more land you pledge, the higher your chance of being elected as a leader. If you cheat, **your land is confiscated** (slashed). **Now, the "Types of PoS" differ in HOW candidates are elected:** - **Direct Democracy (Pure PoS):** Every land-owner votes themselves. - **Representative Democracy (DPoS):** Land-owners elect a small group of "mayors" to vote on their behalf. - **Cooperative Farming (NPoS):** Land-owners form a cooperative, and elected "nominators" vouch for farmers. - **Bonded Lease (BPoS):** Farmers must deposit a bond for 21 days before they can farm — if they misbehave, the bond is cut. - **Notarized Deed (Casper FFG):** A notary walks around checking everyone's deeds and "finalizes" them. > [!TIP] > **Key Insight for Exams:** Every variant of PoS tries to solve a different *trilemma* trade-off between **Security**, **Decentralization**, and **Throughput**. Always mention this trade-off in 14-mark answers to score the L3 (Apply/Analyze) cognitive level marks. --- ### 1.3 Fundamental Economic Primitives Across All PoS Types All PoS variants share three foundational primitives, regardless of type: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Pillars of Every PoS System:** > 1. **Stake (Collateral):** The amount of capital a validator locks. > 2. **Slashing Condition:** The punishment rule for misbehavior. > 3. **Election/Finality Rule:** The mechanism that selects the next block producer. **Standard Slashing Parameters (Industry Norms):** - **Double-signing slash:** **100%** of stake (industry standard across Cosmos, Polkadot, Ethereum 2.0). - **Surround-voting slash (Casper FFG):** **50%** of stake. - **Inactivity leak (Ethereum 2.0):** Linear penalty proportional to offline duration. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Eight Core PoS Variants — Structured Analysis #### 2.1.1 Pure Proof of Stake (PPoS) — Peercoin Model (2012) - **Who validates?** Token holders themselves, weighted by their stake. - **Selection mechanism:** Randomized using a hash of `stake + previous_block_hash` (prevents grinding attacks). - **No slashing in early versions** — uses coin-age (stake × time held) to deter attacks. - **Block reward:** Distributes newly minted coins proportional to stake. > [!NOTE] > **Pioneer Network:** Peercoin (2012) and Nxt (2013) deployed the first pure PoS implementations. #### 2.1.2 Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS) — BitShares / EOSIO Model (2014) - **Who validates?** Token holders **elect** a fixed number (typically 21–101) of "delegates" or "witnesses" or "block producers." - **Selection mechanism:** Continuous approval voting — top-k delegates with most votes produce blocks in round-robin. - **Slashing:** Indirect — delegates lose votes (and thus income) if they misbehave. - **Throughput:** **3,000+ TPS** on EOS, **0.5 sec** block times. **Operational Logic Steps:** 1. Token holders cast votes proportional to their stake. 2. Top-N delegates form an active set. 3. Delegates produce blocks in scheduled time slots. 4. If a delegate misses a slot, the next scheduled delegate produces. 5. Delegates are paid block rewards + fees; share with voters optionally. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Centralization Trade-off:** DPoS sacrifices decentralization for throughput. Critics (e.g., Ethereum founders) call it a "cartel model." #### 2.1.3 Leased Proof of Stake (LPoS) — Waves Platform (2016) - **Who validates?** Full nodes (miners), but **light holders** can lease their stake. - **Leasing mechanism:** Tokens remain in the lessor's wallet but contribute to the miner's effective stake. Leases can be cancelled at any time. - **Benefit:** Increases decentralization by allowing small holders to participate without running infrastructure. > [!NOTE] > **Waves LPoS Detail:** Leased tokens **never leave** the wallet — only a "lease transaction" is signed, making it non-custodial and safe. #### 2.1.4 Liquid Proof of Stake (LPoS) — Tezos (2018) - **Who validates?** "Bakers" (validators) — any address holding **≥ 6,000 XTZ** (one "roll"). - **Delegation:** Token holders **delegate** to bakers without locking funds; delegation can be revoked instantly → hence "liquid." - **Slashing:** Implemented (post-Granada upgrade) for double-baking and double-endorsing. #### 2.1.5 Nominated Proof of Stake (NPoS) — Polkadot / Substrate (2019) - **Who validates?** Validators (limited to 297 active in Polkadot) **nominated** by nominators. - **Selection algorithm:** **Phragmén's method** — a proportional representation election algorithm. - **Reward distribution:** Rewards split based on stake-backing ratio; slashing is **shared** between validator and all its nominators. > [!IMPORTANT] > **NPoS Security Feature:** Nominators have skin-in-the-game. If they nominate a bad validator, **they get slashed too** — this forces due diligence. #### 2.1.6 Bonded Proof of Stake (BPoS) — Cosmos / Tendermint (2019) - **Who validates?** Top-100 validators by bonded stake. - **Unbonding period:** **21 days** during which stake cannot be withdrawn. - **Consensus:** Tendermint BFT (PBFT-style) with **instant finality** (no forks). - **Slashing:** 5% for double-signing, 0.01% for downtime. #### 2.1.7 Casper Friendly Finality Gadget (Casper FFG) — Ethereum 2.0 (2020) - **Architecture:** A **finality overlay** on top of a chain of LMD-GHOST forks. - **Two-epoch finality:** Blocks become "justified" after one epoch and "finalized" after two. - **Validator set:** 32 ETH minimum stake; thousands of validators. - **Slashing conditions:** - **Equivocation** (double-vote): 100% of 32 ETH. - **Surround voting:** 50% of 32 ETH. > [!NOTE] > **Casper FFG = Hybrid:** It overlays BFT finality onto a longest-chain fork-choice rule. It is technically a **Hybrid PoS** (PoW originally, now pure PoS post-Merge). #### 2.1.8 Proof of Authority (PoA) — Off-chain Trust Variant - **Who validates?** Pre-approved authorities (KYC'd entities). - **Use case:** Private/consortium chains (VeChain, BNB Chain initially, Kovan testnet). - **Slashing:** Social/contractual — identity at stake, not just capital. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Symbol | Expression | Meaning | Used In | |------------------|------------|---------|---------| | **Selection Probability** | $P_i = \dfrac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} s_j}$ | Probability of validator $i$ being selected; $s_i$ = stake of $i$ | All PoS variants | | **Coin Age (Peercoin)** | $A = s_i \times t$ | Stake weighted by holding time | Pure PoS (coin-age) | | **Threshold Signature Aggregation** | $\sigma_{agg} = \sum_{v \in V} w_v \cdot \sigma_v$ | BLS signature aggregation in Casper FFG | Casper FFG | | **Slashing Magnitude** | $S = \alpha \cdot s_{stake}$ | $\alpha \in [0,1]$ penalty factor | BPoS, Casper, NPoS | | **NPoS Phragmén Score** | $\Phi = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{n_i}$ | Minimize nominator count weighted allocation | Polkadot NPoS | | **DPoS Vote Weight** | $V_d = \sum_{i=1}^{k} v_i \cdot s_i$ | Total votes received by delegate $d$ | DPoS | | **Bonded Stake (Cosmos)** | $B = s_{active} - s_{unbonding}$ | Stake currently locked | BPoS | | **Inflation Reward** | $R = \dfrac{I_{total}}{N_{validators}}$ | Annual issuance / active validator count | Most PoS chains | | **Finality Time (Casper)** | $T_f = 2 \times t_{epoch}$ | Two-epoch finality = ~12.8 min on ETH2 | Casper FFG | | **LPoS Effective Stake** | $E = s_{self} + s_{leased}$ | Validator's effective voting weight | Waves LPoS | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on Notation:** In the table above, $s_i$ denotes the stake of validator $i$, $t$ denotes time in seconds, $n$ denotes the total number of validators, and $N_{validators}$ is the active validator set size. All formulas use standard mathematical notation with `\vert` (vertical bar) avoided inside markdown tables to preserve rendering integrity. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!TIP] > **Why does KTU include this topic in Module 2?** Every modern blockchain you will encounter in placement interviews — Ethereum, Solana, Polygon, Cardano, Polkadot, Cosmos, Avalanche — uses a variant of PoS. Understanding the differences lets you reason about: > - Which chain is suitable for **DeFi** (need BFT finality → BPoS / Casper). > - Which chain is suitable for **NFTs at scale** (need throughput → DPoS / PoH). > - Which chain is suitable for **enterprise** (need KYC + low node count → PoA). > - Which chain supports **small-holder participation** (need delegation → LPoS / NPoS). **Industry Mapping Table (Frequently Asked in KTU):** | Blockchain | Consensus Type | Year | TPS (Approx.) | |------------|---------------|------|---------------| | Ethereum | Casper FFG (post-Merge) | 2022 | ~30 (Layer 1) | | Polkadot | NPoS | 2020 | ~1,000 | | Cosmos Hub | BPoS (Tendermint) | 2019 | ~1,000 | | Tezos | LPoS | 2018 | ~40 | | EOS | DPoS (21 BPs) | 2018 | ~4,000 | | Waves | LPoS | 2016 | ~100 | | Peercoin | Pure PoS (coin-age) | 2012 | ~10 | | Cardano | Ouroboros (pure PoS) | 2017 | ~250 | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stake-Weighted Validator Selection Probability **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** Given a PoS network with 4 validators whose stakes are $s_1 = 100$, $s_2 = 300$, $s_3 = 50$, $s_4 = 550$ tokens, find the probability that validator 4 is selected as the next block producer in a single slot. **Step 1 — Write the general selection probability formula:** $$P_i = \frac{s_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{n} s_j}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the total stake (denominator):** $$\sum_{j=1}^{4} s_j = s_1 + s_2 + s_3 + s_4$$ $$\sum_{j=1}^{4} s_j = 100 + 300 + 50 + 550 = 1000 \text{ tokens}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the probability for validator 4:** $$P_4 = \frac{s_4}{\sum_{j=1}^{4} s_j} = \frac{550}{1000}$$ $$P_4 = 0.55 = 55\%$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation:** Validator 4, holding 55% of the network stake, has a 55% probability of being selected in any given slot. This is why **stake centralization** is a critical concern in PoS — a single validator with >50% stake can theoretically rewrite history. > [!NOTE] > **Nothing-at-Stake Problem:** In PoS, signing multiple competing forks has zero physical cost. This is why slashing exists — to make multi-fork voting economically painful. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Slashing Penalty Computation (BPoS / Cosmos Model) **Problem Statement:** A Cosmos Hub validator has a bonded stake of $B = 1{,}000{,}000$ ATOM and accidentally double-signs a block. Compute the slashing amount if the double-signing penalty factor is $\alpha_{ds} = 0.05$ and the downtime penalty factor is $\alpha_{dt} = 0.0001$. Assume the validator was offline for 10 hours in a year (8,760 hours). **Step 1 — Define the slashing function:** $$S = \alpha \cdot B$$ **Step 2 — Double-signing penalty:** $$S_{ds} = \alpha_{ds} \cdot B = 0.05 \times 1{,}000{,}000$$ $$S_{ds} = 50{,}000 \text{ ATOM slashed}$$ **Step 3 — Downtime penalty (inactivity leak):** $$S_{dt} = \alpha_{dt} \cdot B \cdot \frac{t_{offline}}{t_{year}}$$ $$S_{dt} = 0.0001 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{10}{8760}$$ $$S_{dt} = 100{,}000 \times 0.001142 = 114.16 \text{ ATOM}$$ **Step 4 — Total penalty:** $$S_{total} = S_{ds} + S_{dt} = 50{,}000 + 114.16$$ $$S_{total} = 50{,}114.16 \text{ ATOM}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering Insight:** The double-signing slash is **~438× larger** than the yearly downtime slash. This asymmetry is intentional: liveness failures are forgiven, safety failures are punished severely. This is the **CAP-theorem-aligned** design philosophy of Tendermint. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: NPoS Phragmén Allocation (Simplified 2-Validator Case) **Problem Statement:** Polkadot has 2 validator slots and 4 nominators with stakes $n_1 = 100$, $n_2 = 200$, $n_3 = 300$, $n_4 = 400$ DOT. All four nominators back both validators equally. Show that Phragmén's method balances the backing per validator to minimize the maximum number of nominators per validator. **Step 1 — Total stake:** $$T = \sum_{i=1}^{4} n_i = 100 + 200 + 300 + 400 = 1000 \text{ DOT}$$ **Step 2 — Phragmén's proportionality condition (equal stake per validator):** $$B_{target} = \frac{T}{k} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 \text{ DOT per validator}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for backing per nominator (sequential load distribution):** Assign highest-stake nominators first, equalize load per validator. After iterative Phragmén assignment (greedy load-balancing): $$B_{v1} = 500 \text{ DOT}, \quad B_{v2} = 500 \text{ DOT}$$ **Step 4 — Verify with the score function:** $$\Phi = \sum_{i=1}^{4} \frac{1}{n_i^{load}}$$ where $n_i^{load}$ is the number of nominators backing validator $i$. The algorithm seeks to minimize this sum while keeping $B_{v1} = B_{v2} = 500$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why NPoS matters:** Polkadot's Phragmén-based election makes bribery attacks expensive — to control $k$ validators, an attacker must win over a *majority* of *all* nominators, not just buy $k$ slots. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Properties of PoS Variants | Property | Pure PoS | DPoS | LPoS (Tezos) | NPoS | BPoS | Casper FFG | |----------|----------|------|--------------|------|------|------------| | **Min Stake** | 0 (any) | 0 (voter) | 6,000 XTZ (1 roll) | 0 (nominator) | 0 (delegator) | 32 ETH | | **Active Set Size** | All | 21–101 | ~400 bakers | 297 | 175 (Cosmos) | ~1M validators | | **Finality** | Probabilistic | Probabilistic | Probabilistic (6 confirmations) | GRANDPA (deterministic) | Instant (Tendermint) | Two-epoch finality | | **Slashing** | No (early) | Indirect (loss of votes) | Yes (post-Granada) | Shared slash | Yes (5% DS) | Yes (50–100%) | | **Delegation** | No | Yes (vote-based) | Yes (liquid) | Yes (nominate) | Yes (delegate) | No (direct staking) | | **Fork Choice** | Longest chain | Longest chain | Longest chain | GRANDPA + BABE | BFT (no forks) | LMD-GHOST + FFG | | **Energy Use vs PoW** | ~99.95% less | ~99.95% less | ~99.95% less | ~99.95% less | ~99.95% less | ~99.95% less | | **Key Weakness** | Nothing-at-stake | Cartel formation | No shared slashing | Complex UX | 21-day unbonding | High min stake | | **Key Strength** | Full decentralization | High TPS | Capital efficiency | Shared security | Instant finality | EVM compatibility | --- ### 3.5 Derivation 4: Economic Security of PoS (Cost-of-Corruption vs Profit-from-Corruption) **Problem Statement:** A PoS network has total staked value $V_{stake} = \$10 \text{ billion}$ and an annual block reward + fees of $R = \$200 \text{ million}$. Compute the *minimum* cost an attacker must bear to mount a 51% attack and determine if the network is economically secure. **Step 1 — Cost to acquire 51% stake:** $$C_{51} = 0.51 \times V_{stake} = 0.51 \times 10{,}000{,}000{,}000$$ $$C_{51} = \$5{,}100{,}000{,}000$$ **Step 2 — Annual reward from honest operation (cost-of-not-attacking):** $$R_{honest} = \$200{,}000{,}000 \text{ per year}$$ **Step 3 — Slashing exposure on attack:** If the attack is detected, the validator is slashed: $$S_{slashed} = 1.00 \times C_{51} = \$5{,}100{,}000{,}000 \text{ (worst case)}$$ **Step 4 — Economic security condition:** A PoS network is economically secure if: $$C_{51} + S_{slashed} \gg R_{attack}$$ In our case, attacking costs **\$5.1B** with an additional **\$5.1B** slash risk, while the one-time attack profit might be ~$1B from double-spends. Since $C_{51} \gg R_{attack}$, the network is **economically secure**. > [!NOTE] > **This is why PoS is called "cryptographically enforced capitalism"** — unlike PoW where you only lose electricity, in PoS you can lose your **entire principal** if you cheat. --- ### 3.6 Comparative Throughput Analysis $$TPS_{max} = \frac{\text{Block Size in bytes}}{\text{Transaction Size in bytes} \times \text{Block Time in seconds}}$$ For a DPoS chain with $3\text{ MB}$ blocks, $250\text{ byte}$ average tx, and $0.5\text{ s}$ block time: $$TPS_{max} = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{250 \times 0.5} = \frac{3{,}000{,}000}{125} = 24{,}000 \text{ TPS (theoretical max)}$$ For Casper FFG on Ethereum 2.0 with $1\text{ MB}$ blocks, $250\text{ byte}$ tx, $12\text{ s}$ slot: $$TPS_{max} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{250 \times 12} = \frac{1{,}000{,}000}{3000} \approx 333 \text{ TPS (theoretical max)}$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** Always include a numerical comparison of TPS, finality time, and min stake when comparing PoS variants. KTU examiners award extra marks for quantified analysis under the **L4 (Analyze)** cognitive level. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Master Taxonomy Diagram: PoS Family Tree ```mermaid graph TD rootA["Proof of Stake Family"] --> rootB["Pure PoS
Peercoin, Nxt"] rootA --> rootC["Delegated PoS
DPoS"] rootA --> rootD["Liquid PoS
LPoS Tezos"] rootA --> rootE["Leased PoS
LPoS Waves"] rootA --> rootF["Nominated PoS
NPoS Polkadot"] rootA --> rootG["Bonded PoS
BPoS Cosmos"] rootA --> rootH["Casper FFG
Ethereum 2.0"] rootA --> rootI["Proof of Authority
PoA"] rootA --> rootJ["Hybrid PoS
Decred"] rootB --> rootB1["Coin-age weighted
2012"] rootC --> rootC1["21-101 delegates
Witnesses"] rootC --> rootC2["EOS, Tron, BNB"] rootD --> rootD1["Liquid delegation
6,000 XTZ roll"] rootE --> rootE1["Lease tokens
Non-custodial"] rootF --> rootF1["Phragmen election
297 validators"] rootG --> rootG1["21-day unbonding
Tendermint BFT"] rootH --> rootH1["Two-epoch finality
LMD-GHOST"] rootI --> rootI1["KYC authorities
VeChain, Kovan"] rootJ --> rootJ1["PoW + PoS blend
Decred"] style rootA fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootB fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootC fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootD fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootE fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootF fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootG fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootH fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootI fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff style rootJ fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff ``` --- ### 4.2 DPoS Block Production Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Token Holders
Cast Votes"] --> stepB["Tally Weighted Votes
By Stake"] stepB --> stepC["Top 21 Delegates
Elected as Block Producers"] stepC --> stepD["Round-Robin
Block Production"] stepD --> stepE["Producer Signs Block
in Assigned Time Slot"] stepE --> stepF{"Block Valid?"} stepF -- Yes --> stepG["Block Added to Chain
Producer Earns Rewards"] stepF -- No --> stepH["Skip Producer
Move to Next"] stepH --> stepD stepG --> stepI["Continuous Re-voting
Voters Re-elect Periodically"] stepI --> stepA style stepA fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000 style stepB fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000000 style stepC fill:#fdba74,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000000 style stepD fill:#fb923c,stroke:#9a3412,color:#000000 style stepE fill:#f97316,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style stepF fill:#ea580c,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style stepG fill:#c2410c,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style stepH fill:#9a3412,stroke:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff style stepI fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#431407,color:#ffffff ``` --- ### 4.3 NPoS Architecture: Validators and Nominators ```mermaid graph LR n1["Nominator 1
Stake 100 DOT"] --> v1["Validator A
Self-stake 10 DOT"] n2["Nominator 2
Stake 200 DOT"] --> v1 n3["Nominator 3
Stake 300 DOT"] --> v2["Validator B
Self-stake 15 DOT"] n4["Nominator 4
Stake 400 DOT"] --> v2 n4 --> v3["Validator C
Self-stake 20 DOT"] v1 --> p1["Phragmen Election
Top 297 by Backing"] v2 --> p1 v3 --> p1 p1 --> r1["Active Set
Produces Relay Chain Blocks"] p1 --> r2["Shared Slashing
Validator + Nominators"] p1 --> r3["Reward Distribution
Proportional to Stake"] style n1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style n2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style n3 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style n4 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style v1 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style v2 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style v3 fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style p1 fill:#93c5fd,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style r1 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff style r2 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff style r3 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff ``` --- ### 4.4 Casper FFG Two-Epoch Finality Mechanism ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Justified Justified --> Justified : First Epoch Votes
2/3 Supermajority Justified --> Finalized : Second Epoch
Consecutive Justification Finalized --> [*] : Block Cannot Be Reverted Justified --> Slashed : Surround or Double Voting Slashed --> [*] : Validator Penalized 50-100% note right of Justified Epoch N Validators vote on checkpoint end note note right of Finalized Epoch N+1 Justified checkpoint becomes final end note ``` --- ### 4.5 BPoS Bonding and Unbonding Lifecycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User Wallet participant V as Cosmos Validator participant BC as Bonded Stake Pool participant UB as Unbonding Pool U->>V: Delegate ATOM (tx) V->>BC: Add to Validator Bond BC-->>U: Voting Power Increased Note over V,BC: Validator Signs Blocks V->>BC: Earn Block Rewards BC-->>U: Distribute Rewards U->>V: Unbond Request V->>UB: Move to Unbonding (21 days) Note over UB: During 21 days,
tokens are slashable UB-->>U: Liquid ATOM Released ``` --- ### 4.6 Comparative Architecture of PoS Variants ```mermaid graph TB subgraph Pure["Pure PoS"] p1["All token holders validate"] --> p2["Coin-age weighted"] end subgraph DPoS["Delegated PoS"] d1["Voters elect delegates"] --> d2["Top 21 produce blocks"] end subgraph LPoS["Liquid PoS"] l1["Delegators keep custody"] --> l2["Bakers use delegated stake"] end subgraph NPoS["Nominated PoS"] n1["Nominators back validators"] --> n2["Phragmen selects active set"] end subgraph BPoS["Bonded PoS"] b1["Top 100 by bonded stake"] --> b2["Tendermint BFT consensus"] end subgraph Casper["Casper FFG"] c1["LMD-GHOST fork choice"] --> c2["Two-epoch finality"] end style Pure fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#831843,color:#000000 style DPoS fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,color:#000000 style LPoS fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000 style NPoS fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000000 style BPoS fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,color:#000000 style Casper fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]** > > **Q1. Differentiate between Proof of Work and Proof of Stake. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Proof of Work (PoW) | Proof of Stake (PoS) | |-----------|--------------------|-----------------------| | Resource | Computational power (electricity) | Capital (staked tokens) | | Attack Cost | Hardware + electricity | Acquire + risk stake | | Energy | High (~150 TWh/year for Bitcoin) | Low (~99.95% less) | | Finality | Probabilistic | Probabilistic or deterministic (BFT) | | Example | Bitcoin, Litecoin (pre-Merge ETH) | Ethereum 2.0, Cardano, Polkadot | In PoW, miners compete to solve a cryptographic puzzle; in PoS, validators are selected proportional to their staked tokens, making attacks economically irrational since the attacker would be slashing their own collateral. **(3 Marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]** > > **Q2. What is Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS)? Mention any two limitations. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Delegated Proof of Stake is a consensus mechanism where token holders vote to elect a fixed number of block producers (typically 21–101), who then take turns producing blocks in a round-robin manner. Used by EOS, Tron, BitShares. **Limitations (1.5 Marks — write any two):** 1. **Cartel formation / centralization** — Top delegates may collude to retain power. 2. **Low voter turnout** — Many users do not vote, weakening decentralization claims. 3. **Plutocracy** — Wealthy token holders dominate voting, making the system a "rich get richer" model. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) --- > **[KTU University Exam - Module 2 Model Paper]** > > ### Question A (14 Marks) > > **(a)** Explain the architecture and working of Delegated Proof of Stake (DPoS). Compare it with Pure Proof of Stake in terms of throughput, decentralization, and slashing mechanism. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Describe the Nominated Proof of Stake (NPoS) consensus used in Polkadot. How does Phragmén's election method improve security against bribery attacks? **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — DPoS Architecture (7 Marks):** **Architecture (3 Marks):** DPoS operates as a representative democracy within a blockchain. The system has three entities: 1. **Token Holders (Voters):** Stake tokens to cast votes. 2. **Witnesses / Block Producers:** Elected delegates who produce blocks. 3. **Standby Nodes:** Reserve delegates ready to step in. **Working (2 Marks):** - Voters cast votes weighted by their stake: $V_d = \sum v_i \cdot s_i$ - Top-N candidates (e.g., 21 in EOS) form the active set. - Block producers create blocks in scheduled time slots (0.5 sec each). - If a producer misses a slot, the next scheduled one takes over. - Voters can revoke votes anytime, creating a continuous meritocracy. **Comparison with Pure PoS (2 Marks):** | Feature | Pure PoS | DPoS | |---------|----------|------| | Throughput | 10–50 TPS | 3,000–4,000 TPS | | Decentralization | All stake-holders validate | Only 21–101 delegates | | Slashing | None (early) / Coin-age burn | Loss of votes (no on-chain slash) | | Finality | Probabilistic | Probabilistic (after 2/3+ confirmations) | **Part (b) — NPoS and Phragmén (7 Marks):** **NPoS Architecture (3 Marks):** - **Nominators** stake DOT and back **Validators** they trust. - Validator candidates register with a fixed self-stake. - Phragmén's method selects 297 active validators based on total backing. - All nominators of a slashed validator share the penalty. **Phragmén's Method (2 Marks):** Phragmén's is a *proportional representation* algorithm. For each validator seat $k$: $$\text{Goal: Maximize } \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{1}{n_i}$$ where $n_i$ is the number of nominators backing validator $i$. This minimizes the "load" (number of nominators per validator), spreading influence evenly. **Security Against Bribery (2 Marks):** In PoS bribery attacks, an attacker can buy votes cheaply because they only need a *majority of stake*, not of *participants*. Phragmén's design forces the attacker to: 1. **Identify and bribe a majority of nominators** (not just stake), OR 2. **Run a validator with 50% backing** — extremely capital-intensive. This makes bribery attacks significantly more expensive than in pure stake-weighted systems. --- > ### Question B (14 Marks) > > **(a)** Explain Casper Friendly Finality Gadget (FFG). Discuss the slashing conditions and two-epoch finality mechanism. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Describe Bonded Proof of Stake (BPoS) as used in the Cosmos network. Explain the roles of validators and delegators, and how the 21-day unbonding period enhances security. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Casper FFG (7 Marks):** **Definition (2 Marks):** Casper FFG is a PoS finality overlay designed for Ethereum 2.0. It provides *deterministic finality* on top of a *probabilistic* chain (LMD-GHOST fork-choice rule). Validators stake 32 ETH and vote on *checkpoints* (specific block hashes at epoch boundaries). **Two-Epoch Finality (3 Marks):** - **Epoch N:** Validators cast FFG votes (Justify and Finalize) on the current epoch checkpoint. - **Epoch N+1:** If a checkpoint receives 2/3 supermajority votes, it becomes *justified*. The parent justified checkpoint then becomes *finalized*. - Once finalized, the block **cannot be reverted** without slashing 1/3 of all staked ETH. Mathematically: $$T_{finality} = 2 \times t_{epoch} = 2 \times 6.4 \text{ min} = 12.8 \text{ min}$$ **Slashing Conditions (2 Marks):** 1. **Equivocation (double-voting):** 100% of stake slashed. 2. **Surround voting:** Voting on $C_1$ that *surrounds* $C_2$ in a way that violates the FFG slashing condition → 50% slashed. 3. **Inactivity leak:** Offline validators lose stake linearly over time. **Part (b) — BPoS in Cosmos (7 Marks):** **Architecture (3 Marks):** The Cosmos Hub runs Tendermint BFT, a BPoS variant with: - **Validators:** Top 175 by bonded ATOM stake. Each signs blocks in a BFT round. - **Delegators:** Token holders who delegate their ATOM to validators without running infrastructure. **Bonding Lifecycle (2 Marks):** - ATOM tokens are **bonded** (locked) to a validator. - Delegators share in rewards proportional to their share of the validator's total bond. - To withdraw, a delegator issues an **Unbond** transaction. - Tokens enter the **Unbonding Pool** for **21 days**. **Why 21-day Unbonding Matters (2 Marks):** - **Slashing protection window:** If the validator misbehaves, the unbonding tokens are still slashable for 21 days. This prevents the "exit-and-cheat" attack. - **Time for detection:** Social consensus has 21 days to coordinate a response to attacks. - **Economic deterrent:** A malicious validator must stake capital that is locked for 21 days, making short-term attacks expensive. The 21-day period is the *safety buffer* of Cosmos — without it, validators could slash + withdraw + profit instantly. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in PoS-Type Questions (KTU Board Pattern):** > > 1. **Confusing DPoS with NPoS:** Many students write that "DPoS uses Phragmén" — *it does not*. DPoS uses simple weighted voting. Phragmén is exclusive to NPoS. Loss: 2–3 marks. > > 2. **Forgetting that LPoS (Tezos) ≠ LPoS (Waves):** Tezos = *Liquid* Proof of Stake (delegation that can be revoked). Waves = *Leased* Proof of Stake (tokens leased without leaving wallet). Same acronym, different mechanism. Loss: 1–2 marks. > > 3. **Missing the "Nothing-at-Stake" problem in pure PoS:** Pure PoS has no slashing → attackers can sign all forks → no cost to attack. Examiners explicitly test this. Always mention it. Loss: 2 marks. > > 4. **Casper FFG ≠ Casper CBC:** FFG is the *finality gadget* deployed on Ethereum. CBC (Correct-by-Construction) is a research framework *never deployed*. Writing that "Ethereum uses Casper CBC" is an instant 2-mark deduction. > > 5. **Not mentioning numerical values:** Writing "BPoS has a 21-day unbonding period" scores higher than "BPoS has a long unbonding period." Examiners reward **specificity** under KTU 2024's L2/L3 rubric. > > 6. **Skipping the "Why" of slashing:** Saying "validators get slashed" without explaining *why* (to defeat nothing-at-stake and long-range attacks) is incomplete. Always tie slashing to a specific attack vector. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Types of PoS** - ☐ **Pure PoS** (Peercoin, 2012): Coin-age weighted; no slashing; vulnerable to nothing-at-stake. - ☐ **DPoS** (BitShares → EOS, 2014): Top 21 delegates via voting; high TPS; prone to cartel formation. - ☐ **LPoS — Tezos** (2018): 6,000 XTZ per "roll"; liquid delegation (revocable); post-Granada slashing. - ☐ **LPoS — Waves** (2016): Leased tokens stay in wallet; non-custodial; increases effective stake. - ☐ **NPoS** (Polkadot, 2019): 297 validators; Phragmén election; nominators share slashing. - ☐ **BPoS** (Cosmos, 2019): Top 175 by bonded stake; Tendermint BFT; 21-day unbonding. - ☐ **Casper FFG** (Ethereum 2.0, 2020): 32 ETH minimum; two-epoch finality; LMD-GHOST + FFG overlay. - ☐ **PoA** (Kovan, VeChain): Pre-approved authorities; identity at stake; consortium use cases. - ☐ **Nothing-at-Stake Problem:** Solved only by slashing — present in all mature PoS variants. - ☐ **Long-Range Attack:** Solved by weak subjectivity / checkpointing / unbonding delays. - ☐ **Selection Probability Formula:** $P_i = s_i / \sum s_j$ — universal across all PoS variants. - ☐ **Slashing Asymmetry:** Safety failures (double-sign) punished **100×** more than liveness failures (downtime). - ☐ **Casper FFG is Hybrid:** It overlays BFT finality on a fork-choice rule — not pure BFT. - ☐ **DPoS is NOT NPoS:** Phragmén is unique to Polkadot/NPoS; DPoS uses simple stake-weighted voting. - ☐ **Energy Comparison:** All PoS variants use **~99.95% less energy** than PoW (Ethereum Merge data). - ☐ **Finality Types:** Probabilistic (DPoS, LPoS), GRANDPA (NPoS), Tendermint BFT (BPoS), FFG (Casper). > **Final Exam Mnemonic — "DPoS LNP-7":** > **D**PoS = **D**elegates, > **L**PoS (Tezos) = **L**iquid, > **L**PoS (Waves) = **L**ease, > **N**PoS = **N**ominate, > **P**oS (Pure) = coin-age, > **7** others = BPoS, Casper, PoA, PoH, Decred-Hybrid, Ouroboros, Snowball.

topics.title: Bitcoin - Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Bitcoin - Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Official KTU Definition**
> **Bitcoin** is a decentralized, peer-to-peer (P2P) digital cash system and cryptocurrency, proposed by the pseudonymous entity **Satoshi Nakamoto** in the 2008 white paper titled *"Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System."* It enables direct online payments between parties without passing through any financial institution or trusted third party, secured through cryptographic proof-of-work consensus on a globally distributed, append-only public ledger known as the **blockchain**.

In formal terms, Bitcoin is simultaneously:
1. A **protocol** (the rules governing message exchange, transaction validation, and consensus).
2. A **distributed data structure** (the blockchain — an ordered, hash-linked list of blocks).
3. A **currency unit** (BTC — the digital asset transferred across the network).

The KTU 2024 syllabus, under Module 2, classifies Bitcoin as the **first practical implementation of blockchain technology** that solved the long-standing *double-spend problem* for purely digital money without a central authority.

### 1.2 Key Terminology Mapping

| Term | KTU-Accepted Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Cryptocurrency** | A digital asset secured by cryptography, not by physical commodity or central issuer. |
| **Decentralization** | Distribution of authority, ledger, and validation across all participating nodes. |
| **Peer-to-Peer (P2P)** | Direct node-to-node communication with no client-server hierarchy. |
| **Double-Spend Problem** | The risk that a digital coin can be copied and spent twice; Bitcoin prevents this. |
| **Proof-of-Work (PoW)** | The consensus algorithm requiring computational effort to propose new blocks. |
| **Satoshi (sat)** | The smallest unit of Bitcoin = **0.00000001 BTC** = **1 sat**. |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Public Notice Board"

> [!NOTE] **Think of Bitcoin Like This...**
> Imagine a village where every transaction is announced on a **public notice board** that every villager can read, but **no single villager owns or controls**. When person A pays person B, the entire village writes it down. The board is protected by a difficult puzzle — to add a new page (a *block*) of transactions, a villager must first solve a math puzzle. The puzzle is hard to solve but easy for everyone else to verify. Once a page is glued to the board, it can never be removed or changed without re-doing all the work on every later page.
>
> - The **notice board** = the **Bitcoin blockchain** (public, append-only ledger).
> - The **villagers** = **nodes** (full nodes, miners, light nodes).
> - The **puzzle** = **Proof-of-Work** (computational effort).
> - The **mathematical glue** = **cryptographic hash linking** (each block contains the previous block's hash).
> - The **money** = **Bitcoin (BTC)**, units of digital scarcity tracked by the ledger.

This analogy captures the three pillars of Bitcoin: *decentralization*, *transparency*, and *tamper-evidence*.

### 1.4 Why Bitcoin is More Than Just "Digital Money"

> [!TIP] **Critical Distinction for KTU Answers**
> Many students incorrectly define Bitcoin as "a digital currency." In KTU valuations, this is a **2-mark deduction** unless expanded. The full marks definition must highlight:
> 1. It is a **system**, not merely a coin.
> 2. It is a **consensus protocol** running on top of an **overlay P2P network**.
> 3. It uses a **distributed timestamp server** (per Nakamoto's white paper) to establish chronological order of transactions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Layers of Bitcoin Architecture

Bitcoin is best understood as a layered system. The KTU 2024 module explicitly groups its working under these three layers:

**Layer 1 — The Network Layer (P2P Overlay)**
- Bitcoin operates over a flat, unstructured P2P network on **TCP port 8333**.
- Each node maintains a pool of 8–125 outgoing connections.
- Node types: **Full Nodes** (store the entire ~500+ GB blockchain and validate all rules), **Mining Nodes** (specialized full nodes that produce blocks), **SPV/Light Nodes** (only store block headers, rely on full nodes for verification).
- Message types: `version`, `verack`, `inv`, `getdata`, `tx`, `block`, `getheaders`, `headers`, `ping`, `pong`.

**Layer 2 — The Data Layer (Blockchain + UTXO Model)**
- The ledger is structured as a chain of blocks, where each block header contains:
  - `version` (4 bytes) — block version number.
  - `prev_block_hash` (32 bytes) — SHA-256 of the previous block header.
  - `merkle_root` (32 bytes) — root of the Merkle tree of all transactions in the block.
  - `timestamp` (4 bytes) — Unix epoch seconds.
  - `n_bits` (4 bytes) — encoded target difficulty.
  - `nonce` (4 bytes) — the value miners vary to find a valid hash.
- Bitcoin uses the **UTXO (Unspent Transaction Output) Model**, not an account/balance model.

> [!NOTE] **UTXO vs. Account Model — KTU Hot Question**
> - **UTXO Model** (Bitcoin): The state is the set of all unspent outputs. Each transaction consumes prior UTXOs entirely and creates new ones (like breaking a ₹500 note and giving ₹300 + ₹200 change).
> - **Account Model** (Ethereum, Banks): Each user has a balance, and transactions debit/credit accounts.

**Layer 3 — The Consensus Layer (Proof-of-Work)**
- The mining puzzle: miners iterate the **nonce** field until
  $$\text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(\text{block header})) < \text{target}$$
- The **target** is automatically adjusted every **2016 blocks** (~2 weeks) to maintain an average block time of **10 minutes**.
- This adjustment is the **difficulty retargeting algorithm**.

### 2.2 The Bitcoin Transaction Lifecycle

A typical Bitcoin transaction goes through the following stages:

1. **Creation** — A wallet constructs a transaction specifying inputs (referencing previous UTXOs) and outputs (new UTXOs assigned to recipients' addresses).
2. **Signing** — The sender signs the transaction inputs using **ECDSA** (Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm) with their **private key**.
3. **Broadcasting** — The signed transaction is sent to neighboring nodes, which propagate it across the P2P network (flooding protocol).
4. **Mempool Acceptance** — Validating nodes place the transaction in their **memory pool (mempool)** after checking:
   - Inputs are valid unspent outputs.
   - Signatures are correct.
   - Total output value ≤ total input value (difference is the implicit fee).
5. **Mining (Block Inclusion)** — A miner selects transactions from the mempool (typically prioritizing by fee rate: satoshis per virtual byte), assembles a candidate block, and searches for a valid PoW solution.
6. **Confirmation** — Once the miner's block is propagated and accepted by other nodes, the transaction has **1 confirmation**. Each subsequent block adds another confirmation. The community standard for "confirmed" is **6 confirmations** (~60 minutes).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP] **Print This Table — High-Yield for Part A (3-mark) Questions**

| # | Concept | Formula / Value | Notes / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Block reward schedule** (initial) | $R_0 = 50 \text{ BTC}$ | Halved every 210,000 blocks |
| 2 | **Halving interval** | $H = 210{,}000$ blocks | ≈ 4 years at 10 min/block |
| 3 | **Block time target** | $T_{\text{target}} = 600$ seconds | 10 minutes per block |
| 4 | **Difficulty adjustment period** | $N_{\text{adj}} = 2016$ blocks | ≈ 2 weeks |
| 5 | **Difficulty update formula** | $\text{new target} = \text{old target} \times \dfrac{T_{\text{actual}}}{2016 \times 600}$ | Clamped to factor of 4 |
| 6 | **Hash function** | $H = \text{SHA-256}(\text{SHA-256}(x))$ | Double SHA-256 |
| 7 | **PoW success condition** | $H(\text{header}) < T$ | $T$ = encoded target |
| 8 | **Total BTC supply cap** | $S_{\max} = 21{,}000{,}000$ BTC | Reached ~year 2140 |
| 9 | **Smallest unit** | $1 \text{ sat} = 10^{-8} \text{ BTC}$ | "Satoshi" |
| 10 | **Confirmation threshold (safe)** | $C_{\text{safe}} = 6$ | Blocks deep |
| 11 | **Signature algorithm** | ECDSA on **secp256k1** | 256-bit private keys |
| 12 | **Address encoding** | Base58Check (legacy) / Bech32 (SegWit) | 160-bit hash of public key |
| 13 | **Block size (pre-SegWit)** | $\leq 1{,}000{,}000$ bytes (1 MB) | Hard cap |
| 14 | **Block weight (post-SegWit)** | $\leq 4{,}000{,}000$ weight units | Equivalent to ~4 MB |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Real-World Importance

Beyond the academic syllabus, Bitcoin's design has influenced:
- **Financial technology (FinTech):** Cross-border remittances without SWIFT intermediaries.
- **Supply chain:** Provenance and tamper-evident audit trails.
- **Decentralized identity (DID):** Self-sovereign identity anchored to blockchain keys.
- **Smart contracts:** Ethereum extended Bitcoin's UTXO-based scripting into a Turing-complete platform.
- **Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs):** Many central banks study Bitcoin's architecture while designing state-issued digital currencies.

For an engineering student, Bitcoin is also a case study in **distributed systems engineering** — illustrating CAP-theorem trade-offs (Bitcoin chooses **Consistency + Availability**, sacrificing strict Partition tolerance with eventual consistency under forks).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section derives two core quantitative aspects of Bitcoin's design: **(A) the total BTC supply** and **(B) the difficulty retargeting arithmetic**. Both are common in KTU 14-mark questions.

### 3.1 Derivation A: Total Bitcoin Supply Cap

Bitcoin's monetary policy is purely algorithmic. The block reward halves every $H = 210{,}000$ blocks, starting at $R_0 = 50$ BTC.

**Step 1 — Express the reward at halving epoch $k$.**

At epoch $k$ (where $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$), exactly $H$ blocks have been mined since the previous halving. The reward is halved each epoch:

$$R_k = \frac{R_0}{2^k} = \frac{50}{2^k} \text{ BTC per block}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the BTC issued across all $H$ blocks of a single epoch.**

The total BTC issued during epoch $k$ equals reward $\times$ number of blocks:

$$S_k = H \times R_k = 210{,}000 \times \frac{50}{2^k}$$

**Step 3 — Sum over all halving epochs to infinity.**

The total ever-issuable BTC is the geometric series:

$$S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} S_k = 210{,}000 \times 50 \times \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^k}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the geometric series.**

Recall the identity:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^k} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}} = 2$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back.**

$$S_{\text{total}} = 210{,}000 \times 50 \times 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC}$$

Thus, the **hard cap** of Bitcoin's supply is exactly **21 million BTC**, never to be exceeded. The last satoshi will be mined around the year **2140**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Note for Valuation:** Full marks in KTU 14-mark answers require showing **all 5 steps** explicitly. Skipping the geometric-series identity reduces marks by 3.

### 3.2 Derivation B: Bitcoin's Difficulty Retargeting

The network re-evaluates difficulty every $N_{\text{adj}} = 2016$ blocks, aiming to keep the average block interval at $T_{\text{target}} = 600$ seconds.

**Step 1 — Define the time actually taken for the last 2016 blocks.**

Let $T_{\text{actual}}$ = (timestamp of block #N) − (timestamp of block #(N − 2016)), measured in seconds.

**Step 2 — Define the ideal time.**

The intended time for 2016 blocks at 10 min each is:

$$T_{\text{ideal}} = 2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600 \text{ seconds (14 days)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the ratio of actual to ideal.**

$$\text{ratio} = \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the difficulty adjustment formula.**

The new target is rescaled by this ratio:

$$\text{target}_{\text{new}} = \text{target}_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600}$$

Equivalently, the new difficulty $D$ is:

$$D_{\text{new}} = \frac{D_{\text{old}}}{\text{ratio}} = D_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{actual}}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the clamping rule (safety bounds).**

To prevent extreme swings (e.g., a hash-power drop due to a power outage), the change is bounded to a factor of 4 in either direction:

$$\frac{1}{4} \leq \frac{D_{\text{new}}}{D_{\text{old}}} \leq 4$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 7-mark sub-question):**

> Suppose the last 2016 blocks took 1,814,400 seconds (21 days) to mine. The current target is $T_{\text{old}} = 2^{200}$. Compute the new target.

*Solution:*

$$\text{ratio} = \frac{1{,}814{,}400}{1{,}209{,}600} = 1.5$$

Since $0.25 \leq 1.5 \leq 4$, no clamping is needed.

$$\text{target}_{\text{new}} = 2^{200} \times 1.5$$

In Bitcoin's compact "nBits" encoding, this is stored as the **mantissa–exponent** form: the lower 24 bits represent the mantissa, the upper 8 bits the exponent.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bitcoin's Core Properties vs. Traditional Currency

| Property | Traditional Fiat (₹, $) | Bitcoin (BTC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Issuer** | Central Bank (RBI, Fed) | No issuer — algorithmic schedule |
| **Supply** | Elastic (central bank can print) | Capped at 21 M BTC |
| **Ledger** | Private, centralized, mutable | Public, decentralized, append-only |
| **Settlement time** | 1–3 business days (SWIFT/ACH) | ~10 min (1 conf) to 60 min (6 conf) |
| **Reversibility** | Chargebacks possible | Irreversible once confirmed |
| **Identity required** | KYC mandatory | Pseudonymous (public-key addresses) |
| **Accessibility** | Requires bank account | Anyone with internet access |
| **Inflation risk** | Yes (money printing) | Predictable, decreasing (deflationary) |
| **Censorship resistance** | Government can freeze accounts | No central party can block valid txs |
| **Verification** | Trusted central authority | Mathematical (cryptographic PoW) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Bitcoin's High-Level Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef blockNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3b66,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef headerNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#a8430a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef dataNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#155015,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef hashNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0d0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    blkN["Block N"]:::blockNode
    blkN1["Block N-1"]:::blockNode
    blkN2["Block N-2"]:::blockNode

    hdrN["Block N Header: version, prev_hash, merkle_root, timestamp, n_bits, nonce"]:::headerNode
    hdrN1["Block N-1 Header"]:::headerNode
    hdrN2["Block N-2 Header"]:::headerNode

    txList["Transaction List: tx1, tx2, ... txN"]:::dataNode
    merk["Merkle Root Hash"]:::hashNode
    prevLink["SHA-256 prev_block_hash link"]:::hashNode

    blkN --> hdrN
    blkN --> txList
    txList --> merk
    hdrN --> prevLink
    prevLink -.links to.-> hdrN1
    blkN1 --> hdrN1
    blkN1 --> txList
    hdrN1 --> blkN2
    blkN2 --> hdrN2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each block contains a header and a transaction list. The Merkle root of the transactions feeds into the header. The header of Block N includes the `prev_block_hash`, which is the double-SHA-256 of Block N−1's header — this single 32-byte reference is what makes the chain **tamper-evident** (changing any past block changes its hash, breaking the chain).

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Bitcoin Transaction Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef walletNode fill:#9467bd,stroke:#4a2c70,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef nodeNode fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3b66,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mempoolNode fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#a8430a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef minerNode fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#155015,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef chainNode fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0d0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    A["Sender Wallet A: signs tx with private key"]:::walletNode
    B["Recipient Wallet B: receives via public address"]:::walletNode
    N1["Full Node 1: validates sig and UTXO"]:::nodeNode
    N2["Full Node 2: validates sig and UTXO"]:::nodeNode
    N3["Full Node N: validates sig and UTXO"]:::nodeNode
    M["Mempool: pending valid transactions"]:::mempoolNode
    MIN["Miner Node: assembles block, runs PoW, finds nonce"]:::minerNode
    CH["Blockchain: Block added after PoW success"]:::chainNode

    A -->|"broadcast signed tx"| N1
    N1 -->|"gossip protocol"| N2
    N2 -->|"gossip protocol"| N3
    N1 -->|"valid tx"| M
    N2 -->|"valid tx"| M
    M -->|"select high-fee txs"| MIN
    MIN -->|"valid block + nonce"| CH
    CH -.confirmation.-> B
```

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — UTXO Consumption and Creation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef utxoNode fill:#17becf,stroke:#0a6670,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef txNode fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#5e5f0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outNode fill:#e377c2,stroke:#732e63,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    U1["UTXO 1: 0.7 BTC locked to A"]:::utxoNode
    U2["UTXO 2: 0.5 BTC locked to A"]:::utxoNode
    TX["Transaction: inputs = U1+U2, outputs = 1.0 BTC to B, 0.199 BTC back to A, 0.001 BTC fee"]:::txNode
    O1["New UTXO: 1.0 BTC to B"]:::outNode
    O2["New UTXO: 0.199 BTC change to A"]:::outNode

    U1 -->|"consumed"| TX
    U2 -->|"consumed"| TX
    TX -->|"created"| O1
    TX -->|"created"| O2
```

**Reading the diagram:** A transaction does not "debit a balance." Instead, it **consumes entire prior UTXOs** and **creates new UTXOs**. The leftover (after sending to the recipient) is returned as "change" to the sender. The difference between inputs and outputs is the implicit **miner fee**.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Halving Schedule (Geometric Decay of Issuance)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef epoch fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3b66,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef total fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0d0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    E0["Epoch 0: 50 BTC/block, Total issued = 10,500,000 BTC"]:::epoch
    E1["Epoch 1: 25 BTC/block, Total issued = 5,250,000 BTC"]:::epoch
    E2["Epoch 2: 12.5 BTC/block, Total issued = 2,625,000 BTC"]:::epoch
    E3["Epoch 3: 6.25 BTC/block, Total issued = 1,312,500 BTC"]:::epoch
    E4["Epoch 4: 3.125 BTC/block, Total issued = 656,250 BTC"]:::epoch
    E5["Epoch N: reward approaches zero, total converges to 21,000,000 BTC"]:::total

    E0 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> E4 --> E5
```

---

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q1, 3 marks]**
> **Q: Define Bitcoin. List any four key characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

Bitcoin is a decentralized, peer-to-peer electronic cash system that enables direct online payments between participants without the need for a trusted third party, as proposed by Satoshi Nakamoto in 2008. It is implemented as an open-source protocol and uses a distributed, append-only blockchain secured by Proof-of-Work consensus.

Four key characteristics:
1. **Decentralization** — No central authority controls the network.
2. **Pseudonymity** — Users are identified by cryptographic addresses, not real-world IDs.
3. **Limited Supply** — Total cap of 21 million BTC, enforced algorithmically.
4. **Irreversibility** — Confirmed transactions cannot be reversed or censored.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Q2, 3 marks]**
> **Q: What is the smallest unit of Bitcoin? State the block time target and the difficulty retargeting interval.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Smallest unit:** 1 satoshi = 0.00000001 BTC = 10⁻⁸ BTC.
- **Block time target:** 10 minutes (600 seconds).
- **Difficulty retargeting interval:** every 2016 blocks (approximately 2 weeks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (University ESE Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, Module 2, 14 marks]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Bitcoin in detail, covering the P2P network layer, the blockchain data structure, and the Proof-of-Work consensus mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the total maximum supply of Bitcoin, showing all intermediate steps. State the year by which the last Bitcoin is expected to be mined. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question A**

**(a) Bitcoin Architecture (7 marks):**

Bitcoin's architecture is a three-layer stack.

1. **P2P Network Layer (2 marks):** Bitcoin runs on a flat, unstructured peer-to-peer overlay network. Each participating node (full node, mining node, or lightweight SPV client) connects to 8–125 peers and exchanges messages such as `version`, `inv`, `getdata`, `tx`, `block`, and `ping/pong`. There is no central server; information propagates via a gossip-style flooding protocol with an average hop count of 12 to reach the entire network.

2. **Blockchain Data Layer (2.5 marks):** The shared ledger is a chain of cryptographically linked blocks. Each block header contains six fields: `version`, `prev_block_hash` (32-byte double-SHA-256 of the previous header), `merkle_root` (root hash of all transactions), `timestamp`, `n_bits` (encoded difficulty target), and `nonce`. The ledger is not a balance-database; it uses the **UTXO model** in which the state of the system is the set of all unspent transaction outputs.

3. **Proof-of-Work Consensus (2.5 marks):** To add a new block, miners must find a nonce such that the double-SHA-256 hash of the block header is numerically less than a network-wide target. This requires brute-force trial. The average target is recalibrated every 2016 blocks to maintain a 10-minute block interval. The longest valid chain (with the most accumulated work) is accepted as the canonical ledger, providing probabilistic finality.

**(b) Total Bitcoin Supply Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** The block reward at halving epoch $k$ is $R_k = \dfrac{50}{2^k}$ BTC. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Each epoch spans $H = 210{,}000$ blocks. The total BTC issued in epoch $k$ is $S_k = 210{,}000 \times \dfrac{50}{2^k}$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3:** Summing across all epochs:

$$S_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} 210{,}000 \times \frac{50}{2^k} = 210{,}000 \times 50 \times \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^k} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4:** Applying the geometric series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \dfrac{1}{2^k} = 2$:

$$S_{\text{total}} = 210{,}000 \times 50 \times 2 = 21{,}000{,}000 \text{ BTC} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5:** The last satoshi will be mined around the year **2140** (approximately 32 halving epochs after the genesis block of January 2009). **(1 mark)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the structure of a Bitcoin block. Explain the role of the Merkle tree in transaction verification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difficulty retargeting algorithm in Bitcoin. A network observes that 2016 blocks took 1,512,000 seconds instead of the ideal 1,209,600 seconds. Calculate the new target given the old target was $2^{220}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Answer — Question B**

**(a) Bitcoin Block Structure (7 marks):**

A Bitcoin block consists of two parts: a **block header** (80 bytes) and a **transaction list** (variable, up to ~1 MB pre-SegWit).

**Block header fields (with byte size):** `(see diagram below)`

```
+----------------+--------------------+
| Field          | Size (bytes)       |
+----------------+--------------------+
| version        | 4                  |
| prev_block_hash| 32                 |
| merkle_root    | 32                 |
| timestamp      | 4                  |
| n_bits         | 4                  |
| nonce          | 4                  |
+----------------+--------------------+
       Total = 80 bytes
```

**Role of the Merkle tree (3 marks):** All transactions in the block are pairwise hashed (SHA-256) and combined bottom-up to form a single 32-byte **Merkle root**, which is included in the block header. This allows **Simple Payment Verification (SPV)**: a light client can prove a transaction is included in a block by providing only $\log_2(n)$ hashes (the Merkle path), without downloading all transactions. Any tampering with a single transaction alters the Merkle root, which in turn alters the block hash, breaking the chain.

**(b) Difficulty Retargeting (7 marks):**

The difficulty is retargeted every $N_{\text{adj}} = 2016$ blocks. The new target is computed as:

$$\text{target}_{\text{new}} = \text{target}_{\text{old}} \times \frac{T_{\text{actual}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

where $T_{\text{ideal}} = 2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600$ seconds.

**Step 1:** Compute the ratio. **(1 mark)**

$$\text{ratio} = \frac{1{,}512{,}000}{1{,}209{,}600} = 1.25$$

**Step 2:** Check clamping ($0.25 \leq 1.25 \leq 4$ → no clamping). **(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Apply the formula. **(1.5 marks)**

$$\text{target}_{\text{new}} = 2^{220} \times 1.25 = 2^{220} \times \frac{5}{4} = 5 \times 2^{218}$$

**Step 4:** Conclude that since actual time exceeded ideal time, the network was **too slow** (or hash power dropped), so the target was **raised** to make puzzles easier. **(0.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Defining Bitcoin as "just a digital currency."** This loses 2 marks. You must emphasize it is a *system, protocol, and distributed ledger* — not just a coin.
> 2. **Forgetting the double-hash.** Bitcoin uses `SHA-256(SHA-256(x))` everywhere (block hash, transaction hash, address checksum). Writing only single SHA-256 costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing UTXO with account/balance model.** Always state explicitly that Bitcoin has **no account balances**; it tracks **unspent transaction outputs**.
> 4. **Skipping the geometric-series justification in the supply derivation.** A 1-line answer "$21$ M because halving" gets only 1 mark. You must show the sum.
> 5. **Omitting the ±4× clamping rule** in difficulty retargeting. Examiners allocate 1 mark specifically for this bound.
> 6. **Saying "10 minutes" without specifying "block time target."** Always pair the value with the term.
> 7. **Mixing up "block height" and "block time."** Height counts blocks; time is the timestamp.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- **Bitcoin** = decentralized P2P electronic cash system (Nakamoto, 2008) using blockchain + PoW.
- **Three components:** protocol rules, distributed ledger, digital asset (BTC).
- **Smallest unit:** 1 satoshi = 10⁻⁸ BTC.
- **Block time target:** 10 minutes (600 s).
- **Difficulty retarget:** every 2016 blocks, formula $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \times T_{\text{actual}} / T_{\text{ideal}}$, clamped to **factor of 4**.
- **Block reward halves** every 210,000 blocks; starts at 50 BTC.
- **Total supply cap:** **21,000,000 BTC**, reached ~year 2140.
- **Hash function:** double SHA-256 (SHA-256 of SHA-256).
- **Signature scheme:** **ECDSA on secp256k1**.
- **Consensus:** longest valid chain rule (Nakamoto consensus) → **probabilistic finality**.
- **Block header = 80 bytes:** version(4) + prev_hash(32) + merkle_root(32) + timestamp(4) + n_bits(4) + nonce(4).
- **State model:** **UTXO**, not accounts.
- **Confirmations:** community standard = 6 confirmations ≈ 60 minutes.
- **Genesis block:** mined by Satoshi on **3 January 2009**, embedded message: *"The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks."*
- **Bitcoin ≠ Blockchain.** Bitcoin is one application of blockchain; blockchain is the underlying data structure.
- **Decentralization + cryptographic proof + economic incentive (mining reward) = trustless consensus.**
- **Six confirmations** is the practical safety threshold; deeper blocks exponentially harder to reverse.
- **Block reward + transaction fees** = total miner revenue; fees become dominant post-2140.

> **End of Module 2 — Bitcoin Definition Notes | PEECT867 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
