- The block reward halves every **210,000 blocks**: $R(h) = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor h/210000 \rfloor}$ BTC. For Genesis: $R(0) = 50$ BTC.
- The Genesis Block pattern is **analogous to a root certificate in PKI** — both serve as trust bootstrapping mechanisms in their respective systems.
- **Mining condition**: $H_i < T_{\text{target}}$ — the Genesis Block satisfies this condition (Bitcoin's Genesis hash starts with ~40 leading zero bits).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mining - Tasks of Miners, Mining Algorithm, Hash Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mining - Tasks of Miners, Mining Algorithm, Hash Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — Mining: Tasks of Miners, Mining Algorithm & Hash Rate

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Mining? (KTU Formal Definition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cryptocurrency Mining** is the process by which new transactions are verified and added to a blockchain ledger, and new cryptocurrency units are created as a reward, through the expenditure of computational power to solve a cryptographic puzzle (typically a Proof-of-Work problem).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, mining is defined as a **distributed consensus mechanism** that simultaneously achieves three engineering goals:

1. **Transaction Validation** – ensuring double-spending does not occur.
2. **Block Generation** – bundling validated transactions into a tamper-evident block.
3. **Currency Issuance** – introducing new coins into the system in a decentralized, deterministic manner.

### 1.2 The Six Core Tasks of a Miner

A miner in a blockchain network (e.g., Bitcoin, Ethereum PoW, Litecoin) performs the following tasks in a continuous loop:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Tasks of a Miner (KTU Board-Favorite Listing)**
> 1. **Listen** to the peer-to-peer network for newly broadcast, unconfirmed transactions (the *mempool*).
> 2. **Validate** each transaction: verify digital signatures, check sender's balance, and confirm no double-spend.
> 3. **Assemble** a candidate block by selecting transactions (preferably with the highest fees) and constructing the **block header**.
> 4. **Compute** the Merkle Root of all transactions in the candidate block.
> 5. **Solve** the cryptographic puzzle (Proof of Work) by iterating the *nonce* until the block hash is below the network difficulty target.
> 6. **Broadcast** the solved block to the network. Upon acceptance, claim the **block reward** + **transaction fees**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "National Lottery Drawing" 🎰

Imagine a giant national lottery held every 10 minutes:

- The lottery organizers (network) announce a **target number** (difficulty target).
- Millions of people (miners) buy thousands of tickets (guesses/nonces) per second.
- The **first person** whose ticket number, when passed through a special mixing machine (hash function), produces a result **smaller than the target number**, wins the prize (block reward + fees).
- The mixing machine is a **one-way, deterministic function** — anyone can verify the winning ticket instantly, but no one can predict the winner in advance.

> This analogy captures the **asymmetry** of mining: *guessing* the nonce is extremely hard (brute force), but *verifying* the result is trivial (single hash computation).

### 1.4 Standard Mining Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value (Bitcoin, 2024) |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Time** | $T_{block}$ | **10 minutes** |
| **Block Reward (post-2024 halving)** | $R$ | **3.125 BTC** |
| **Hash Function** | $H$ | **SHA-256** (double-applied) |
| **Difficulty Adjustment Period** | — | **2016 blocks (≈ 2 weeks)** |
| **Network Hash Rate (2024 avg.)** | $H_{net}$ | **≈ 600 EH/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Block Header (Bitcoin-Style)

The miner does **not** re-hash the entire block. Instead, it repeatedly hashes only the **80-byte block header** while varying the *nonce*. The header has six fields:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Header Structure (Bitcoin)**
> | Field | Size | Description |
> |---|---|---|
> | Version | 4 bytes | Protocol version number |
> | Previous Block Hash | 32 bytes | SHA-256(SHA-256) of the previous block's header |
> | Merkle Root | 32 bytes | SHA-256(SHA-256) of all transactions in the block |
> | Timestamp | 4 bytes | Unix epoch seconds |
> | Difficulty Target (Bits) | 4 bytes | Compact encoding of target $T$ |
> | Nonce | 4 bytes | The variable the miner iterates: $0 \to 2^{32}-1$ |

## 2.2 The Mining Algorithm — Proof of Work (PoW)

### 2.2.1 Formal Statement of the Puzzle

Given a block header $B$ and a network target $T$, the miner must find a 32-bit integer *nonce* $n$ such that:

$$\text{SHA256}\big(\text{SHA256}(B \,\|\, n)\big) \;<\; T$$

where $\|$ denotes concatenation and $<$ is interpreted as a 256-bit integer comparison.

### 2.2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Initialize the Block Header**  
> The miner constructs fields 1–5 (Version, Prev Hash, Merkle Root, Timestamp, Bits). Only the *nonce* is unknown.

> **Step 2 — Begin the Nonce Search Loop**  
> For $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^{32}-1$, compute the double-SHA-256 hash of the header.

> **Step 3 — Compare Hash with Target**  
> Convert the resulting 256-bit digest to an integer $h$. Check the condition $h < T$.

> **Step 4 — Failure Branch**  
> If $h \geq T$, increment $n$ and return to Step 2. Modern miners exhaust nonces in microseconds, so they also vary the **extra-nonce** field in the coinbase transaction (a hidden, second degree of freedom).

> **Step 5 — Success Branch**  
> If $h < T$, the puzzle is solved. The block is broadcast, and the miner claims the reward.

> **Step 6 — Pooled Mining Note**  
> In mining pools, the actual target is replaced with a *share target* (much easier), so the pool can statistically distribute work and rewards proportionally.

### 2.3 The Difficulty Target

The **target** $T$ is inversely related to **difficulty** $D$. In Bitcoin:

$$D \;=\; \frac{D_{\text{ref}}}{T} \;=\; \frac{\text{0x1d00ffff in 256-bit form}}{T}$$

The reference difficulty $D_{ref}$ corresponds to the genesis-era target. A higher difficulty $D$ means a smaller target $T$, meaning a harder puzzle.

### 2.4 Difficulty Retargeting

Every 2016 blocks (≈ 2 weeks), the network recalibrates to maintain a 10-minute block interval:

$$T_{\text{new}} \;=\; T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{\text{Actual Time}}{\text{Expected Time}}$$

The actual time is the timestamp difference between the first and last block in the 2016-block window. The result is **clamped** to a factor of $\times 4$ max increase or $\times 0.25$ max decrease per adjustment.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1 — Essential Mining Formulas for KTU 2024 Examinations**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PoW Condition | $H(\text{header} \,\|\, \text{nonce}) < T$ | $H$ = SHA-256 (double); $T$ = 256-bit target |
| 2 | Hash Probability per Try | $p = \dfrac{T_{\text{int}}}{2^{256}}$ | $T_{\text{int}}$ = target as integer |
| 3 | Expected Nonces to Solve | $E[N] = \dfrac{1}{p} = \dfrac{2^{256}}{T_{\text{int}}}$ | Geometric distribution mean |
| 4 | Expected Time to Solve | $E[t] = \dfrac{E[N]}{r}$ | $r$ = miner's hash rate in H/s |
| 5 | Block Reward (Bitcoin) | $R_n = 50 \cdot 2^{-\lfloor n/210000 \rfloor}$ | $n$ = block height; halving every 210,000 blocks |
| 6 | Network Hash Rate from Difficulty | $H_{\text{net}} \approx \dfrac{2^{32} \cdot D}{T_{\text{block}}}$ | $D$ = difficulty; $T_{block}$ = 600 s |
| 7 | Difficulty Retarget | $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta t_{\text{actual}}}{\Delta t_{\text{expected}}}$ | Clamped to $[0.25T_{\text{old}}, 4T_{\text{old}}]$ |
| 8 | Mining Pool Payout (PPS) | $\text{Reward} = \dfrac{\text{Share Difficulty} \cdot \text{Block Reward}}{\text{Network Difficulty}}$ | Pay-Per-Share scheme |
| 9 | Solo Mining Block-Found Probability | $P_{\text{find}} = 1 - \left(1 - \dfrac{r}{H_{\text{net}}}\right)^{\!N}$ | $N$ = trials in window |
| 10 | Energy/Hash (ASIC) | $J/\text{hash} \approx 10^{-12}$ to $10^{-13}$ | Modern ASIC efficiency |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The PoW + Difficulty mechanism is a textbook example of a *self-regulating cyber-physical control loop* — it ties a digital parameter (target) to a physical constraint (real-world wall-clock time) using only decentralized consensus. This pattern is studied in **distributed systems, control theory, and digital economics**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expected Number of Nonces to Solve a Block

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

We model each nonce trial as an **independent Bernoulli trial** with success probability $p$ (the hash falls below the target). Let $N$ be the number of trials until the first success.

### 3.1.2 Derivation

The probability that exactly $N = k$ trials are needed equals the probability of $(k-1)$ failures followed by 1 success:

$$
P(N = k) \;=\; (1 - p)^{k-1} \cdot p
$$

This is the **geometric distribution**. The expected value of the geometric distribution is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[N] &= \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} \cdot p \\
&= p \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} k \cdot (1-p)^{k-1} \\
&= p \cdot \frac{1}{p^2} \quad \text{(using the identity } \sum_{k=1}^\infty k x^{k-1} = \frac{1}{(1-x)^2}\text{ with } x = 1-p\text{)} \\
&= \frac{1}{p}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Substituting $p$ for SHA-256 Mining

The target $T$ is a 256-bit integer. A uniformly random 256-bit hash falls below $T$ with probability:

$$
p \;=\; \frac{T}{2^{256}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
E[N] \;=\; \frac{2^{256}}{T}
$$

For Bitcoin's reference difficulty, $T_{\text{ref}} = 0x00000000\text{FFFF0000}\ldots$, giving $E[N] \approx 2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{9}$ trials on average.

> **Engineering Insight:** A single modern ASIC performs $\approx 100$ TH/s $= 10^{14}$ hashes/s. The expected time-to-solve is therefore $E[N]/r \approx 4.3 \times 10^{9} / 10^{14} \approx 43\,\mu s$ **if the miner owned the entire network hashrate** — illustrating why solo mining against the full network is futile.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Network Hash Rate from Difficulty and Block Time

### 3.2.1 Setup

We know that on average, the network produces one block every $T_{\text{block}}$ seconds. The average number of nonces tried by the entire network in that time equals $H_{\text{net}} \cdot T_{\text{block}}$.

### 3.2.2 Derivation

Setting the expected number of trials equal to the inverse success probability:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{\text{net}} \cdot T_{\text{block}} &= \frac{2^{256}}{T} \\
H_{\text{net}} &= \frac{2^{256}}{T \cdot T_{\text{block}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Recognizing that $D = 2^{256}/T$ (by definition of difficulty), we obtain the canonical estimator:

$$
H_{\text{net}} \;=\; \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{T_{\text{block}}}
$$

The factor $2^{32}$ is conventional — it converts the integer difficulty representation used in Bitcoin Core into a units count of hashes-per-second per unit difficulty.

### 3.2.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example — KTU Board Pattern**
>
> *Given:* A blockchain has block time $T_{block} = 600$ s, difficulty $D = 50{,}000$, and the mining algorithm requires $2^{32}$ hashes per unit difficulty.
>
> *Find:* The network hash rate.
>
> **Solution:**
> 
> $$H_{\text{net}} = \frac{50{,}000 \times 2^{32}}{600}$$
> 
> Compute numerator: $50{,}000 \times 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 = 2.147 \times 10^{14}$
> 
> $$H_{\text{net}} = \frac{2.147 \times 10^{14}}{600} = 3.579 \times 10^{11}\,\text{H/s} = 357.9\,\text{GH/s}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Solo Miner's Probability of Finding a Block

### 3.3.1 Setup

A solo miner with hash rate $r$ controls a fraction $\alpha = r / H_{\text{net}}$ of the total network. In one block interval (≈ $T_{block}$ seconds), the network performs $H_{\text{net}} \cdot T_{block}$ trials, of which the miner performs $r \cdot T_{block} = \alpha \cdot H_{\text{net}} \cdot T_{block}$.

### 3.3.2 Derivation

Each trial is independent. The probability of **at least one success** in $M = r \cdot T_{block}$ trials:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{find}} &= 1 - (1-p)^{M} \\
&= 1 - \left(1 - \frac{T}{2^{256}}\right)^{M}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $p \ll 1$ and $M \gg 1$, the binomial limit gives the **Poisson approximation**:

$$
P_{\text{find}} \;\approx\; 1 - e^{-pM} \;=\; 1 - e^{-\alpha}
$$

since $p \cdot M = (T/2^{256}) \cdot (\alpha \cdot 2^{256}/T) = \alpha$.

### 3.3.3 Numerical Example

> A miner with $\alpha = 0.001$ (0.1% of network) tries for one full block interval.
> 
> $$P_{\text{find}} = 1 - e^{-0.001} = 1 - 0.999 = 0.001 \approx 0.1\%$$

> **Key Takeaway:** Even owning 0.1% of the network yields only a 0.1% chance per block, confirming the lottery analogy.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Difficulty Retarget Computation

### 3.4.1 Setup

Suppose the previous 2016 blocks took $\Delta t_{actual} = 1{,}209{,}600$ seconds (= 14 days = expected). Difficulty stays the same. If instead $\Delta t_{actual} = 1{,}008{,}000$ s (12 days, blocks too fast):

### 3.4.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{new}} &= T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{1{,}008{,}000}{1{,}209{,}600} \\
&= T_{\text{old}} \cdot 0.8333
\end{aligned}
$$

Difficulty increases (target shrinks) by a factor of $1/0.8333 = 1.2$ (i.e., 20% harder).

> **Clamping check:** $0.8333$ lies in $[0.25, 4]$, so no clamp applies.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm vs Hash Rate vs Task Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1 — Cross-Reference Matrix (KTU High-Yield)**

| Miner Task | Algorithm Component | Hash-Rate Dependency | Energy Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| Listen / Validate | Mempool + ECDSA verify | None (CPU-bound, lightweight) | Negligible |
| Build Merkle Root | SHA-256 tree hashing | Negligible | Negligible |
| Solve PoW (Nonce Search) | SHA-256(double) brute force | **Linear** — higher $r$ ⇒ linear higher $E[\text{reward}/s]$ | **Dominant** — 95%+ of ASIC power |
| Broadcast Block | P2P gossip protocol | None | Network bandwidth only |
| Verify Other's Block | Single SHA-256 double-hash check | None | Trivial |
| Collect Reward | Coinbase maturity (100 blocks) | None | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Mining Loop — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start New Mining Cycle] --> B[Listen to P2P Network]
    B --> C[Collect Unconfirmed Transactions from Mempool]
    C --> D[Verify Signatures and Balances]
    D --> E{All Transactions Valid?}
    E -- No --> C
    E -- Yes --> F[Assemble Candidate Block Header]
    F --> G[Compute Merkle Root of Txns]
    G --> H[Fetch Previous Block Hash and Bits]
    H --> I[Set Timestamp and Initial Nonce = 0]
    I --> J[Compute H = SHA256 of SHA256 of Header]
    J --> K{H less than Target T?}
    K -- No --> L[Increment Nonce]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M[Solved Block Found]
    M --> N[Broadcast Block to Network]
    N --> O{Other Nodes Accept?}
    O -- No --> C
    O -- Yes --> P[Claim Block Reward plus Tx Fees]
    P --> A
```

## 4.2 Block Header Field Relationships

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Version 4B] --> H[Block Header 80B]
    B[Previous Block Hash 32B] --> H
    C[Merkle Root 32B] --> H
    D[Timestamp 4B] --> H
    E[Bits Difficulty 4B] --> H
    F[Nonce 4B] --> H
    H --> X[SHA256 once]
    X --> Y[SHA256 twice]
    Y --> Z[256-bit Block Hash]
    Z --> Q{Hash less than Target?}
    Q -- Yes --> W[Solved - Broadcast]
    Q -- No --> R[Change Nonce or ExtraNonce and Retry]
```

## 4.3 Hash Rate Hierarchy (Unit Scale)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[1 H per second H/s] --> A2[1000 H/s = 1 kH/s]
    A2 --> A3[1000 kH/s = 1 MH/s]
    A3 --> A4[1000 MH/s = 1 GH/s]
    A4 --> A5[1000 GH/s = 1 TH/s]
    A5 --> A6[1000 TH/s = 1 PH/s]
    A6 --> A7[1000 PH/s = 1 EH/s]
    A7 --> A8[1000 EH/s = 1 ZH/s]
    A8 --> A9[Bitcoin Network 2024 approx 600 EH/s]
```

## 4.4 Mining Pool Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Pool Server] --> W1[Worker 1 ASIC]
    P --> W2[Worker 2 ASIC]
    P --> W3[Worker N ASIC]
    W1 --> S1[Submit Shares easy target]
    W2 --> S2[Submit Shares easy target]
    W3 --> S3[Submit Shares easy target]
    S1 --> V[Pool Validates Shares]
    S2 --> V
    S3 --> V
    V --> M{Mining Pool Found Full Block?}
    M -- No --> W1
    M -- Yes --> D[Distribute Reward minus Fee by Share Contribution]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain the six core tasks performed by a Bitcoin miner.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Transaction Listening:** The miner runs a full node and continuously receives newly broadcast, unconfirmed transactions from peers via the peer-to-peer gossip protocol.
2. **Transaction Validation:** Each transaction's digital signature (ECDSA) is verified, the sender's UTXO balance is confirmed, and double-spend attempts are rejected.
3. **Block Assembly:** Valid transactions are sorted (typically by fee density) and a candidate block is constructed with a coinbase transaction awarding the reward.
4. **Merkle Root Computation:** A binary hash tree of all transactions is built; its root (32 bytes) is inserted into the block header.
5. **Proof-of-Work Search:** The miner iterates the 32-bit *nonce*, computing `SHA256(SHA256(header))` on each trial, until the resulting hash is numerically smaller than the network target.
6. **Block Broadcast & Reward:** The solved block is propagated. Upon network acceptance (consensus), the miner claims the block subsidy + accumulated transaction fees.

*(Each task: 0.5 marks — 3 marks total)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define hash rate. List the standard SI-style units used to measure hash rate from H/s to ZH/s.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Hash Rate** is the number of cryptographic hash computations a mining device (or the entire network) can perform in one second. It is the fundamental measure of computational power in Proof-of-Work blockchains.

| Unit | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Hash/second | H/s | $10^0$ |
| Kilohash/s | kH/s | $10^3$ |
| Megahash/s | MH/s | $10^6$ |
| Gigahash/s | GH/s | $10^9$ |
| Terahash/s | TH/s | $10^{12}$ |
| Petahash/s | PH/s | $10^{15}$ |
| Exahash/s | EH/s | $10^{18}$ |
| Zettahash/s | ZH/s | $10^{21}$ |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks for unit table = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Proof-of-Work mining algorithm** in detail, including the structure of the block header, the role of the nonce, and the meaning of the difficulty target. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A blockchain network has the following parameters:
- Block time $T_{block} = 600$ s
- Current difficulty $D = 9.2 \times 10^{12}$
- One unit of difficulty corresponds to $2^{32}$ hashes
- A solo miner operates an ASIC rated at $r = 50$ TH/s

Compute: (i) the network hash rate, (ii) the miner's fraction $\alpha$ of the network, and (iii) the expected time in days for the miner to find a block. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Proof-of-Work Mining Algorithm — Detailed Explanation (7 marks)**

The **Proof-of-Work (PoW)** algorithm is the cornerstone of Nakamoto consensus. It serves three functions simultaneously: (i) it provides a Sybil-resistance mechanism by tying block production to a scarce physical resource (electricity); (ii) it introduces a tunable, time-consuming computational puzzle that regulates block production rate; and (iii) it makes block history computationally tamper-evident — altering an old block would require redoing all subsequent PoW.

**Block Header Structure:** The 80-byte header contains six fields — Version (4 B), Previous Block Hash (32 B), Merkle Root (32 B), Timestamp (4 B), Bits/Difficulty (4 B), and Nonce (4 B). The miner fixes the first five fields and varies the *nonce* to search for a valid hash. (2 marks)

**Role of the Nonce:** The nonce is the only freely variable 32-bit integer in the header. It is the "search knob" — the miner iterates $n = 0, 1, 2, \dots, 2^{32}-1$ until a valid hash is found. When the nonce space is exhausted, the miner varies the *extra-nonce* field embedded in the coinbase transaction, which alters the Merkle Root and effectively resets the search space. (2 marks)

**Difficulty Target:** The target $T$ is a 256-bit number encoded compactly in the Bits field. The puzzle is satisfied when:

$$H(\text{header} \,\|\, n) \;<\; T$$

A smaller $T$ means a harder puzzle. Difficulty $D$ is defined as $D = 2^{256}/T$, so $D$ and $T$ are inversely related. The network recalibrates $T$ every 2016 blocks to keep the 10-minute block interval stable. (3 marks)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Network Hash Rate (2 marks):**

$$
H_{\text{net}} = \frac{D \cdot 2^{32}}{T_{block}} = \frac{9.2 \times 10^{12} \times 4.294 \times 10^{9}}{600}
$$

$$
H_{\text{net}} = \frac{3.951 \times 10^{22}}{600} = 6.585 \times 10^{19}\,\text{H/s} = 65.85\,\text{EH/s}
$$

**(ii) Miner's Network Fraction (2 marks):**

$$
\alpha = \frac{r}{H_{\text{net}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{12}}{6.585 \times 10^{19}} = 7.59 \times 10^{-7}
$$

That is, the miner controls $\approx 0.0000759\%$ of the network hashrate.

**(iii) Expected Time to Find a Block (3 marks):**

The probability of finding a block in one block interval (600 s) using the Poisson approximation:

$$
P_{\text{find}} = 1 - e^{-\alpha} \approx \alpha = 7.59 \times 10^{-7}
$$

The expected number of intervals until success is $1/\alpha$:

$$
E[N_{\text{intervals}}] = \frac{1}{7.59 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1.318 \times 10^{6}\,\text{intervals}
$$

Converting to time:

$$
E[t] = 1.318 \times 10^{6} \times 600\,\text{s} = 7.906 \times 10^{8}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
E[t] = \frac{7.906 \times 10^{8}}{86{,}400} \approx 9{,}150\,\text{days} \approx 25.07\,\text{years}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A 50 TH/s ASIC operating solo against a 65.85 EH/s network would take, on average, **over 25 years** to mine a single block — illustrating why **pooled mining** is essential for small operators.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the **difficulty retargeting mechanism** in Bitcoin. Why is the 2016-block window used? What are the clamping limits, and what would happen if no retargeting existed? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mining pool has 200 members with a combined hash rate of 800 PH/s. The network hash rate is 600 EH/s, block reward is 3.125 BTC, and transaction fees average 0.5 BTC per block. Calculate: (i) the pool's expected daily BTC revenue, and (ii) the expected time for the pool to find its next block. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Difficulty Retargeting Mechanism (7 marks)**

Bitcoin targets an average block interval of **10 minutes**. However, hashrate can change dramatically as miners join or leave the network. Without adjustment, faster hardware would gradually shorten block times, increasing orphan rates and destabilizing the currency's issuance schedule.

**The 2016-Block Window:** Every 2016 blocks (≈ 2 weeks at 10 min/block), every full node independently recomputes the new target. The expected time is $2016 \times 600 = 1{,}209{,}600$ s. The formula is: (2 marks)

$$
T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} \cdot \frac{\Delta t_{\text{actual}}}{1{,}209{,}600}
$$

**Why 2016 blocks?** This window is long enough to produce a statistically stable estimate of hashrate (law of large numbers) yet short enough to react to major hashrate changes within 2 weeks. (2 marks)

**Clamping Limits:** The ratio is bounded to $[0.25, 4]$, meaning difficulty can at most **quadruple** or **drop to one-quarter** in a single adjustment. This prevents malicious hashrate oscillations from causing wild swings that would destabilize the network. (2 marks)

**What if no retargeting existed?** A sudden hashrate doubling would halve the block interval, doubling the inflation rate and increasing stale-block rates. Conversely, hashrate collapse would slow blocks to hours or days, breaking transaction confirmation times. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Pool's Network Fraction and Block-Finding Rate (3 marks):**

Convert units: $800\,\text{PH/s} = 800 \times 10^{15} = 8.0 \times 10^{17}$ H/s. Network: $600\,\text{EH/s} = 6.0 \times 10^{20}$ H/s.

$$
\alpha_{\text{pool}} = \frac{8.0 \times 10^{17}}{6.0 \times 10^{20}} = 1.333 \times 10^{-3} = 0.1333\%
$$

In one day (86,400 s), the network produces $86{,}400/600 = 144$ blocks. The pool's expected blocks/day:

$$
E[\text{blocks/day}] = 144 \times 0.001333 = 0.192\,\text{blocks/day}
$$

**(ii) Daily Revenue and Time-to-Block (4 marks):**

Per block revenue = $3.125 + 0.5 = 3.625$ BTC.

$$
\text{Daily BTC} = 0.192 \times 3.625 = 0.696\,\text{BTC/day}
$$

Expected time to next block:

$$
E[t_{\text{block}}] = \frac{1}{0.192}\,\text{days} \approx 5.21\,\text{days}
$$

Or equivalently:

$$
E[t_{\text{block}}] = \frac{T_{block}}{\alpha_{pool}} = \frac{600}{0.001333} = 450{,}000\,\text{s} = 125\,\text{hours} \approx 5.21\,\text{days}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The pool can expect to find a block every ~5.2 days, earning ≈ 0.7 BTC per day before pool fees.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting Double SHA-256:** Many students write only `SHA256(header) < T`. The protocol requires **two** applications. **−1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing $D$ and $T$:** Difficulty $D$ is **inversely** proportional to target $T$. A higher $D$ ⇒ smaller $T$ ⇒ harder puzzle. Writing them in the wrong relation loses a mark.
> 3. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Hash rate units span 21 orders of magnitude. Converting EH/s to H/s incorrectly (e.g., using $10^{16}$ instead of $10^{18}$) cascades errors through the entire solution. **−2 marks.**
> 4. **Missing the Clamping Rule:** Students often state the retarget formula without the $[0.25, 4]$ clamp. This is a **favourite KTU 2024 Scheme question** — full marks require both the formula *and* the bounds.
> 5. **Treating "Nonce" as a Single Field:** Modern miners also vary the *extra-nonce* in the coinbase. Examiners reward students who mention this nuance. **+1 bonus mark.**
> 6. **Poisson vs Geometric Confusion:** Use the Poisson approximation $1 - e^{-\alpha}$ only when $\alpha \ll 1$. The geometric $1 - (1-p)^M$ is the exact form.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: Mining**

- **Six miner tasks:** Listen → Validate → Assemble → Merkle → Solve PoW → Broadcast/Reward.
- **Block header** is 80 bytes; only the **nonce** (4 B) is brute-forced.
- **PoW condition:** $\text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(\text{header} \,\|\, \text{nonce})) < T$.
- **Target $T$** and **Difficulty $D$** are inversely related: $D = 2^{256}/T$.
- **Block reward halves** every 210,000 blocks (Bitcoin); current = 3.125 BTC post-April 2024 halving.
- **Retarget period** = 2016 blocks ≈ 2 weeks; **clamp** = $[0.25, 4]$.
- **Network hash rate** estimator: $H_{\text{net}} = D \cdot 2^{32} / T_{block}$.
- **Expected nonces** to solve a block: $E[N] = 2^{256}/T$.
- **Poisson probability** of solo miner finding a block: $P = 1 - e^{-\alpha}$, where $\alpha = r/H_{net}$.
- **Hash rate units:** H/s → kH → MH → GH → TH → PH → EH → ZH (each step $\times 10^{3}$).
- **Mining pools** use **share targets** (easier than network target) to distribute work and reward by contribution.
- **Energy dominance:** ~95% of mining power is consumed by the PoW nonce search, not transaction processing.
- **Block time for Bitcoin = 10 min**, Ethereum PoW (deprecated) = ~13 s, Litecoin = 2.5 min, Dogecoin = 1 min.
- **Mempool** = the holding area for unconfirmed transactions visible to all full nodes.
- **Coinbase transaction** is special: it creates new BTC out of thin air and pays the miner; it also embeds the *extra-nonce*.
- **51% Attack:** If a miner controls $\alpha > 0.5$, double-spend becomes feasible. Mining pools approaching this threshold are a recurring security concern in KTU case-study questions.
- **Block confirmation:** A block is considered "settled" after ~6 confirmations (≈ 60 min) for Bitcoin, reducing reorg probability to $< 0.1\%$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wallets - Types of Wallets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wallets - Types of Wallets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Blockchain Wallet** is a software application, hardware device, or paper-based artifact that stores the cryptographic key pair (a **private key** and a mathematically derived **public key**) required to interact with a blockchain network. It does not store *coins* or *tokens* themselves; rather, it manages the credentials that prove ownership of on-chain assets and authorizes the signing of transactions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim-style):**
> A cryptocurrency wallet is a digital wallet that allows users to store, send, and receive digital currency. The actual currency is stored on the blockchain, and the wallet manages the user's private and public keys to interact with the network.

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition | Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **Private Key (k)** | A 256-bit secret scalar chosen uniformly at random from the secp256k1 field | Authorizes spending; must never be revealed |
| **Public Key (K)** | The point `K = k·G` on the secp256k1 elliptic curve | Shared openly; used to derive the address |
| **Address** | A short, hashed, encoded identifier derived from `K` | Acts as the user's "account number" |
| **Seed / Mnemonic** | A 12/24-word phrase (BIP-39) that deterministically regenerates the private key | Master backup mechanism |
| **Signature (σ)** | `(r, s)` pair produced by ECDSA signing of a transaction hash | Proves ownership without revealing `k` |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Physical Safe" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: A Bank Locker vs. a Blockchain Wallet**
>
> Imagine a bank vault where the *locker itself* is on a public street (the blockchain) and the *key to the locker* is in your pocket (the wallet). Your locker is identified by a unique number painted on its door — that is the **address** (public). The **private key** is the metal key that opens it. Anyone can drop money into your locker (because the door number is public), but only the person holding the key can take money *out*. Losing the key means losing access forever — no bank manager can reset it.

This analogy maps cleanly onto blockchain:
- **Address** → publicly visible locker number
- **Private Key** → secret physical key
- **Public Key** → blueprint (mathematically derived from the key, but cannot reverse-engineer it)
- **Wallet software** → the keychain that organizes and uses the key

## 1.4 The Core Distinction: Custodial vs. Non-Custodial

Wallets are broadly classified by **who controls the private key**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Custodial Wallet**: A third party (e.g., an exchange like Coinbase) holds your private key. Convenient but introduces *counterparty risk*.
> - **Non-Custodial Wallet**: *You* hold the private key. Full sovereignty ("Not your keys, not your coins").

---

## 1.5 Why Wallets Exist — The Engineering Problem They Solve

Blockchains are **decentralized, pseudonymous, append-only ledgers**. There is no central database of "account balances keyed by username/password." Instead, ownership is established purely by demonstrating knowledge of the private key corresponding to an unspent transaction output (UTXO) or account nonce. The wallet is the *human-facing abstraction layer* that:

1. Generates key pairs using cryptographically secure random number generators (CSPRNGs).
2. Derives addresses from public keys.
3. Constructs, signs, and broadcasts transactions.
4. Tracks balance by scanning the UTXO set or world state.
5. Manages backups via mnemonic seeds.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy of a Wallet

A modern blockchain wallet performs the following logical pipeline for every outgoing transaction:

### Step-by-Step Transaction Lifecycle

1. **Initiation**: User inputs recipient address, amount, and fee.
2. **UTXO/State Lookup**: The wallet queries the chain (via a local node or light client) to find spendable inputs.
3. **Transaction Construction**: A raw transaction object is assembled containing `inputs`, `outputs`, `version`, `locktime`, and `sequence`.
4. **Hashing**: The transaction bytes are double-hashed: `txid = SHA256(SHA256(raw_tx))`.
5. **Signing**: For each input, ECDSA produces a signature using the corresponding private key and the `sighash` (a modified hash that commits to specific fields of the transaction).
6. **Broadcasting**: The signed transaction is propagated to the mempool.
7. **Confirmation**: Miners/validators include it in a block; the chain state updates.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 2 (Lookup)** is necessary because a wallet must never attempt to spend an already-spent output (a *double-spend*).
- **Step 4 (Hashing)** converts variable-length transaction data into a fixed 256-bit digest that the signature algorithm can process.
- **Step 5 (Signing)** binds the user's intent (the specific tx) to their key — preventing malleability and replay.
- **Step 6 (Broadcast)** requires network connectivity; this is why wallets are often categorized as *hot* or *cold* (Section 2.4).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Valuation**

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K = k \cdot G$ | Public key derivation (EC scalar multiplication) | $G$ is the generator; $k \in [1, n-1]$ |
| 2 | $n \approx 1.158 \times 10^{77}$ | Order of secp256k1 (≈ $2^{256}$) | Total possible private keys |
| 3 | $\text{addr} = \text{Base58Check}(\,0\text{x}00 \,\vert\, \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(K))\,\vert\, \text{checksum}\,)$ | Bitcoin address derivation | 4-byte checksum appended |
| 4 | $H = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(m))$ | Double-hash used in txid | $m$ = message bytes |
| 5 | $\sigma = (r, s) = \text{ECDSA\_Sign}(k, H)$ | Signature pair | $r, s \in [1, n-1]$ |
| 6 | $\text{verify}(K, H, \sigma) = \text{TRUE}$ iff $r \equiv (s^{-1} H + s^{-1} r)\,G \pmod{n}$ | Verification equation | Decentralized trust anchor |
| 7 | $I = \text{SHA256}(\text{mnemonic}) \mod{2048}$ | BIP-39 word index | 2048-word wordlist |
| 8 | $k_{\text{child}} = \text{HMAC-SHA512}(k_{\text{parent}}, c_{\text{index}})$ | BIP-32 hierarchical derivation | $c$ is chain code |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

The wallet is the **single most security-critical surface** in a user's blockchain journey. In real-world engineering, wallets power:

- **Retail crypto payments** (e.g., Bitcoin Lightning, Ethereum dApps)
- **Enterprise custody solutions** (e.g., BitGo, Fireblocks) using **Multi-Party Computation (MPC)** and **Hardware Security Modules (HSMs)**
- **DeFi protocol interaction** (MetaMask signing smart-contract calls)
- **CBDC pilot programs** (digital rupee, e-CNY) where the wallet is regulated KYC-compliant software

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Significance:**
> As of 2024, the global crypto-wallet user base exceeds **580 million** addresses, and wallet-related exploits (private-key leaks, phishing, poor RNG) account for the majority of on-chain theft — making the study of wallet architecture a **core cybersecurity competency**.

## 2.5 Wallet Architecture Layers

A wallet is best understood as a **three-layer stack**:

| Layer | Function | Example Technologies |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | UI/UX, balance display, dApp browser | MetaMask UI, Trust Wallet |
| **Key Management Layer** | Generation, storage, signing, derivation | BIP-32/39/44, secure enclaves |
| **Network Layer** | Communicates with nodes/chain | JSON-RPC, gRPC, WebSockets |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Private Key to Bitcoin Address

This is the **most frequently tested derivation** in KTU exams. We trace how a 256-bit private scalar `k` becomes a human-readable address.

### Step A — Private Key Generation

A cryptographically secure random number generator produces a 256-bit integer.

$$
k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}, \quad n = \text{order of } \text{secp256k1}
$$

**Logic:** A CSPRNG ensures uniform distribution over the curve's scalar field. Reject $k = 0$ and any $k \geq n$ to avoid trivial/degenerate keys.

### Step B — Public Key Derivation (Elliptic Curve Multiplication)

$$
K = k \cdot G
$$

where $G$ is the fixed generator point of the secp256k1 curve. The result $K$ is a 2D point $(x, y)$ on the curve. **Unidirectional**: computing $K$ from $k$ is fast; the inverse (ECDLP) is computationally infeasible.

### Step C — SHA-256 Hash

$$
H_1 = \text{SHA256}(K_{\text{uncompressed}})
$$

where $K_{\text{uncompressed}}$ is the 65-byte serialization `0x04 \vert x \vert y`.

**Logic:** SHA-256 is a one-way function that compresses the public key to a fixed 32-byte digest, making the next hash function faster and the address shorter.

### Step D — RIPEMD-160 Hash

$$
H_2 = \text{RIPEMD160}(H_1)
$$

**Logic:** RIPEMD-160 compresses the 32-byte digest to **20 bytes (160 bits)**, producing the *public key hash*. This is the unique fingerprint of the user's public key — collisions are computationally infeasible (≈ $2^{80}$ work).

### Step E — Versioned Payload

$$
P = 0\text{x}00 \,\|\, H_2
$$

The leading `0x00` is the **version byte** for the Bitcoin mainnet (P2PKH address type).

### Step F — Checksum Computation

$$
C = \text{first 4 bytes of } \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(P))
$$

**Logic:** The checksum detects typos and transcription errors — crucial because addresses are often hand-copied.

### Step G — Final Pre-Base58 Payload

$$
P_{\text{final}} = P \,\|\, C
$$

This is 25 bytes: 1 version byte + 20 hash bytes + 4 checksum bytes.

### Step H — Base58Check Encoding

$$
\text{Address} = \text{Base58Check}(P_{\text{final}})
$$

The Base58 alphabet (excluding `0`, `O`, `I`, `l`) is chosen to be human-friendly and avoid visual ambiguity. A typical address looks like `1A1zP1eP5QGefi2DMPTfTL5SLmv7DivfNa`.

### Final Compact Equation

$$
\boxed{\,\text{Addr} = \text{Base58Check}\big(\,0\text{x}00 \,\|\, \text{RIPEMD160}(\text{SHA256}(k \cdot G)) \,\|\, \text{checksum}\,\big)\,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: ECDSA Signature Generation

For a message digest $H$ and private key $k$:

1. Generate a per-message random nonce:
   $$
   k_{\text{nonce}} \in_R [1, n-1]
   $$

2. Compute the nonce point:
   $$
   (x_1, y_1) = k_{\text{nonce}} \cdot G
   $$

3. Compute $r$:
   $$
   r = x_1 \bmod n
   $$

4. Compute $s$:
   $$
   s = k_{\text{nonce}}^{-1} \big(H + r \cdot k\big) \bmod n
   $$

5. Signature: $\sigma = (r, s)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why randomness matters:** Reusing $k_{\text{nonce}}$ for two different messages leaks $k$ — the catastrophic **Sony PS3 hack (2010)**. Modern wallets use **RFC 6979** deterministic nonces.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: BIP-32 Hierarchical Key Derivation

For a master seed $S$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \text{HMAC-SHA512}(\text{Key}=\text{"Bitcoin seed"}, \text{Data}=S) \\
I_L &= \text{left 256 bits} = k_{\text{master}} \\
I_R &= \text{right 256 bits} = c_{\text{master}} \quad \text{(chain code)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a child key at index $i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } i < 2^{31} \text{ (non-hardened):} \quad I &= \text{HMAC-SHA512}(c_{\text{parent}}, K_{\text{parent}} \,\|\, i) \\
\text{If } i \geq 2^{31} \text{ (hardened):} \quad I &= \text{HMAC-SHA512}(c_{\text{parent}}, 0\text{x}00 \,\|\, k_{\text{parent}} \,\|\, i)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
k_{\text{child}} = (I_L + k_{\text{parent}}) \bmod n
$$

**Logic:** Hardened derivation breaks the parent-child mathematical link, ensuring that a leaked child key cannot expose the parent.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Wallet Selection Criteria

| Criterion | Hot Wallet | Cold Wallet | Custodial | Non-Custodial | Hardware |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Internet exposure** | Always online | Air-gapped | Depends | Depends | Minimal |
| **Key control** | User | User | Third party | User | User |
| **Convenience** | High | Low | High | Medium | Medium |
| **Attack surface** | Large | Tiny | Trust-based | Medium | Small |
| **KTU-mark weight** | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| **Best for** | Daily spending | Long-term HODL | Beginners | Sovereignty | Mid-tier balance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wallet Taxonomy (Complete Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["BLOCKCHAIN WALLETS"] --> B["BY CONNECTIVITY"]
    A --> C["BY KEY CONTROL"]
    A --> D["BY KEY GENERATION"]
    A --> E["BY ARCHITECTURE"]

    B --> B1["HOT WALLETS"]
    B --> B2["COLD WALLETS"]

    B1 --> B1a["Desktop Wallet"]
    B1 --> B1b["Mobile Wallet"]
    B1 --> B1c["Web Wallet"]
    B1 --> B1d["Browser Extension"]

    B2 --> B2a["Hardware Wallet"]
    B2 --> B2b["Paper Wallet"]
    B2 --> B2c["Brain Wallet"]
    B2 --> B2d["Air-gapped Offline PC"]

    C --> C1["Custodial"]
    C --> C2["Non Custodial"]

    C1 --> C1a["Exchange Wallet"]
    C1 --> C1b["Hosted Web Wallet"]

    C2 --> C2a["Self Hosted Software"]
    C2 --> C2b["Hardware Wallet"]

    D --> D1["Non Deterministic JBOK"]
    D --> D2["Deterministic HD"]

    D2 --> D2a["BIP32 HD Wallet"]
    D2 --> D2b["BIP44 Multi Account"]
    D2 --> D2c["BIP39 Mnemonic Seed"]

    E --> E1["Single Signature"]
    E --> E2["Multi Signature"]
    E --> E3["Shamir Secret Sharing"]
    E --> E4["MPC Wallet"]
```

## 4.2 Transaction Signing & Verification Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["User Intent: Send 0.1 BTC"] --> B["Wallet Constructs Raw TX"]
    B --> C["Hash TX: H = SHA256 SHA256 raw"]
    C --> D["ECDSA Sign: sigma r s using private k"]
    D --> E["Signed TX: raw + sigma"]
    E --> F["Broadcast to Mempool"]
    F --> G["Miner/Validator Verifies"]
    G --> H{"Signature Valid?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Include in Block"]
    H -- No --> J["Reject and Drop"]
    I --> K["Chain State Updated"]
```

## 4.3 HD Wallet Tree Derivation Path (BIP-44)

```mermaid
graph TD
    M["Master Seed: 12/24 words"] --> M1["m: Master Private Key"]
    M1 --> P1["m/44: Purpose BIP44"]
    P1 --> P2["m/44/0: Coin Type BTC"]
    P2 --> P3["m/44/0/0: Account 0"]
    P3 --> P4["m/44/0/0/0: External Chain Receive"]
    P3 --> P5["m/44/0/0/1: Internal Chain Change"]
    P4 --> A1["Address 0: m/44/0/0/0/0"]
    P4 --> A2["Address 1: m/44/0/0/0/1"]
    P4 --> A3["Address 2: m/44/0/0/0/2"]
```

## 4.4 Custodial vs. Non-Custodial Trust Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph SCENARIO_A["Custodial"]
        U1["User"] -->|deposits| EX["Exchange Holds Keys"]
        EX -->|onchain TX| BC1["Blockchain"]
    end
    subgraph SCENARIO_B["Non Custodial"]
        U2["User"] -->|signs locally| WA["Wallet Holds Keys"]
        WA -->|broadcasts| BC2["Blockchain"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between a custodial and a non-custodial wallet. State one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Custodial | Non-Custodial |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Custody** | A third party (e.g., exchange) holds the private key. | The user alone holds the private key. |
| **Trust Model** | Requires trust in the custodian; subject to counterparty risk (e.g., exchange hacks like Mt. Gox). | Trustless — user is sovereign. |
| **Recovery** | Password reset via email/2FA (typical). | Only via the seed phrase; no reset. |
| **Example** | WazirX, Coinbase Wallet (hosted mode), Binance. | MetaMask, Trust Wallet, Ledger Live. |

*Mark split: Custodial definition (1) + Non-custodial definition (1) + Example (1).*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**What is a "brain wallet"? Mention its single biggest security weakness.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **brain wallet** is a cryptocurrency wallet in which the private key is *memorized* by the user, typically derived from a self-chosen passphrase via a hashing function (commonly SHA-256 of the passphrase). The user needs no physical device or written backup.

**Weakness (1 mark):** Human-chosen passphrases have **low entropy** and are vulnerable to *offline dictionary/brute-force attacks*. Attackers pre-compute hashes of common phrases, dictionaries, famous quotes, and song lyrics. The majority of brain-wallet funds have historically been swept within minutes of creation. Modern best practice disallows brain wallets in favor of BIP-39 mnemonic seeds generated from a CSPRNG.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** — [14 Marks] *(Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of a **Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) wallet** as per BIP-32 and BIP-39 standards. With a neat diagram, show how a 12-word mnemonic seed is used to derive child keys.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Compare **Hot Wallets, Cold Wallets, and Hardware Wallets** across the dimensions of connectivity, security, convenience, and ideal use case. Which would you recommend for (i) a daily active trader, (ii) a long-term HODLer, and (iii) a small merchant accepting crypto payments? Justify each recommendation.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**BIP-39 Mnemonic Generation (3 marks):**
1. Generate 128–256 bits of entropy $E$ using a CSPRNG.
2. Append a checksum: the first $\text{ENT}/32$ bits of $\text{SHA256}(E)$.
3. Split the resulting bitstream into 11-bit groups. Each 11-bit group indexes one of the 2048 words in the BIP-39 English wordlist, producing the 12/24-word mnemonic.
4. The mnemonic is then passed through **PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512** with the optional passphrase as salt to produce a 512-bit seed $S$.

**BIP-32 Hierarchical Derivation (3 marks):**
- The 512-bit seed $S$ is split into a 256-bit master private key $k_{\text{master}}$ and a 256-bit chain code $c_{\text{master}}$.
- For non-hardened child derivation at index $i$:
   $$I = \text{HMAC-SHA512}(c_{\text{parent}}, K_{\text{parent}} \,\|\, i)$$
- For hardened derivation (BIP-44 uses hardened for the first three levels):
   $$I = \text{HMAC-SHA512}(c_{\text{parent}}, 0\text{x}00 \,\|\, k_{\text{parent}} \,\|\, i)$$
- Child key: $k_{\text{child}} = (I_L + k_{\text{parent}}) \bmod n$, child chain code: $c_{\text{child}} = I_R$.

**Diagram & Justification (1 mark):** A tree diagram showing $m \rightarrow m/44 \rightarrow m/44'/0' \rightarrow m/44'/0'/0' \rightarrow m/44'/0'/0'/0/0$ is drawn and explained.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

| Dimension | Hot Wallet | Cold Wallet | Hardware Wallet |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Connectivity** | Online (web, mobile, extension) | Air-gapped (paper, offline PC) | Offline signing, online broadcast |
| **Security** | Low–Medium (key on internet-connected device) | Very High (key never touches net) | Very High (key in secure element) |
| **Convenience** | Very High | Low (manual signing) | Medium (requires physical device) |
| **Ideal Use Case** | Daily transactions, dApp interaction | Long-term storage / cold storage | Balanced: signing + strong security |

**Recommendations (justified, 1 mark each):**
- **(i) Daily active trader** → **Hot Wallet (e.g., MetaMask or a non-custodial exchange-trading wallet)** — speed and dApp integration outweigh the modest risk; trader should keep only "spending capital" in it.
- **(ii) Long-term HODLer** → **Cold Wallet (paper or air-gapped hardware like Coldcard)** — keys are isolated from any attack surface; ideal for vaults.
- **(iii) Small merchant** → **Hardware Wallet (e.g., Ledger Nano S/X or Trezor)** — receives frequent payments, requires signing, but cannot afford to keep large sums on an always-online device. The hardware wallet provides the optimal trade-off.

---

### **Question B** — [14 Marks] *(Choice 2, Internal Choice Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Sample Paper]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive step-by-step how a Bitcoin address is generated from a 256-bit private key. State the role of **SHA-256**, **RIPEMD-160**, the version byte, the checksum, and **Base58Check encoding**.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **Multi-Signature (Multisig) Wallet**? Explain the `m-of-n` scheme. Describe a **2-of-3** setup for a corporate treasury and discuss its advantages and one limitation.

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Step-by-step derivation (each step carries ≈ 0.5–1 mark):**

1. **Private Key $k$**: A 256-bit random integer, $1 \le k \le n-1$. (1 mark for stating role — sole secret)
2. **Public Key $K = k \cdot G$** on secp256k1. (1 mark)
3. **SHA-256 hash** of uncompressed $K$: condenses 65 bytes to 32 bytes. (1 mark)
4. **RIPEMD-160 hash** of the SHA-256 output: condenses to 20 bytes — this is the *public key hash*. (1 mark)
5. **Version byte** `0x00` (mainnet P2PKH) is prepended. (1 mark)
6. **Checksum** = first 4 bytes of `SHA256(SHA256(versioned_payload))`. (1 mark)
7. **Base58Check** encoding transforms the 25-byte binary payload into a 26–35 character human-readable address. (1 mark)

*Final formula as in Section 3.1 may be included for full marks.*

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution [7 marks]

**Multi-Signature Wallet definition (2 marks):**
A multisig wallet is a wallet that requires **$m$ valid signatures out of $n$ possible signers** to authorize a transaction. It is implemented via a special P2SH script `OP_CHECKMULTISIG` (Bitcoin) or smart-contract wallets (Ethereum Gnosis Safe).

**2-of-3 Corporate Treasury Setup (2 marks):**
A company generates three keypairs, held by (i) the CEO, (ii) the CFO, and (iii) a board-member/auditor. Any transaction must be signed by **at least two of the three**. This prevents unilateral action and single-point-of-failure theft.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- **Redundancy**: Loss of one key does not lock funds.
- **Collusion resistance**: No single insider can misappropriate funds.
- **Audit-friendly**: Transparent governance on-chain.

**One Limitation (1 mark):**
- Higher transaction fees (larger scripts) and slower throughput; also requires robust key-ceremony procedures to prevent collusion.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Wallet Questions**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Wallet" with "Storage"** — students often claim the wallet "stores coins." It does **not**; coins live on-chain. Wallets store *keys*. (Costs 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Skipping the version byte / checksum** in the address derivation — both are mandatory; omitting them loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Treating "Brain Wallet" as secure** — examiners expect you to flag the *low-entropy* flaw.
> 4. **Writing `Public Key = f(Private Key)` in words only** — examiners expect the equation $K = k \cdot G$ and the curve identifier (secp256k1).
> 5. **Forgetting internal choice clarity** — in 14-mark Part B, you **must** answer either Choice A or Choice B in full, including both sub-parts. Mixing partial answers across choices = zero.
> 6. **Not drawing a diagram** for HD wallets — KTU awards 1 mark for a correct tree/diagram.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- 🔑 A wallet **stores keys**, not coins. Coins are always on-chain.
- 🔐 **Private key** = 256-bit secret scalar. **Public key** = `k·G` on secp256k1. **Address** = Base58Check(RIPEMD160(SHA256(K)) ‖ checksum).
- 🌡️ **Hot wallet** = online (convenient, riskier). **Cold wallet** = offline (safer, slower).
- 🧊 **Hardware wallet** = secure-element device; best balance of security and usability.
- 🧠 **Brain wallet** = key derived from a memorized phrase. **Avoid**: low entropy → brute-forceable.
- 🌱 **HD wallet** (BIP-32/39/44) = one seed → infinite keys via tree derivation; hardened derivation at top levels.
- 👥 **Multisig** (m-of-n) = requires multiple signatures; ideal for corporate treasury and escrow.
- 🏦 **Custodial** = third party holds keys (exchange). **Non-custodial** = user holds keys ("Not your keys, not your coins").
- 🧮 Master equation: $K = k \cdot G$, $n \approx 2^{256}$, $H = \text{SHA256}(\text{SHA256}(m))$.
- 🛡️ **Never reuse** ECDSA nonces — use RFC 6979 determinism.
- 📜 Always provide a **diagram** for HD wallet questions — KTU rewards visual clarity.
- 🎯 **KTU Hot Keywords** to memorize: *HD, BIP-32, BIP-39, BIP-44, m-of-n, custody, P2PKH, Base58Check, secp256k1, ECDSA, deterministic*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Smart Contracts - Definition, Smart Contract Templates, Oracles, Types of Oracles, Deploying Smart Contracts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Smart Contracts - Definition, Smart Contract Templates, Oracles, Types of Oracles, Deploying Smart Contracts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: SMART CONTRACTS, ORACLES & DEPLOYMENT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Smart Contracts - Formal Definition

A **Smart Contract** is a self-executing computer program that runs on a blockchain network, automatically enforcing, executing, and documenting the terms of an agreement between parties without the need for a trusted intermediary. The contract code, once deployed, is immutable and its execution is guaranteed by the underlying consensus mechanism of the blockchain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (2024 Scheme):** A smart contract is a tamper-proof, deterministic, decentralized application (DApp) whose business logic is encoded in software, deployed on a distributed ledger, and executed atomically by the network's consensus protocol. The state changes triggered by a smart contract are validated, replicated, and persisted across all nodes of the blockchain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy - The Vending Machine

Imagine a vending machine:
- **You insert money** (input/condition) → **You press B5** (function call) → **The machine releases a Coca-Cola** (output/execution).
- The machine is **trustless**: you don't need to trust the vendor personally; the machine's mechanical logic guarantees the transaction.
- It is **deterministic**: same input always produces same output.
- It is **immutable once installed**: the price and rules cannot be secretly changed.

A smart contract works the **exact same way**, but on a global, distributed computer (the blockchain) instead of a physical machine. Instead of a coin slot, it accepts **cryptocurrency** or **cryptographic signatures** as input.

### 1.3 Smart Contract Templates

A **Smart Contract Template** is a pre-audited, reusable, parameterized code structure that encapsulates common business logic patterns. Instead of writing a contract from scratch (which is error-prone and costly), developers use templates for standard use cases such as:

- **ERC-20 Token Template** (fungible tokens on Ethereum)
- **ERC-721 Template** (non-fungible tokens / NFTs)
- **Escrow Templates** (multi-party fund holding)
- **Voting/Dao Templates** (governance logic)
- **Crowdsale Templates** (token sales)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why templates matter in KTU context:** Templates reduce development time, lower audit costs, and minimize security vulnerabilities. They embody battle-tested best practices and are usually provided by the blockchain platform's standard library (e.g., OpenZeppelin for Ethereum).

### 1.4 Oracles - The Bridge to the Real World

An **Oracle** is a trusted third-party service (or a decentralized network of services) that provides external, off-chain data to a smart contract. Since blockchains are **deterministic closed systems** and cannot natively fetch data from the outside world (websites, sensors, APIs), oracles act as a **secure data bridge**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Oracle Problem:** Blockchains are isolated by design to maintain consensus. However, most real-world contracts (insurance, finance, supply chain) require real-world data (temperature, stock prices, election results). Oracles solve this isolation problem.

**Intuition:** Think of a smart contract as a judge in a courtroom who is **blindfolded** and **deaf**. The judge can only rule based on documents handed to them. An **oracle is the witness** who goes outside the courtroom, observes real-world events, and brings verified information back to the judge.

### 1.5 Types of Oracles

Oracles are classified along **three orthogonal dimensions**:

| Dimension | Categories | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Software / Hardware | Where does the data come from? |
| **Direction** | Inbound / Outbound | Which way does the data flow? |
| **Trust** | Centralized / Decentralized | How many data sources/provers are used? |

Additional specialized types: **Contract-Specific Oracles**, **Human Oracles**, **Compute Oracles**.

### 1.6 Deploying Smart Contracts

**Deployment** is the irreversible process of uploading compiled smart contract bytecode to a blockchain network, after which the contract obtains a unique on-chain address and becomes part of the distributed ledger. Deployment requires payment of a transaction fee (gas in Ethereum, measured in **Gwei**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Once deployed, a smart contract **cannot be modified**. If bugs are found, the only remediation is to deploy a **new contract** and migrate users — this is a critical security and economic consideration.

---

## 2. Key Engineering Properties (Industry Standard)

The following **mandatory properties** of smart contracts are examinable in KTU 2024:

- **Determinism:** Given the same input state, every node must produce the exact same output (otherwise consensus fails).
- **Immutability:** Deployed bytecode cannot be altered.
- **Transparency:** Source code (and state) is publicly viewable on the blockchain explorer.
- **Autonomy:** No central party controls execution after deployment.
- **Trust Minimization:** Execution is guaranteed by cryptography and consensus, not human trust.
- **Atomicity:** Either the entire transaction succeeds (state changes committed) or it fails (state rolled back).
- **Termination:** Execution halts after a finite number of steps (guaranteed by gas limits).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Smart Contract Execution Lifecycle (Operational Breakdown)

The complete lifecycle of a smart contract, as required by KTU 2024 syllabus, follows these logical steps:

1. **Specification Phase:** Business requirements are translated into formal logic (often using state-transition models).
2. **Development Phase:** Source code is written in a high-level language (e.g., **Solidity** for Ethereum, **Rust** for Solana, **Move** for Aptos).
3. **Compilation Phase:** Source code is compiled into **bytecode** (low-level machine instructions for the blockchain Virtual Machine, e.g., EVM).
4. **Testing Phase:** Code is run on test networks (testnets) to verify correctness.
5. **Deployment Phase:** A signed transaction containing the bytecode is broadcast to the network.
6. **Execution Phase:** User transactions trigger the contract's functions; miners/validators execute the code.
7. **State Update Phase:** Resulting state changes are validated via consensus and written to the blockchain.
8. **Termination Phase:** Contract becomes a permanent, read-only, callable artifact (self-destruct option in some platforms).

### 2.2 Smart Contract Templates - Architectural Analysis

Templates are typically designed around the **Factory Pattern** or **Library Pattern**:

- **Factory Pattern:** A master contract deploys child contract instances with shared logic. Saves gas by reusing deployed code.
- **Library Pattern:** Reusable functions are stored in a separate contract address and called via `DELEGATECALL` (Ethereum) or equivalent opcodes.

**Key Template Components:**
- **Owner/Admin Controls:** `Ownable` pattern using `msg.sender` checks.
- **Access Control:** Role-based permissions (`RBAC`).
- **Safety Mechanisms:** `Pausable` (emergency stop), `ReentrancyGuard` (prevents recursive call attacks).
- **Upgradeability Patterns:** Proxy patterns (Transparent, UUPS, Beacon) that allow logic to be swapped while preserving state.

### 2.3 Oracle Mechanism - Detailed Theoretical Working

The oracle mechanism operates through the following **request-response cycle**:

1. **Smart Contract emits a Query Event:** The contract logs a request for external data (e.g., "What is the ETH/USD price at timestamp T?").
2. **Off-chain Oracle Node Listens:** An oracle service monitors these events using blockchain node infrastructure.
3. **Data Fetching:** The oracle queries external sources (REST APIs, IoT sensors, web scrapers).
4. **Cryptographic Signing:** The oracle signs the data with its private key.
5. **On-chain Submission:** The oracle submits a transaction containing the data and signature to the contract.
6. **Verification & Storage:** The contract verifies the signature, validates freshness, and stores the value in its state.
7. **Trigger Execution:** The original contract logic resumes, now informed by real-world data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Consideration:** A single centralized oracle is a single point of failure. Hence **Decentralized Oracle Networks (DONs)** like **Chainlink** aggregate data from multiple independent nodes and use cryptographic proofs (e.g., threshold signatures) to ensure tamper resistance.

### 2.4 Types of Oracles - Exhaustive Classification

#### A. By Source of Information

- **Software Oracles:** Interact with online data sources (websites, servers, databases). Most common type. Examples: Chainlink Price Feeds, API3.
- **Hardware Oracles:** Interface with physical sensors and IoT devices (e.g., RFID scanners, temperature sensors in supply chain, GPS trackers).
- **Human Oracles:** Individuals with specialized knowledge manually input data (used in niche cases like arbitration).

#### B. By Direction of Information Flow

- **Inbound Oracles:** Bring off-chain data *into* the blockchain (e.g., price feeds, weather data). **Most common type.**
- **Outbound Oracles:** Send blockchain data *out* to the external world (e.g., triggering a payment in a bank's legacy system when an on-chain event occurs).

#### C. By Trust Model

- **Centralized Oracles:** Single entity controls data provision. Fast and cheap, but introduces a central point of trust (defeats the purpose of decentralization).
- **Decentralized Oracles:** Multiple independent nodes provide data; consensus is reached on-chain. Trust-minimized but more expensive and complex.

#### D. By Specificity

- **Contract-Specific Oracles:** Dedicated to a single smart contract (custom-built, expensive).
- **General-Purpose Oracles:** Public infrastructure serving many contracts (e.g., Chainlink, Provable, Witnet).

#### E. By Computation

- **Compute Oracles:** Perform off-chain computation that is too expensive to do on-chain (e.g., verifiable random functions, machine learning inference, zero-knowledge proof generation).

### 2.5 Deployment Process - Step-by-Step Operational Theory

1. **Compilation:** High-level code → bytecode + **Application Binary Interface (ABI)**. The ABI defines the contract's function signatures and is essential for clients to interact.
2. **Gas Estimation:** Developer estimates the deployment cost using the formula:
   
   $$\text{Deployment Cost} = \text{Gas Used} \times \text{Gas Price}$$
   
   where Gas Used depends on bytecode size and constructor logic.
3. **Transaction Creation:** A special type of transaction (with `to = null` and `data = bytecode`) is constructed and signed by the deployer's private key.
4. **Broadcasting:** The transaction is propagated to the mempool.
5. **Consensus & Inclusion:** Validators/miners include the transaction in a block after consensus.
6. **Address Generation:** The contract's address is deterministically derived from the deployer's address and their transaction **nonce**:
   
   $$\text{Contract Address} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{RLP}(\text{deployer\_address}, \text{nonce}))[12:]$$
   
   where `[12:]` takes the last 20 bytes of the 32-byte hash.
7. **State Initialization:** The contract's storage is initialized, and the constructor function executes exactly once.
8. **Confirmation:** The deployment is considered final after a certain number of block confirmations (e.g., **12 blocks** for Ethereum ≈ 2.4 minutes).

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|
| $G_{\text{used}}$ | Gas consumed by contract execution | Gas units |
| $P_{\text{gas}}$ | Price per unit of gas | **Gwei** ($10^{-9}$ ETH) |
| $C_{\text{deploy}}$ | Total deployment cost | ETH |
| $N_{\text{conf}}$ | Number of block confirmations for finality | Blocks (e12 for ETH) |
| $H$ | Keccak-256 hash of deployment inputs | 256-bit |
| $B_{\text{size}}$ | Size of compiled bytecode | Bytes (deploy cost grows linearly) |
| $S_{\text{c}}$ | Contract storage size after deployment | Bytes / Slots |
| $\text{nonce}$ | Transaction counter of deployer | Integer (monotonic) |
| $\vert \text{Validators} \vert$ | Number of nodes in DON | n (threshold $t < n$) |

**Core Equations for KTU 2024:**

$$C_{\text{deploy}} = G_{\text{used}} \times P_{\text{gas}}$$

$$\text{Address} = \text{Keccak256}(\text{RLP}(\text{sender} \, \vert\vert \, \text{nonce}))_{last \, 20 \, bytes}$$

$$t_{\text{finality}} = N_{\text{conf}} \times T_{\text{block}}$$

where $T_{\text{block}}$ is the average block time (**13 seconds** for Ethereum post-Merge, **400ms** for Solana).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Decentralized Finance (DeFi):** Smart contracts power lending protocols (Aave), exchanges (Uniswap), and stablecoins (DAI) — all using oracles for price feeds.
- **Supply Chain Management:** Track goods using smart contracts + hardware oracles (IoT sensors).
- **Insurance:** Parametric crop insurance auto-pays farmers using weather data from oracles.
- **NFTs & Digital Art:** Smart contract templates (ERC-721, ERC-1155) govern ownership and royalties.
- **Voting & Governance:** Smart contracts execute DAO voting with proposal execution.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Smart Contract vs Traditional Contract

| Parameter | Smart Contract | Traditional Contract |
|---|---|---|
| **Enforcement** | Code + Consensus | Legal system / Courts |
| **Trust Required** | Trustless (cryptographic) | Trusted third parties |
| **Execution Speed** | Seconds to minutes | Days to years |
| **Cost Structure** | Gas fee (per execution) | Legal fees, intermediaries |
| **Mutability** | Immutable (post-deploy) | Amendable by parties |
| **Transparency** | Public (on-chain) | Private |
| **External Data Access** | Requires Oracles | Direct human input |
| **Dispute Resolution** | Algorithmic | Judicial |

### 3.2 Mathematical Analysis of Deployment Cost

Consider deploying a smart contract with the following parameters:

- **Bytecode size:** $B = 24{,}000$ bytes (a typical ERC-20 token contract)
- **Gas per byte of deployment:** $G_{\text{byte}} = 200$ gas (Ethereum constant)
- **Base transaction cost:** $G_{\text{base}} = 21{,}000$ gas
- **Constructor execution cost:** $G_{\text{ctor}} = 50{,}000$ gas
- **Current gas price:** $P_{\text{gas}} = 30$ **Gwei**

**Step 1:** Calculate gas for storing bytecode during deployment.

$$G_{\text{code}} = B \times G_{\text{byte}}$$

$$G_{\text{code}} = 24{,}000 \times 200 = 4{,}800{,}000 \text{ gas}$$

**Step 2:** Add base transaction cost.

$$G_{\text{tx}} = G_{\text{base}} + G_{\text{code}} + G_{\text{ctor}}$$

$$G_{\text{tx}} = 21{,}000 + 4{,}800{,}000 + 50{,}000 = 4{,}871{,}000 \text{ gas}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to ETH.

$$C_{\text{deploy}} = G_{\text{tx}} \times P_{\text{gas}}$$

$$C_{\text{deploy}} = 4{,}871{,}000 \times 30 \text{ Gwei}$$

$$C_{\text{deploy}} = 146{,}130{,}000 \text{ Gwei}$$

**Step 4:** Convert Gwei to ETH ($1 \text{ ETH} = 10^9 \text{ Gwei}$).

$$C_{\text{deploy}} = \frac{146{,}130{,}000}{10^9} = 0.14613 \text{ ETH}$$

This represents a typical mid-range deployment cost and explains why developers obsess over bytecode optimization.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Oracle Type Selection

| Use Case | Recommended Oracle Type | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Crypto price feed for DeFi | **Decentralized Software Inbound** | High attack value; needs tamper-resistance |
| Cold-chain temperature monitoring | **Hardware Inbound** | Requires physical sensor data |
| Triggering SWIFT payment from DeFi | **Outbound Centralized** | Legacy integration, lower security threshold |
| Random number generation in NFT mint | **Compute Oracle (VRF)** | On-chain randomness is deterministic/cheatable |
| Off-chain credit score lookup | **Centralized Software** | Data source itself is centralized |
| Multi-source market data aggregation | **Decentralized DON** | Reduces single-point-of-failure risk |
| Arbitration in legal disputes | **Human Oracle** | Requires expert judgment, not raw data |

### 3.4 Logical Derivation: Why Blockchain Needs Oracles (Formal Proof Sketch)

**Premise 1:** All nodes in a blockchain network must reach **deterministic consensus** on the post-state after transaction execution.

**Premise 2:** Determinism requires that the output depends *only* on the transaction inputs and pre-state.

**Premise 3:** External data sources (APIs, sensors) are **non-deterministic** due to:
- Network latency
- Server downtime
- Geographic routing differences
- Time-sensitive updates

**Conclusion:** If a smart contract directly calls an external API, different nodes will receive different responses at different times, leading to **consensus failure** and a **fork**.

**Solution:** Introduce oracles that **commit data on-chain** (in a transaction) before the contract reads it. Since the on-chain data is part of the pre-state (agreed via consensus), determinism is preserved.

$$\text{Contract reads: } \texttt{oracleData}[id] = \text{pre-state value committed by oracle tx}$$

$$\therefore \text{All nodes see the same } \texttt{oracleData} \Rightarrow \text{Consensus holds}$$

### 3.5 Smart Contract Template Derivation - The ERC-20 Standard

The ERC-20 token standard mandates the following **mandatory interface** (functions every ERC-20 template must implement):

$$\text{Interface}_{\text{ERC20}} = \{ \texttt{totalSupply}, \texttt{balanceOf}, \texttt{transfer}, \texttt{transferFrom}, \texttt{approve}, \texttt{allowance} \}$$

Plus **mandatory events**:

$$\text{Events}_{\text{ERC20}} = \{ \texttt{Transfer}, \texttt{Approval} \}$$

This template allows **universal wallet compatibility** — any wallet that supports the ERC-20 interface can interact with any compliant token. This is the power of standardization: it creates an **interoperability layer** without central coordination.

### 3.6 Deployment Finality Time Calculation

Given:
- Block time $T_{\text{block}} = 13$ seconds (Ethereum)
- Confirmation threshold $N_{\text{conf}} = 12$ blocks (industry standard for high-value transactions)

$$t_{\text{finality}} = N_{\text{conf}} \times T_{\text{block}}$$

$$t_{\text{finality}} = 12 \times 13 = 156 \text{ seconds} \approx 2.6 \text{ minutes}$$

For high-value deployments (>$10M), KTU students should note that many exchanges wait for **64 confirmations** (≈ 14 minutes) before crediting deposits.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Smart Contract Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Application or DApp Frontend] --> B[Construct Signed Transaction]
    B --> C[Broadcast to Blockchain Network]
    C --> D[Validator Node Receives Tx]
    D --> E{Tx Targets Contract Address?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Load Contract Bytecode from State]
    E -->|No| G[Simple Value Transfer]
    F --> H[Execute Function in EVM]
    H --> I[Compute Gas Used]
    I --> J{Gas Limit Sufficient?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Apply State Changes]
    J -->|No| L[Revert State, Burn Gas]
    K --> M[Propagate New State to Peers]
    L --> M
    M --> N[Block Finalized via Consensus]
    N --> O[Event Logs Emitted to DApp]
    O --> A
```

### 4.2 Oracle Interaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BC[BLOCKCHAIN NETWORK]
        SC[Smart Contract]
        OS[Oracle Storage Variable]
    end
    subgraph OFF[OFF CHAIN WORLD]
        EXT[External Data Source API Sensor]
        ON[Oracle Node]
    end
    SC -->|Step 1 Emit Query Event| ON
    ON -->|Step 2 HTTP GET| EXT
    EXT -->|Step 3 Return Data| ON
    ON -->|Step 4 Sign and Submit Tx| OS
    OS -->|Step 5 Contract Reads| SC
```

### 4.3 Smart Contract Deployment Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Phase 1 Write Source Code Solidity] --> P2[Phase 2 Compile to Bytecode plus ABI]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Unit Test on Testnet]
    P3 --> P4{Security Audit Passed?}
    P4 -->|No| P1
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Phase 4 Estimate Gas Cost]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 5 Sign Deployment Transaction]
    P6 --> P7[Phase 6 Broadcast to Mainnet]
    P7 --> P8[Phase 7 Validators Include in Block]
    P8 --> P9[Phase 8 Contract Address Generated]
    P9 --> P10[Phase 9 Constructor Executes Once]
    P10 --> P11[Phase 10 Contract Live Forever]
```

### 4.4 Oracle Type Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Oracles] --> SRC[By Source]
    ROOT --> DIR[By Direction]
    ROOT --> TRU[By Trust]
    ROOT --> SPEC[By Specificity]

    SRC --> SW[Software Oracles]
    SRC --> HW[Hardware Oracles]
    SRC --> HU[Human Oracles]

    DIR --> IN[Inbound Oracles]
    DIR --> OUT[Outbound Oracles]

    TRU --> CEN[Centralized Oracles]
    TRU --> DEC[Decentralized Oracles DON]

    SPEC --> GEN[General Purpose Oracles]
    SPEC --> CUS[Contract Specific Oracles]
    SPEC --> CMP[Compute Oracles VRF ZK]
```

### 4.5 Block Level Architecture: Smart Contract in a Blockchain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BLOCK[BLOCK N]
        H[Block Header Parent Hash Merkle Root Timestamp]
        T1[Tx 1 Value Transfer]
        T2[Tx 2 Contract Deployment Bytecode]
        T3[Tx 3 Contract Call Function X Args]
        T4[Tx 4 Oracle Data Submission]
    end
    subgraph STATE[WORLD STATE]
        S1[Account A Balance 10 ETH]
        S2[Contract C Storage Variable]
        S3[Oracle Data Feed Price 3500 USD]
    end
    BLOCK --> STATE
```

### 4.6 Template Based Contract Development Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LIB[OpenZeppelin Library] --> T1[ERC20 Template]
    LIB --> T2[ERC721 Template]
    LIB --> T3[AccessControl Template]
    LIB --> T4[Pausable Template]

    DEV[Developer Custom Contract] -->|Inherits| T1
    DEV -->|Inherits| T2
    DEV -->|Inherits| T3
    DEV -->|Inherits| T4

    T1 --> DPL[Deployed Contract Instance]
    T2 --> DPL
    T3 --> DPL
    T4 --> DPL
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a smart contract. List any four of its key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A smart contract is a self-executing program deployed on a blockchain that automatically enforces the terms of an agreement without intermediaries, using consensus and cryptography for trust.

**Four key properties:**
1. **Determinism** — Same input always produces the same output across all nodes.
2. **Immutability** — Once deployed, the contract code cannot be modified.
3. **Transparency** — Code and state are publicly viewable on the ledger.
4. **Autonomy** — Execution is not controlled by any single central authority.

*(Any 4 valid properties accepted; full marks for definition + 4 properties.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**What is an Oracle in blockchain? Why is it needed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An Oracle is a trusted third-party service (or decentralized network) that provides external, off-chain data to a smart contract and submits it on-chain in a deterministic format.

**Why needed:**
- Blockchains are **isolated closed systems** by design to preserve consensus.
- Smart contracts cannot natively access real-world data (prices, weather, IoT readings).
- Oracles act as a **secure bridge** between the off-chain world and on-chain logic, enabling contracts to react to real events without breaking determinism.

---

### PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each - Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture and working of a Smart Contract with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of bytecode and ABI in deployment. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A smart contract is a program that resides at a specific address on the blockchain and exposes functions for external invocation. Its architecture consists of:

1. **State Variables:** Persistent storage in the contract's account.
2. **Functions:** Executable code units (read, write, or pure).
3. **Events:** Logging mechanism for off-chain listeners.
4. **Constructor:** One-time initialization function.

**Working Flow:**
- A user signs a transaction calling a function with arguments.
- The transaction is broadcast; validators execute the contract code in a sandboxed EVM.
- State changes are tentatively applied, gas is consumed, and the result is proposed to the network.
- On consensus, the new state is finalized.

**Role of Bytecode:** When a high-level language (e.g., Solidity) is compiled, the output is **EVM bytecode** — low-level instructions the blockchain can execute. Bytecode is what gets stored on-chain at the contract's address. Every node must run the same bytecode to reach consensus.

**Role of ABI (Application Binary Interface):** The ABI is a JSON descriptor that defines the contract's function names, arguments, and return types. It is **not stored on-chain** but is required by external clients (wallets, DApps) to encode function calls and decode responses. Without ABI, a client cannot construct a valid transaction to interact with the contract.

> [!NOTE]
> *The diagram from Section 4.1 should be drawn in the exam; it carries 2-3 marks of the 7.*

---

**(b)** What is a Smart Contract Template? Explain the Factory Pattern and Library Pattern with their advantages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **Smart Contract Template** is a pre-written, audited, reusable code structure that implements common business logic, allowing developers to deploy standardized contracts (e.g., ERC-20, ERC-721) by inheriting or importing existing implementations rather than coding from scratch.

**Factory Pattern:**
- A master "factory" contract is deployed once.
