topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Blockchain and Allied Technologies - Blockchain and Cloud Computing, Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Blockchain and Cloud Computing — Formal Definition

**Blockchain-Cloud Convergence (BCC)** is a hybrid distributed-systems paradigm that fuses the **immutable, decentralized ledger** properties of blockchain technology with the **elastic, on-demand, utility-style compute and storage** services of cloud computing. The objective is to overcome the inherent limitations of both technologies when deployed in isolation: blockchain addresses the **single point of failure**, **trust deficit**, and **lack of auditability** in centralized cloud infrastructure, while cloud computing addresses blockchain's limitations in **storage scalability**, **computational throughput**, and **cost-efficient deployment**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Blockchain-Cloud Integration is the architectural coupling of a Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT) layer with a virtualized Cloud Services layer (IaaS / PaaS / SaaS) to deliver a trust-anchored, scalable, and auditable service-delivery framework.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Public Notary on a Shared Filing Cabinet

Imagine a government office where citizens deposit sensitive documents into a **shared filing cabinet** (the *Cloud*). Traditionally, any clerk with the master key can read, alter, or destroy any file. Now, suppose every deposit is **simultaneously notarized** by a network of independent notaries (the *Blockchain*) who sign a tamper-proof public register. Even if a corrupt clerk tampers with a file, the notary network's signed register proves what the original was, when it was deposited, and by whom. **Cloud** provides the *room* (storage + compute), **Blockchain** provides the *honest witness* (provenance + immutability).

### Core Entities and Properties (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Pillars of Blockchain-Cloud Integration (KTU Module 3):**
> 1. **Trust Layer** — Blockchain anchors verifiable, tamper-evident records of cloud transactions, access logs, and SLA breaches.
> 2. **Resource Layer** — Cloud provides elastic storage, VM orchestration, and compute offloading for blockchain nodes.
> 3. **Service Layer** — Smart contracts automate cloud-service-level agreements, billing, and resource provisioning.

| Property | Centralized Cloud | Cloud + Blockchain |
|---|---|---|
| Data Tampering | Vulnerable | **Immutable** audit trail |
| Single Point of Failure | Yes | **Eliminated** via consensus |
| SLA Enforcement | Manual / Legal | **Automated** via smart contracts |
| Trust Anchor | Third-party auditor | **Cryptographic consensus** |
| Latency | **Low (~ms)** | Higher (consensus overhead) |

---

## 1.2 Blockchain and Artificial Intelligence — Formal Definition

**Blockchain-AI Convergence (BAC)** refers to the architectural and algorithmic integration of **Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT)** with **Artificial Intelligence / Machine Learning (AI/ML)** systems to address the latter's well-documented weaknesses in **data integrity**, **model interpretability**, **monopoly of training data**, and **decision accountability**. Blockchain supplies AI with a **verifiable, provenance-tracked dataset layer** and a **decentralized governance fabric**, while AI supplies blockchain with **intelligent anomaly detection**, **predictive analytics for network optimization**, and **automation of contract adjudication**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Blockchain-AI Integration is the synergistic coupling of decentralized trust infrastructure with intelligent inference engines to create auditable, explainable, and democratized AI systems.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Self-Driving Courtroom

Consider a courtroom where a judge (**AI Model**) rules on cases using evidence files (**training data**). Historically, no one knows which evidence files were tampered with, who provided them, or whether the judge was bribed. **Blockchain** acts as a **public, append-only evidence locker** that timestamps every piece of data, records who submitted it, and locks it cryptographically. A **fraud-detection AI** watches the locker in real-time and flags suspicious patterns. If the AI's verdict itself is questioned, a **smart-contract notary** publishes the entire decision pipeline for public audit. *Blockchain ensures the data is clean; AI ensures the data is interpreted intelligently.*

### Core Convergence Patterns (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Four Canonical Integration Patterns of Blockchain + AI (KTU Module 3):**
> 1. **AI for Blockchain** — AI optimizes consensus, detects fraud, predicts gas fees, and routes transactions.
> 2. **Blockchain for AI** — Blockchain secures training data provenance, audits model decisions, and enables decentralized AI marketplaces.
> 3. **Smart Contract Oracles** — Oracles feed trusted off-chain AI inferences into on-chain smart contracts.
> 4. **Decentralized AI (DeAI)** — Distributed compute networks (e.g., federated learning + blockchain) train models without a central aggregator.

| Dimension | Pure AI | AI + Blockchain |
|---|---|---|
| Data Provenance | Opaque | **Verifiable, timestamped** |
| Model Auditing | Black-box | **On-chain decision log** |
| Trust in Outputs | Provider-controlled | **Consensus-validated** |
| Compute Model | Centralized GPU farms | **Distributed / federated** |
| Bias Detection | Post-hoc | **Real-time on-chain monitoring** |

---

## 1.3 Why These Alliances Matter — KTU 2024 Engineering Rationale

Both integrations are classified by KTU under **"Blockchain and Allied Technologies"** because they represent the **next-generation trust-compute paradigms** that B.Tech students must understand to architect systems in 2024+ enterprise environments (BFSI, healthcare, supply chain, smart cities, and government e-governance). The convergence moves us from a **trust-by-authority** model to a **trust-by-cryptography** model, a foundational shift mandated by the **NEP 2020** emphasis on interdisciplinary, application-driven engineering.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip (3-Mark Questions):** KTU frequently asks *"Why integrate blockchain with cloud / AI?"* — Always answer in three axes: **(i) Trust**, **(ii) Scalability / Intelligence**, **(iii) Decentralized governance**. Memorize the analogy in this section — examiners reward clarity and real-world examples.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Architecture — Blockchain + Cloud

### 2.1.1 Layered Architecture

The integration is modeled as a **six-layer reference architecture**, standardized in KTU 2024 Module 3:

1. **Application Layer** — DApps, mobile/web clients consuming hybrid services.
2. **Smart Contract Layer** — Solidity / Rust contracts automating SLAs and billing.
3. **Blockchain / Consensus Layer** — Permissioned (Hyperledger Fabric) or permissionless (Ethereum) ledger.
4. **Off-Chain Cloud Storage Layer** — IPFS, AWS S3, Azure Blob (storing bulk data; only hashes on-chain).
5. **Virtualization & Orchestration Layer** — Kubernetes, Docker, OpenStack managing compute pools.
6. **Physical / Edge Layer** — Data centers, edge nodes, IoT gateways.

### 2.1.2 Operational Steps

- **Step 1 — Request Initiation:** A client DApp requests cloud resources through a smart contract acting as an SLA-broker.
- **Step 2 — Off-Chain Execution:** The cloud provider (e.g., AWS EC2) provisions VMs/containers dynamically; resource usage is metered.
- **Step 3 — Cryptographic Commitment:** Metered usage data is hashed (e.g., SHA-256) and the digest is written to the blockchain.
- **Step 4 — Smart Contract Settlement:** The SLA contract auto-executes billing, penalty assessment, and refund logic based on committed metrics.
- **Step 5 — Auditability:** Any third party (auditor, regulator) can verify the off-chain cloud activity by re-computing hashes and comparing them with the on-chain digest.

### 2.1.3 Why This Works — The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why off-chain bulk storage?** Storing entire files on-chain is **prohibitively expensive** (Ethereum storage costs ~$10,000 per GB as of 2024). Only **cryptographic fingerprints (hashes)** go on-chain.
- **Why hashes and not full data?** Hash functions are **collision-resistant** (probability of two different inputs producing the same hash is $\approx 2^{-256}$ for SHA-256) — any tampering with the off-chain file is detected by a hash mismatch.
- **Why smart contracts for SLA?** They are **deterministic, unstoppable, and self-enforcing**, eliminating the need for legal intermediaries.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Architecture — Blockchain + AI

### 2.2.1 Data Pipeline Architecture

The integration typically follows a **closed-loop provenance pipeline**:

1. **Data Acquisition** — IoT / web / enterprise sources generate raw data.
2. **Provenance Anchoring** — Each data record is hashed and timestamped on-chain (e.g., transaction hash $T_x = H(\text{data} \, \vert\vert \, \text{timestamp})$).
3. **AI Training** — Off-chain ML models (TensorFlow, PyTorch) consume the verified dataset; model checkpoints are anchored on-chain.
4. **Inference & Oracle Posting** — AI outputs are pushed to the blockchain via **oracle services** (Chainlink, Band Protocol).
5. **Smart Contract Action** — Downstream contracts (insurance, DeFi, supply chain) consume verified AI inferences.
6. **Continuous Audit** — A separate **AI-watchdog model** monitors on-chain decisions for bias, drift, or anomaly.

### 2.2.2 Why This Works — The 'Why' Behind Each Step

- **Why anchor data provenance on-chain?** AI models are only as trustworthy as their training data. **Data poisoning attacks** (deliberately corrupting training data) cost the industry an estimated **$1B+ annually** (Gartner 2023). On-chain provenance makes such attacks **cryptographically detectable**.
- **Why use oracles for AI inference?** Blockchains cannot natively call external APIs (the *"Oracle Problem"*). Oracles bridge off-chain intelligence to on-chain logic **without breaking determinism**.
- **Why anchor model checkpoints?** To prove a deployed model is the *same* model that was audited — preventing *"version-swap attacks"* where a malicious vendor substitutes a biased model post-approval.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Cheat-Sheet

### 2.3.1 Cryptographic Commitment Formulas

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations — they appear in 14-mark derivations.**

$$
C = H(D \, \vert\vert \, t \, \vert\vert \, pk_{\text{signer}})
$$

*Where $C$ is the on-chain commitment, $D$ is the off-chain data, $t$ is the timestamp, $pk$ is the signer's public key, and $H$ is a cryptographic hash function (e.g., SHA-256).*

$$
\text{Verification: } V = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE} & \text{if } H(D' \, \vert\vert \, t' \, \vert\vert \, pk') = C_{\text{stored}} \\ \text{FALSE} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

*Verification succeeds only if the recomputed hash equals the on-chain stored commitment.*

$$
T_{\text{throughput}}^{\text{hybrid}} = \min\!\left(T_{\text{blockchain}},\; T_{\text{cloud\_offload}}\right) \cdot \eta_{\text{consensus}}
$$

*Hybrid throughput is bounded by the slower of the two subsystems; $\eta_{\text{consensus}} \in [0,1]$ is the consensus efficiency factor.*

$$
C_{\text{on-chain}} = \text{Size}_{\text{bytes}} \times \text{Gas}_{\text{per\_byte}}
$$

*On-chain storage cost scales linearly with data size — justifying the off-chain-with-on-chain-hash pattern.*

### 2.3.2 Comparison Matrix (High-Yield for 3-Mark Questions)

| Dimension | Centralized Cloud | Blockchain-Cloud | Centralized AI | Blockchain-AI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trust Model** | Authority-based | Cryptographic | Provider trust | Verifiable |
| **Data Integrity** | Mutable logs | Immutable ledger | Mutable datasets | Provenance-locked |
| **Fault Tolerance** | Single point of failure | Byzantine tolerant | Single point of failure | Distributed |
| **Scalability** | **High** (elastic) | Moderate (consensus bottleneck) | High | Limited (compute) |
| **Cost Model** | Pay-per-use | Pay-per-use + gas | CapEx + OpEx | Token-incentivized |
| **Auditability** | Manual | **Native** | Post-hoc | **Real-time** |
| **Regulatory Fit** | GDPR concerns | Privacy via zero-knowledge | Black-box risk | Explainable by design |
| **Use Case** | SaaS apps | SLA enforcement, audit logs | Predictive analytics | Audited ML, DeAI |

### 2.3.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU 2024)

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Consensus Latency | $L_c$ | 1–60 s | vs. decentralization |
| Off-Chain Throughput | $T_{\text{off}}$ | 1,000–100,000 TPS | vs. cost |
| On-Chain Throughput | $T_{\text{on}}$ | 3–100,000 TPS | vs. finality |
| Data Hash Collision Probability | $P_{\text{coll}}$ | $\leq 2^{-256}$ | vs. hash length |
| AI Model Audit Time | $A_t$ | Seconds (on-chain) vs. days (manual) | vs. storage cost |
| SLA Violation Detection | $D_{\text{SLA}}$ | Real-time | vs. oracle cost |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Healthcare:** Patient records stored encrypted in cloud; access logs and consent on blockchain; AI diagnoses verified via anchored model checkpoints.
- **Finance:** Cloud executes high-frequency trades; blockchain audits settlement; AI flags fraudulent transactions; smart contracts auto-reverse fraudulent trades.
- **Supply Chain:** IoT sensors (cloud-orchestrated) feed AI demand-forecast models; blockchain anchors shipment provenance; oracles trigger automated insurance payouts.
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic AI runs on cloud; citizen-data access controlled by blockchain-based self-sovereign identity (SSI); sensor tampering detected by AI and recorded immutably.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — On-Chain Storage Cost Justification

This derivation is **frequently asked as a 7-mark sub-question** ("Explain why we store only hashes on-chain and not the full data, with mathematical justification").

### Step 1: Define On-Chain Storage Cost Function

The cost of storing $n$ bytes of arbitrary data on a public blockchain is:

$$
C_{\text{on-chain}}(n) = n \cdot g_b \cdot P_{\text{gas}}
$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $n$ = number of bytes to store
- $g_b$ = gas consumed per byte (e.g., Ethereum SSTORE = 20,000 gas for 32 bytes, $\approx 625$ gas/byte)
- $P_{\text{gas}}$ = market price of gas (e.g., 30 Gwei = $30 \times 10^{-9}$ ETH)

### Step 2: Substitute Numerical Values

For a 1 MB medical-imaging file ($n = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes) on Ethereum at 30 Gwei and ETH = $3{,}000$:

$$
C_{\text{on-chain}} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 625 \times 30 \times 10^{-9} \times 3{,}000
$$

**Convert to USD:**

$$
C_{\text{on-chain}} = 1{,}048{,}576 \times 625 \times 9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ ETH}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
1{,}048{,}576 \times 625 &= 655{,}360{,}000 \text{ gas} \\
655{,}360{,}000 \times 30 \times 10^{-9} &= 19.6608 \text{ ETH} \\
19.6608 \times 3{,}000 &= \$58{,}982.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Storing a 1 MB file on-chain costs approximately **$58,982** at 2024 prices — prohibitively expensive.

### Step 4: Compare with Off-Chain + On-Chain Hash Pattern

For the same file, we store only a 32-byte SHA-256 hash on-chain:

$$
C_{\text{hash}} = 32 \times 625 \times 30 \times 10^{-9} \times 3{,}000
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{hash}} &= 20{,}000 \times 9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ ETH} \\
&= 1.8 \text{ ETH} \\
&= \$5{,}400
\end{aligned}
$$

**The off-chain file is stored in IPFS / S3 at $\approx \$0.023$/GB/month**, i.e., a one-time $\$0.000023$ for 1 MB.

### Step 5: Cost Ratio

$$
\text{Cost Ratio} = \frac{C_{\text{on-chain full}}}{C_{\text{hash}}} = \frac{58{,}982.40}{5{,}400.00} \approx 10.93\times
$$

Even the hash-on-chain approach is $10.93\times$ costlier than a full off-chain solution — but **integrity guarantees** justify the premium. For 100 KB files, the on-chain cost is only **$5.40**, making it viable for *small critical metadata* (e.g., consent records, audit logs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight for KTU:** The breakeven file size where on-chain storage becomes cheaper than off-chain + on-chain hash is **never reached** in practice. Hence, the **hash-anchoring pattern is universally adopted**.

---

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Trust Score for Hybrid System

A frequently asked 7-mark question: *"Derive a composite trust score for a hybrid blockchain-cloud-AI system."*

### Step 1: Define Component Trust Scores

Let $T_{\text{BC}} \in [0,1]$ = trust score of blockchain layer (proportional to decentralization, e.g., number of validators $n$).

Let $T_{\text{CL}} \in [0,1]$ = trust score of cloud layer (proportional to SLA adherence, certifications).

Let $T_{\text{AI}} \in [0,1]$ = trust score of AI layer (proportional to model interpretability, audit frequency).

### Step 2: Individual Trust as a Function of Validator Count

For a Byzantine Fault Tolerant (BFT) blockchain with $n$ validators tolerating $f$ Byzantine faults:

$$
T_{\text{BC}}(n) = 1 - \left(\frac{f}{n}\right)
$$

For a typical BFT system, $f = \lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor$, giving:

$$
T_{\text{BC}}(n) = 1 - \frac{\lfloor (n-1)/3 \rfloor}{n}
$$

### Step 3: Cloud Trust as SLA Adherence

$$
T_{\text{CL}} = \frac{\text{Uptime}_{\text{achieved}}}{\text{Uptime}_{\text{promised}}} \cdot w_1 + \text{CertScore} \cdot w_2
$$

Where $w_1 + w_2 = 1$ (e.g., $w_1 = 0.7$, $w_2 = 0.3$).

### Step 4: AI Trust via Audit Frequency

$$
T_{\text{AI}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda A}
$$

Where $A$ = number of audits per year, $\lambda$ = audit quality coefficient (e.g., 0.1).

### Step 5: Composite Hybrid Trust Score

The composite trust is a **weighted geometric mean** (preferred over arithmetic mean because it penalizes weak links):

$$
T_{\text{hybrid}} = T_{\text{BC}}^{w_b} \times T_{\text{CL}}^{w_c} \times T_{\text{AI}}^{w_a}
$$

Subject to $w_b + w_c + w_a = 1$, typically $w_b = 0.4$, $w_c = 0.3$, $w_a = 0.3$.

### Step 6: Worked Example

Suppose $n = 21$ validators, uptime ratio = 0.999, cert score = 0.95, audits = 12/year, $\lambda = 0.1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{BC}} &= 1 - \frac{\lfloor 20/3 \rfloor}{21} = 1 - \frac{6}{21} = 0.714 \\
T_{\text{CL}} &= 0.999 \times 0.7 + 0.95 \times 0.3 = 0.6993 + 0.285 = 0.9843 \\
T_{\text{AI}} &= 1 - e^{-0.1 \times 12} = 1 - e^{-1.2} = 1 - 0.3012 = 0.6988
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{hybrid}} &= 0.714^{0.4} \times 0.9843^{0.3} \times 0.6988^{0.3} \\
&= 0.8917 \times 0.9952 \times 0.9079 \\
&= 0.806
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A composite trust score of **0.806 (80.6%)** indicates a robust hybrid system suitable for high-stakes deployments (BFSI, healthcare).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Blockchain-Cloud Integration Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client DApp / Web Portal]
    SC[Smart Contract Layer - SLA Broker]
    BC[Blockchain Consensus Layer - Hyperledger]
    OC[Off-Chain Cloud Storage - IPFS / S3]
    ORC[Orchestration Layer - Kubernetes]
    PHY[Physical Infrastructure - Data Centers]
    AUD[Auditor / Regulator Node]
    
    Client -->|1. Request Service| SC
    SC -->|2. Trigger Provisioning| ORC
    ORC -->|3. Spin Up VMs| PHY
    PHY -->|4. Store Bulk Data| OC
    OC -->|5. Return Hash H| SC
    SC -->|6. Anchor H to Ledger| BC
    BC -->|7. Emit SLA Receipt| Client
    BC -->|8. Audit Trail| AUD
    AUD -->|9. Re-hash and Verify| OC
```

**Reading the diagram:** The numbered arrows (1→9) depict the request-to-audit lifecycle. Note that bulk data **never** traverses the blockchain — only the 32-byte SHA-256 hash does (step 5→6).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Blockchain-AI Integration Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Data Sources - IoT / Web / Enterprise]
    PROV[Provenance Anchor - On-Chain Hash]
    ML[AI/ML Training Layer - Off-Chain GPU]
    CHK[Model Checkpoint Anchor - On-Chain]
    INF[Inference Engine - On-Demand]
    ORC[Oracle Service - Chainlink / Band]
    CT[Smart Contract Consumer - DeFi / Insurance]
    AUD[AI Watchdog - Bias / Drift Detector]
    
    SRC -->|Raw Data H| PROV
    PROV -->|Verified Dataset| ML
    ML -->|Model v1 Hash| CHK
    CHK -->|Deployed Model| INF
    INF -->|Prediction Y| ORC
    ORC -->|Signed Inference| CT
    CT -->|Decision Log| AUD
    AUD -->|Flag Anomaly| ML
    ML -->|Updated Model v2| CHK
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is a **closed feedback loop**. The AI watchdog (bottom) continuously audits the inference pipeline and triggers model retraining when bias or drift is detected. The updated model is re-anchored on-chain, ensuring **end-to-end auditability**.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decision Flow for Integration Choice

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[New Engineering Project]
    Q1{Need Immutable Audit Trail?}

    BC[Use Blockchain Standalone]

    START --> Q1
    Q1 -->|No| TRAD
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q4

```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **KTU 2024 decision rubric** for choosing an integration pattern. The hybrid `Blockchain + Cloud + AI` (bottom-right) is the most powerful but most expensive — reserved for high-stakes domains like autonomous-vehicle fleets or national healthcare AI.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Trust Score Composition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Blockchain Trust T_BC]
    B[Cloud Trust T_CL]
    C[AI Trust T_AI]
    W1[Weight w_b = 0.4]
    W2[Weight w_c = 0.3]
    W3[Weight w_a = 0.3]
    G[Geometric Mean T_hybrid]
    
    A --> G
    B --> G
    C --> G
    W1 --> G
    W2 --> G
    W3 --> G
    G --> OUT[Final Trust Score - 0 to 1]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Mirrors the **composite trust score derivation** in Section 3.3. The geometric mean ensures that a **single weak layer drags down the entire score** — embodying the engineering principle *"a chain is only as strong as its weakest link."*

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **Define Blockchain-Cloud Integration. List any four benefits of combining blockchain with cloud computing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Blockchain-Cloud Integration is the architectural coupling of a Distributed Ledger Technology (DLT) layer with a virtualized Cloud Services layer (IaaS/PaaS/SaaS) to deliver a trust-anchored, scalable, and auditable service-delivery framework.

**Four Benefits (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Immutable Audit Trail** — Cloud access logs, SLA breaches, and resource usage are anchored on-chain, preventing tampering.
2. **Automated SLA Enforcement** — Smart contracts execute penalties and refunds without manual intervention.
3. **Elimination of Single Point of Failure** — Distributed ledger nodes replicate cloud state across geographies.
4. **Cost-Efficient Storage** — Bulk data stays off-chain (IPFS/S3); only cryptographic hashes are anchored, reducing gas costs by ~95%.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]

> **Explain how blockchain enhances AI systems. Give two real-world use cases.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Explanation (1 Mark):** Blockchain enhances AI by providing **verifiable data provenance**, **immutable model audit logs**, and **decentralized governance** for training data and model checkpoints, thereby addressing the *black-box* and *data-poisoning* weaknesses of standalone AI.

**Use Cases (2 Marks — 1 each):**

1. **Healthcare AI Diagnosis (1 Mark):** Patient MRI scans are hashed and anchored on-chain before being used to train a tumor-detection AI. The model checkpoint is also anchored, allowing regulators to verify that the deployed model is the same as the audited one. Any data tampering is cryptographically detectable.
2. **Decentralized AI Marketplaces (1 Mark):** Platforms like *Ocean Protocol* use blockchain to enable data owners to monetise datasets for AI training without surrendering custody, with smart contracts handling licensing and royalty distribution automatically.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block-level architecture diagram, explain the six-layer Blockchain-Cloud integration framework. Discuss how smart contracts automate SLA enforcement in cloud environments.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the on-chain storage cost equation $C_{\text{on-chain}}(n) = n \cdot g_b \cdot P_{\text{gas}}$ and justify mathematically why only cryptographic hashes (and not full data) are stored on-chain. Use a numerical example with $n = 1$ MB on Ethereum.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Architecture Diagram (2 Marks):** Draw the six-layer stack:

1. **Application Layer** — DApps and clients (e.g., a SaaS dashboard)
2. **Smart Contract Layer** — SLA-broker contracts in Solidity
3. **Blockchain / Consensus Layer** — Hyperledger Fabric / Ethereum
4. **Off-Chain Cloud Storage Layer** — IPFS, AWS S3
5. **Virtualization & Orchestration Layer** — Kubernetes, Docker
6. **Physical / Edge Layer** — Data centers, edge nodes

**Explanation of Each Layer (3 Marks — 0.5 each):**

- **Application:** End-user interfaces consume hybrid services.
- **Smart Contract:** Encode SLA terms (uptime, latency, refund clauses) in immutable logic.
- **Blockchain:** Anchors cryptographic commitments of cloud events.
- **Off-Chain Storage:** Stores bulk data cheaply; only hashes go on-chain.
- **Orchestration:** Auto-scales VMs/containers based on smart-contract triggers.
- **Physical:** Real infrastructure providing compute, network, storage.

**SLA Automation via Smart Contracts (2 Marks):**

- Cloud provider publishes real-time metrics (uptime, latency) as signed messages.
- An **oracle** posts these metrics on-chain at fixed intervals.
- The SLA smart contract compares actuals vs. promised; if breach detected, it auto-executes: *(i)* charges penalty, *(ii)* issues refund voucher, *(iii)* notifies client — **without any human intermediary**.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Derive the Cost Equation (2 Marks):**

On a public blockchain, storing $n$ bytes requires invoking the `SSTORE` opcode, which consumes $g_b$ gas per byte. At market gas price $P_{\text{gas}}$ (in ETH/gas, converted to fiat), the total cost is:

$$
C_{\text{on-chain}}(n) = n \cdot g_b \cdot P_{\text{gas}}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical Example with $n = 1$ MB (3 Marks):**

Convert 1 MB to bytes: $n = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes. Ethereum: $g_b \approx 625$ gas/byte, $P_{\text{gas}} = 30$ Gwei = $30 \times 10^{-9}$ ETH. Assume ETH = $3{,}000.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{on-chain}} &= 1{,}048{,}576 \times 625 \times 30 \times 10^{-9} \times 3{,}000 \\
&= 655{,}360{,}000 \times 9 \times 10^{-5} \text{ ETH} \\
&= 58.9824 \text{ ETH} \\
&\approx \$58{,}982.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Hash-Only Pattern (1 Mark):**

Storing only a 32-byte SHA-256 hash costs:

$$
C_{\text{hash}} = 32 \times 625 \times 30 \times 10^{-9} \times 3{,}000 = 1.8 \text{ ETH} \approx \$5{,}400
$$

**Step 4 — Justification (1 Mark):**

Cost ratio = $58{,}982 / 5{,}400 \approx 10.93\times$. Since hash anchoring reduces cost by an order of magnitude **while preserving cryptographic integrity** (SHA-256 collision probability $\approx 2^{-256}$), the hash-only pattern is the **universally adopted** pattern in Blockchain-Cloud architectures.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the Blockchain-AI integration pipeline with a block diagram. Discuss the role of oracles in feeding AI inferences into smart contracts.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Derive a composite trust score for a hybrid Blockchain-Cloud-AI system using a weighted geometric mean. Compute the score for $n = 21$ validators, uptime ratio = 0.999, cert score = 0.95, and 12 audits/year with $\lambda = 0.1$.

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Pipeline Diagram (2 Marks):** Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 — *Data Sources → Provenance Anchor → AI Training → Checkpoint Anchor → Inference → Oracle → Smart Contract → Watchdog → Feedback*.

**Six-Stage Pipeline Explanation (3 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Data Acquisition** — Raw data from IoT/enterprise sources.
2. **Provenance Anchoring** — Each record hashed and timestamped on-chain: $C = H(D \,\vert\vert\, t)$.
3. **AI Training** — Off-chain ML models consume verified data.
4. **Checkpoint Anchoring** — Model version hash anchored on-chain.
5. **Inference & Oracle Posting** — Predictions pushed on-chain via oracles.
6. **Smart Contract Action & Continuous Audit** — Contracts consume inferences; watchdog AI monitors for bias.

**Role of Oracles (2 Marks):**

- Blockchains are **deterministic** and **cannot** natively call external APIs — this is the *"Oracle Problem"*.
- Oracles (e.g., **Chainlink**, **Band Protocol**) are cryptographically signed middleware that fetch off-chain AI inferences and post them on-chain.
- Oracles use **decentralized consensus** among multiple nodes to prevent a single oracle from submitting false data.
- Smart contracts consume these signed inferences **trustlessly**, enabling use cases like *AI-driven insurance payouts* (oracle posts rainfall data → contract auto-pays farmer).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Define Component Trusts (1 Mark):**

$$
T_{\text{BC}} = 1 - f/n, \quad T_{\text{CL}} = 0.7 \cdot U + 0.3 \cdot S, \quad T_{\text{AI}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda A}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Each Component (3 Marks):**

For $n = 21$, $f = \lfloor 20/3 \rfloor = 6$:

$$
T_{\text{BC}} = 1 - 6/21 = 0.714
$$

For $U = 0.999$, $S = 0.95$:

$$
T_{\text{CL}} = 0.7 \times 0.999 + 0.3 \times 0.95 = 0.6993 + 0.285 = 0.9843
$$

For $A = 12$, $\lambda = 0.1$:

$$
T_{\text{AI}} = 1 - e^{-1.2} = 1 - 0.3012 = 0.6988
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Weighted Geometric Mean (2 Marks):**

With $w_b = 0.4$, $w_c = 0.3$, $w_a = 0.3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{hybrid}} &= T_{\text{BC}}^{0.4} \times T_{\text{CL}}^{0.3} \times T_{\text{AI}}^{0.3} \\
&= 0.714^{0.4} \times 0.9843^{0.3} \times 0.6988^{0.3} \\
&= 0.8917 \times 0.9952 \times 0.9079 \\
&\approx 0.806
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 Mark):**

A composite trust score of **0.806 (80.6%)** indicates a **robust hybrid system** suitable for high-stakes applications such as BFSI and healthcare. The weakest link is the AI layer (0.6988), suggesting that **increasing audit frequency** or **adopting more interpretable models** would raise the composite score.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Conflating "Cloud" and "Blockchain" definitions** — Cloud is *centralized*; Blockchain is *decentralized*. Integration gives a *hybrid* model. Writing only the definition of cloud without contrasting it with blockchain = **−2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the "Why hash only?" derivation** — A 7-mark question explicitly asks for *mathematical justification*. Writing only "it is expensive" without showing $C_{\text{on-chain}}(n) = n \cdot g_b \cdot P_{\text{gas}}$ = **−3 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the Oracle Problem** — In Blockchain-AI answers, never claim blockchain can directly call an AI model. Always mention the **Oracle Problem** and middleware like Chainlink = **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Using arithmetic mean for trust composition** — Use the **geometric mean** $T = \prod T_i^{w_i}$, not the arithmetic mean. Arithmetic mean is mathematically incorrect because it does not penalize weak links = **−1.5 marks**.
> 5. **No block diagram in 7-mark architecture questions** — KTU mandates a diagram. Missing it = **−2 marks**, even if text is perfect.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section as a one-page cheat sheet before your exam.**

**🔑 Core Concepts to Memorize:**
- [ ] Blockchain-Cloud = **Trust (BC) + Elasticity (Cloud)** — solves *single-point-of-failure* and *immutability* gaps.
- [ ] Blockchain-AI = **Verifiable data + Auditable intelligence** — solves *black-box* and *data-poisoning* gaps.
- [ ] The **Oracle Problem**: blockchains cannot natively call external APIs; oracles (Chainlink, Band) bridge off-chain AI to on-chain contracts.
- [ ] The **Hash-anchoring pattern**: store bulk data off-chain; anchor 32-byte SHA-256 hash on-chain.
- [ ] The **Six-Layer Architecture**: Application → Smart Contract → Blockchain → Off-Chain Storage → Orchestration → Physical.

**🔑 Key Equations to Memorize:**
- [ ] $C_{\text{on-chain}}(n) = n \cdot g_b \cdot P_{\text{gas}}$ — on-chain storage cost.
- [ ] $C = H(D \,\vert\vert\, t \,\vert\vert\, pk)$ — cryptographic commitment.
- [ ] $T_{\text{hybrid}} = T_{\text{BC}}^{w_b} \cdot T_{\text{CL}}^{w_c} \cdot T_{\text{AI}}^{w_a}$ — composite trust (geometric mean).
- [ ] $T_{\text{BC}} = 1 - f/n$ — BFT validator trust.
- [ ] $T_{\text{AI}} = 1 - e^{-\lambda A}$ — AI audit-based trust.

**🔑 Key Numbers & Constants:**
- [ ] SHA-256 collision probability $\approx 2^{-256}$.
- [ ] On-chain storage cost for 1 MB on Ethereum $\approx \$58{,}982$ (2024 prices).
- [ ] Typical weights for trust composition: $w_b = 0.4$, $w_c = 0.3$, $w_a = 0.3$.

**🔑 Four Integration Patterns of Blockchain + AI:**
- [ ] **AI for Blockchain** (anomaly detection, gas prediction)
- [ ] **Blockchain for AI** (data provenance, model audit)
- [ ] **Smart Contract Oracles** (off-chain inference → on-chain action)
- [ ] **Decentralized AI / DeAI** (federated learning + blockchain)

**🔑 Decision Rubric (Last-Minute Recall):**
- [ ] Need *immutable audit only*? → **Blockchain-Cloud**.
- [ ] Need *intelligent automation*? → **Blockchain-AI**.
- [ ] Need *both audit + intelligence + scale*? → **Blockchain-Cloud-AI** (high-stakes only).
- [ ] Need *neither*? → **Traditional Cloud + AI** (cheapest, but trust gaps remain).

**🔑 Real-World Examples (Use in 3-Mark Answers):**
- [ ] Healthcare: anchored MRI scans + auditable tumor-detection AI.
- [ ] Finance: cloud trading + blockchain settlement + AI fraud detection.
- [ ] Supply Chain: IoT sensors + AI demand forecast + blockchain provenance.
- [ ] Smart Cities: traffic AI on cloud + citizen-data controlled by blockchain SSI.

**🔑 Examiner Pet-Peeves (Avoid These):**
- [ ] Never use the **arithmetic mean** for trust — always **geometric mean**.
- [ ] Never say "blockchain calls AI" — always say "**oracles** bridge AI to blockchain."
- [ ] Never omit the **diagram** in 7-mark architecture questions.
- [ ] Never use Python or programming code in this theory paper — the KTU exam is **descriptive + mathematical**, not coding.
- [ ] Never claim blockchain is "fully anonymous" — it is **pseudonymous** with traceability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voting, Auction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT867 - BLOCK CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Voting, Auction**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Blockchain-Based Voting — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A *Blockchain-Based Voting System* is a decentralized electronic voting protocol in which votes are recorded as cryptographically signed transactions on an immutable distributed ledger, executed and tallied through *self-executing smart contracts* that enforce voter eligibility, prevent double-spending (double-voting), and guarantee universal verifiability without requiring a trusted central authority.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, voting on blockchain is treated as a **decentralized consensus-on-preference** problem — every node (voter) casts a signed message (vote) that is committed to a public ledger where:
- **Authenticity** is enforced via public-key signatures.
- **Integrity** is enforced via Merkle-trees and block hashes.
- **Anonymity** is enforced via pseudonymity, ring signatures, or zero-knowledge proofs.
- **Finality** is enforced via the underlying consensus protocol (PoW, PoS, PBFT, etc.).

## 1.2 Blockchain-Based Auction — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** A *Blockchain-Based Auction* is a smart-contract-mediated negotiation protocol in which bidders submit cryptographically sealed bids to a public ledger; the contract automatically determines the winner and the settlement price based on pre-defined auction rules (English, Dutch, Sealed-Bid, Vickrey) without the need for a human auctioneer or centralized escrow.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Voting Analogy — "The Transparent Glass Ballot Box"
Imagine a **glass ballot box in a town square** that anyone can see through, but no one can tamper with. Each voter places a sealed paper inside, and once a paper enters, it is *permanently bonded* to the box — you cannot remove or alter it. Furthermore, every citizen holds a *unique wax seal* (private key) that only they possess, proving the paper came from them. At the end, the box *automatically* shakes itself and counts the votes. This is exactly what a blockchain voting system does — the chain is the glass box, the wallet's private key is the wax seal, and the smart contract is the automatic counter.

### Auction Analogy — "The Locked, Self-Closing Shop"
Imagine an auction house where the auctioneer's podium is replaced by a **robot**. The robot's only job is to (1) accept sealed bids, (2) open them all at a precise deadline, (3) announce the highest bidder, and (4) automatically transfer the item and money. The robot cannot be bribed, cannot be turned off mid-auction, and remembers every bid forever. The blockchain auction is this robot — the smart contract.

## 1.4 Key Design Metrics (in Bold)

The following metrics are **explicitly tested** in the KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination):

- **Universal Verifiability** — anyone can verify that all valid votes/bids were correctly tallied.
- **Eligibility Verifiability** — only authorized participants can vote/bid.
- **Privacy / Anonymity** — the link between a voter/bidder identity and their choice is cryptographically hidden.
- **Receipt-Freeness** — a voter cannot prove to a third party how they voted (prevents vote-buying).
- **Coercion-Resistance** — a coercer cannot force a voter to cast a specific vote.
- **Fairness** — no partial results are revealed before the deadline (critical for sealed-bid auctions).
- **Atomicity** — settlement of payment and transfer of asset (or vote finalization) happens *all-or-nothing*.
- **Finality** — once a vote/bid is included in a block, it cannot be reversed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Module 4 specifically demands understanding of *Self-Tallying Voting*, the *Open Vote Network (OVN)*, *Boardroom Voting*, and the cryptographic primitives that make these systems privacy-preserving.

---

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Voting Protocol Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

A blockchain-based voting system follows a strict four-phase lifecycle. Understanding each phase is essential for KTU's 14-mark long-answer questions.

### Phase 1: Setup (Off-chain, done by the Election Authority — EA)
1. The EA generates a **public/private key pair** for the election.
2. A list $\mathcal{V} = \{v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n\}$ of eligible voters is published.
3. A Merkle tree of eligible voter public keys $pk_i$ is constructed; the Merkle root $R$ is committed on-chain.
4. A smart contract $S_E$ is deployed to the network containing:
   - Election parameters (deadline $T_d$, candidates $\mathcal{C}$, voter list root $R$).
   - Tally function $T(\cdot)$ that converts encrypted votes to plaintext tallies.

### Phase 2: Vote Casting (On-chain, anonymous/pseudonymous)
1. Voter $v_i$ constructs their encrypted ballot $b_i = \text{Enc}(pk_E, c_j)$ where $c_j \in \mathcal{C}$ is their chosen candidate.
2. Voter $v_i$ signs the encrypted ballot with their private key: $\sigma_i = \text{Sign}(sk_i, b_i)$.
3. The signed ballot is sent as a transaction: $\text{tx}_i = (b_i, \sigma_i, pk_i)$.
4. The smart contract $S_E$ validates:
   - $pk_i$ is in the Merkle tree (eligibility).
   - $\sigma_i$ is a valid signature over $b_i$ (authenticity).
   - $v_i$ has not voted before (one-vote rule via a *nullifier set*).

### Phase 3: Tallying (Smart Contract Execution)
1. After deadline $T_d$, no more ballots are accepted.
2. The smart contract iterates through all valid ballots $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$.
3. If homomorphic encryption is used: $B_{\text{sum}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} b_i$, then $T = \text{Dec}(sk_E, B_{\text{sum}})$.
4. Final tallies for each candidate are written to chain storage.

### Phase 4: Verification (Public, Any-Time)
1. Any observer downloads the full vote list $\{b_i, \sigma_i\}_{i=1}^n$.
2. Re-validates signatures and Merkle proofs.
3. Re-executes the tally function.
4. Compares the result with the on-chain final tally.

## 2.2 The Auction Protocol Lifecycle (Step-by-Step)

### Phase 1: Asset & Contract Deployment
1. Seller deploys an **ERC-721 (NFT)** or ERC-20 token representing the asset.
2. Seller deploys the auction smart contract $S_A$ with parameters:
   - Asset contract address.
   - Bidding rules (English / Dutch / Vickrey).
   - Minimum bid increment $\Delta b_{\min}$.
   - Auction deadline $T_d$.
   - Reserve price $P_{\text{res}}$.
3. Seller transfers the asset to $S_A$ (escrow).

### Phase 2: Bidding
1. **English Auction**: Bidders call `bid()` with increasing $b_i > b_{i-1} + \Delta b_{\min}$. Highest bid wins.
2. **Dutch Auction**: Price decays $P(t) = P_{\max} - \alpha(t - t_0)$. First bidder to call `buy()` wins at $P(t_{\text{buy}})$.
3. **Sealed-Bid Auction**: Bidders submit $b_i = \text{Enc}(pk_A, \text{amount}, \text{address})$. Commitments revealed after $T_d$.
4. **Vickrey Auction**: Same as sealed-bid, but winner pays the *second-highest* price.

### Phase 3: Settlement
1. Smart contract transfers asset to winner.
2. Smart contract transfers winning payment to seller.
3. Losing bids are refunded atomically (using `transfer` or `withdraw` pattern).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for voting/auction problems. It is the single most important block for last-minute revision before KTU ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Description | Key Constraint |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | Merkle Root of Voter Set | $R = H(H(\ldots H(pk_1 \vert\vert pk_2) \vert\vert \ldots) \vert\vert H(pk_n))$ | Compact commitment to eligible voters | $R$ stored on-chain |
| 2 | Nullifier for Double-Vote Prevention | $n_i = H(sk_i \vert\vert \text{electionID})$ | One nullifier per voter per election | $n_i \in \mathcal{N}_{\text{used}}$ set |
| 3 | Homomorphic Vote Aggregation | $B_{\Sigma} = \bigoplus_{i=1}^{n} \text{Enc}(pk_E, v_i)$ | Tally via additive homomorphism | $\text{Dec}(sk_E, B_{\Sigma}) = \sum v_i$ |
| 4 | Self-Tallying Ballot Verification | $\text{Verify}(B_i) = \prod_j (B_i[j])^{sk_i} = g^{v_i \cdot H(\text{ID})}$ | Each ballot reveals tally in exponent | ElGamal pairing variant |
| 5 | English Auction Highest Bid | $b_{\text{win}} = \max_{i \in [n]} b_i$ | Winner is highest bidder | $b_{\text{win}} \geq P_{\text{res}}$ |
| 6 | Dutch Auction Price Decay | $P(t) = P_{\max} - \alpha(t - t_0)$ | Linear price decrease | $P(t) \geq P_{\text{res}}$ |
| 7 | Vickrey (2nd-Price) Settlement | $p_{\text{settle}} = \max_{j \neq i^*} b_j$ | Winner pays 2nd-highest bid | $p_{\text{settle}} \leq b_{\text{win}}$ |
| 8 | Block Finality (PoW) | $P_{\text{final}} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z}{k} q^k (1-q)^{z-k}$ | Probability of reversal after $z$ confirmations, $q$ = attacker hash power | For $q = 0.1$, $z = 6 \Rightarrow P_{\text{final}} \approx 0.999$ |
| 9 | Ballot Signature Verification | $\text{Verify}(pk_i, b_i, \sigma_i) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{true}$ | Authenticity check | ECDSA over secp256k1 |
| 10 | Auction Settlement Atomicity | $\text{Tx}_{\text{final}} = \text{transfer}(\text{asset}, w) \wedge \text{transfer}(ETH, s)$ | Either both happen or neither | Solidity `require` / Reentrancy guard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When writing the Dutch Auction price formula, **never use a vertical bar inside the table** — use `P(t) = Pmax - alpha(t - t0)` or render as LaTeX outside the table.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Why Blockchain? |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| Government Elections | National, State, Municipal e-voting | Removes trusted tallying authority |
| Corporate Governance | Shareholder voting (Boardroom, Aragon) | Immutable proxy voting |
| DAO Governance | Token-weighted proposals (MakerDAO, Uniswap) | Censorship-resistant execution |
| NFT Marketplaces | OpenSea, LooksRare (English auctions) | Trustless escrow + settlement |
| Spectrum Auctions | FCC-style auctions (e.g., FCC's CBRS) | Public, auditable bid history |
| Sealed-Bid Procurement | Government tenders, supply chain | Bid confidentiality + fairness |
| Domain Name Auctions | ENS (Ethereum Name Service) | Decentralized registrar |
| Carbon Credit Auctions | Climate markets | Prevents double-spending of credits |

---

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Homomorphic Encryption Solves Private Tallying

The central problem of blockchain voting is the apparent contradiction:
> *"We want public verification (anyone can count), but private ballots (no one sees individual votes)."*

**Plain-text tallying breaks privacy. Plain-text ballots break verifiability.** Homomorphic encryption resolves this.

Let $\mathcal{C} = \{c_1, c_2, \ldots, c_k\}$ be the set of $k$ candidates. Voter $v_i$ wishes to cast a vote for candidate $c_{j_i}$. We encode this as a one-hot vector:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i = (v_i^{(1)}, v_i^{(2)}, \ldots, v_i^{(k)}) \in \{0,1\}^k
$$

where $v_i^{(j)} = 1$ if $v_i$ votes for $c_j$, and $0$ otherwise. The sum over all voters is:

$$
\mathbf{T} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{v}_i = (T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_k)
$$

Each $T_j$ is the total votes for candidate $c_j$. We want $\mathbf{T}$ to be publicly computable, but each individual $\mathbf{v}_i$ must remain private.

Using an additively homomorphic encryption scheme such as **Paillier** or **exponential ElGamal**, voter $v_i$ encrypts each component:

$$
\mathbf{b}_i = (E_{pk}(v_i^{(1)}), E_{pk}(v_i^{(2)}), \ldots, E_{pk}(v_i^{(k)}))
$$

By the additive homomorphism property of the cryptosystem, the smart contract can compute the encrypted aggregate:

$$
B^{(j)} = E_{pk}(v_1^{(j)}) \oplus E_{pk}(v_2^{(j)}) \oplus \ldots \oplus E_{pk}(v_n^{(j)}) = E_{pk}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i^{(j)}\right) = E_{pk}(T_j)
$$

The election authority (or a threshold group) holds the private key $sk$ and decrypts:

$$
T_j = D_{sk}(B^{(j)}) \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, k
$$

**Result:** The on-chain transaction $B^{(j)}$ reveals *zero* information about individual votes, yet the final tallies are cryptographically bound to the sum. This is the **core insight** behind Maci's, Civitas, and Open Vote Network.

## 3.2 Derivation: Self-Tallying Voting (Open Vote Network — OVN)

The OVN eliminates the trusted tallier entirely. The derivation below shows how a single vote contributes a publicly verifiable tally.

Consider a pairing-friendly elliptic curve $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ with generator $g$ and bilinear pairing $e : G_1 \times G_2 \to G_T$. Let $H : \{0,1\}^* \to G_1$ be a hash-to-curve function.

**Voter $v_i$** with private key $sk_i \in \mathbb{Z}_q^*$:
- Public key: $pk_i = g^{sk_i}$.
- The vote is encoded as $v_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$.
- The voter computes the ballot:

$$
B_i = (g^{r_i}, \, pk_i^{r_i} \cdot H(\text{electionID})^{v_i})
$$

for a random nonce $r_i \in \mathbb{Z}_q^*$.

**Anyone (post-deadline) can compute the tally** by multiplying the right components:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{right}_i = \prod_{i=1}^{n} pk_i^{r_i} \cdot H(\text{electionID})^{v_i}
$$

Since the discrete logarithm is hard, individual $v_i$ cannot be extracted, but the *aggregate exponent* of $H(\text{electionID})$ is computable via a Pollard's lambda / baby-step-giant-step search over the small space $[1, k]$:

$$
H(\text{electionID})^V = \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{right}_i}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} pk_i^{r_i}}
$$

where $V = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i$ is the total. The verifier then brute-forces $V$ over $[n, nk]$ (a tractable space), recovers $V$, and any party can publish the per-candidate tallies by identifying which integers $V_c$ are consistent.

**Why is this "self-tallying"?** Because every voter $v_i$ knows $sk_i$, they can compute:

$$
\text{right}_i^{sk_i^{-1}} = g^{r_i} \cdot H(\text{electionID})^{v_i \cdot sk_i^{-1}}
$$

Using the pairing:

$$
e(g^{r_i}, \, H(\text{electionID})^{v_i \cdot sk_i^{-1}}) = e(g, H(\text{electionID}))^{r_i \cdot v_i \cdot sk_i^{-1}}
$$

Each voter's contribution is independently verifiable, yet the global tally requires all ballots to be aggregated. This is the foundation of **Boardroom Voting** and the **Open Vote Network**.

## 3.3 Derivation: Vickrey Auction Settlement Mechanics

Consider $n$ sealed bidders with bids $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$ sorted in descending order:

$$
b_{(1)} \geq b_{(2)} \geq \ldots \geq b_{(n)}
$$

In a **Vickrey (2nd-Price) auction**:
- Winner: bidder with bid $b_{(1)}$.
- Settlement price: $p_{\text{settle}} = b_{(2)}$.

**Economic justification (derivation of incentive compatibility):**

Suppose bidder $i$ has a true valuation $v_i$. The utility of bidding $b_i$ when others bid $b_{-i}$ is:

$$
U_i(b_i, b_{-i}) = \begin{cases} v_i - p_{\text{settle}} & \text{if } b_i = b_{(1)} \text{ and } b_i \geq b_{(2)} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

- If $b_i < v_i$: bidding $b_i$ yields $U_i = v_i - b_{(2)}$ if you win, or $0$ if you lose. You could increase your bid and still win at price $b_{(2)}$ — strictly better to bid truthfully.
- If $b_i > v_i$: bidding $b_i$ might win at price $b_{(2)} > v_i$, yielding negative utility. You should lower your bid until $b_i \leq v_i$.
- Therefore, **truthful bidding ($b_i = v_i$) is a dominant strategy**.

This is the celebrated **revenue equivalence theorem** in mechanism design.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Voting Protocols

| Protocol | Privacy Mechanism | Tally Authority | Receipt-Freeness | Coercion-Resistance | On-Chain Cost |
|----------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------|---------------------|---------------|
| **Plain Voting** | Pseudonymity | Central | ❌ | ❌ | Low |
| **Open Vote Network** | Discrete log + Pairing | Self (anyone) | ❌ | ❌ | Medium |
| **Boardroom** | BLS Signatures | Self | ❌ | ❌ | Low |
| **MACI (Minimal Anti-Collusion)** | zk-SNARKs | Smart Contract | ✅ | Partial | High |
| **Civitas** | Mix-nets + Threshold | Trusted | ✅ | ✅ | Very High |
| **zkVoting (e.g., zkDAO)** | zk-SNARKs | Smart Contract | ✅ | ✅ | High |
| **Agora** | Homomorphic + Threshold | Threshold | ❌ | ❌ | Medium |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Auction Types

| Auction Type | Bid Visibility | Winner Pays | Use Case | Suitability on Blockchain |
|--------------|----------------|-------------|----------|---------------------------|
| **English (Open Outcry)** | Public, real-time | Highest bid | NFTs, art, collectibles | ✅ High (gas-efficient) |
| **Dutch (Descending)** | Public price, hidden acceptance | Current price at moment of buy | Perishable goods, initial token offerings | ✅ High (single taker) |
| **Sealed-Bid (1st Price)** | Hidden until reveal | Highest bid | Procurement, tenders | ⚠️ Medium (needs commit-reveal) |
| **Vickrey (2nd Price)** | Hidden until reveal | 2nd highest | Digital advertising, keywords | ⚠️ Medium (needs commit-reveal + zk) |
| **Continuous Double Auction (CDA)** | Order book | Matched bid-ask | DEX trading, exchanges | ✅ High (Uniswap-style AMM) |

---

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Blockchain Voting Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EA[Election Authority] --> S1[Phase 1: Setup]
    S1 --> MR[Compute Merkle Root R of eligible voters]
    MR --> SC[Deploy Smart Contract SE on-chain]
    SC --> R[(Public Ledger)]

    V1[Voter v1] --> B1[Encrypt ballot bi = Enc pkE cj]
    V2[Voter v2] --> B2[Encrypt ballot bi]
    V3[Voter vn] --> B3[Encrypt ballot bi]
    Vn[Voter vn] --> Bn[Encrypt ballot bi]

    B1 --> S1A[Sign with ski]
    B2 --> S2A[Sign with ski]
    B3 --> S3A[Sign with ski]
    Bn --> S4A[Sign with ski]

    S1A --> V[Phase 2: Vote Casting - Smart Contract Validates]
    S2A --> V
    S3A --> V
    S4A --> V

    V --> C1{Eligible?}
    C1 -- No --> RJ[Reject]
    C1 -- Yes --> C2{Already voted?}
    C2 -- Yes --> RJ
    C2 -- No --> ST[Store ballot + add nullifier]

    ST --> R

    T[Phase 3: After Deadline Td] --> AG[Aggregate encrypted ballots homomorphically]
    AG --> DC[EA decrypts aggregate]
    DC --> FT[Phase 4: Final tally written to chain]

    R --> FT
    FT --> VR[Any observer verifies]
```

## 4.2 Smart Contract Architecture for a Vickrey Auction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Seller] --> D[Deploy Auction Contract SA]
    D --> E[Escrow Asset ERC-721 to SA]
    D --> P[Publish Parameters: Td, Pmin, Pmax]

    B1[Bidder 1] --> C1[Commit Phase: H bid, address, nonce]
    B2[Bidder 2] --> C2[Commit Phase: H bid, address, nonce]
    B3[Bidder 3] --> C3[Commit Phase: H bid, address, nonce]

    C1 --> CH[Smart Contract stores commitment]
    C2 --> CH
    C3 --> CH

    CH --> W[Wait for Deadline Td]

    W --> RV[Reveal Phase: Bidders submit bid, address, nonce]
    RV --> CK{Contract checks H bid, address, nonce == commitment}

    CK -- Mismatch --> FOR[Forfeit Bid]
    CK -- Match --> SRT[Sort bids descending]

    SRT --> W1[Winner = b1 highest]
    SRT --> S2[Settle price = b2 second highest]

    W1 --> TR1[Transfer Asset to Winner]
    S2 --> TR2[Transfer Payment to Seller]
    TR1 --> RF1[Refund b1 - b2 to Winner]
    TR2 --> RF2[Refund other bids]
```

## 4.3 State Machine: Lifecycle of a Blockchain Auction

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Created
    Created --> BiddingOpen: deploy + escrow
    BiddingOpen --> BiddingOpen: valid bid received
    BiddingOpen --> RevealPhase: deadline Td reached
    BiddingOpen --> Cancelled: seller cancels
    RevealPhase --> RevealPhase: valid reveal
    RevealPhase --> Settled: all reveals complete
    RevealPhase --> Settled: timeout with default
    Settled --> [*]
    Cancelled --> [*]
```

## 4.4 Architecture: zk-SNARK Privacy Layer for Voting (MACI-style)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Voter] --> CI[Coordinator Issues Credits]
    CI --> PV[Post Vote = Encrypted Ballot]
    PV --> ZK[Generate zk-SNARK Proof]
    ZK --> BC[Submit to Blockchain]
    BC --> PR[Process via zk-Rollup]
    PR --> TR[Final Tally Decrypted Off-chain]
    TR --> VT[Anyone verifies zk-Proof]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between First-Price Sealed-Bid Auction and Vickrey (Second-Price) Auction. Which one is incentive-compatible and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | First-Price Sealed-Bid | Vickrey (Second-Price) |
|---------|------------------------|------------------------|
| Winner Pays | Own bid (highest) | Second-highest bid |
| Information | Bids hidden until reveal | Bids hidden until reveal |
| Dominant Strategy | Bid shading (less than valuation) | **Truthful bidding** |
| Incentive Compatibility | ❌ Not IC | ✅ IC (dominant strategy) |

In a Vickrey auction, bidding one's true valuation $v_i$ is a **dominant strategy** because: (1) if you underbid, you might lose a profitable auction; (2) if you overbid, you risk paying more than your valuation. Therefore, the Vickrey auction is **strategy-proof and incentive-compatible**. *(Award full 3 marks for the table + 1-line justification.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**What is a Self-Tallying Voting System? List two of its key advantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **self-tallying voting system** is a voting protocol in which the tally is publicly computable by any observer directly from the ballots, without the need for a trusted tallying authority. Each ballot contains a cryptographic structure (e.g., ElGamal pairings) such that the product over all ballots' right-components yields the encrypted aggregate of all votes, which can then be decrypted using only public information.

**Two key advantages:**
1. **Trust elimination** — No central authority can manipulate the count; the math decides.
2. **Universal verifiability** — Any third party can recompute the tally from on-chain data and confirm correctness.

*(Award 1.5 marks for the definition and 1.5 marks for the advantages.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of a blockchain-based voting system with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Election Authority, smart contract, and Merkle tree in ensuring eligibility, integrity, and verifiability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the Open Vote Network (OVN) protocol in detail. Explain how the discrete logarithm assumption and bilinear pairings are used to enable self-tallying. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Architecture of a Blockchain Voting System (4 marks):**

The architecture consists of three primary components:

1. **Off-chain Layer (Election Authority — EA):**
   - The EA generates the key pair $(pk_E, sk_E)$ for the election.
   - Compiles the eligible voter list $\mathcal{V} = \{v_1, \ldots, v_n\}$.
   - Constructs a Merkle tree where each leaf is the hash of a voter's public key $H(pk_i)$. The Merkle root $R$ is published on-chain.

2. **On-chain Layer (Smart Contract $S_E$):**
   - Deployed with the Merkle root $R$, candidate list $\mathcal{C}$, and deadline $T_d$.
   - Validates every incoming transaction:
     - Merkle proof verification of $pk_i$ against $R$ (eligibility).
     - Signature verification $\text{Verify}(pk_i, b_i, \sigma_i) = 1$ (authenticity).
     - Nullifier check $n_i \notin \mathcal{N}_{\text{used}}$ (one-person-one-vote).
   - Executes the tally after $T_d$.

3. **Public Verification Layer (Anyone):**
   - Downloads the full list of ballots.
   - Independently re-verifies and re-tallies.
   - Compares with the on-chain final result.

**Roles (3 marks):**

| Component | Property Ensured | Mechanism |
|-----------|------------------|-----------|
| Election Authority | Issuance of credentials | Threshold key generation |
| Merkle Tree $R$ | Eligibility verifiability | O(log n) Merkle proof |
| Smart Contract $S_E$ | Integrity + atomicity | Deterministic, unstoppable code |
| Public Ledger | Universal verifiability | Immutable ballot history |

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EA[Election Authority] -->|Generates keys pkE skE| SC[Smart Contract SE]
    EA -->|Builds Merkle Tree| MR[Merkle Root R]
    MR -->|Stored on-chain| SC
    V1[Voter] -->|Signed Encrypted Ballot| SC
    V2[Voter] -->|Signed Encrypted Ballot| SC
    SC -->|After Td| TL[Tally Result]
    TL --> OB[Public Verifier]
    SC --> OB
```

**Award: 4 marks for components + 3 marks for diagram and property mapping.**

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Open Vote Network (OVN) — Detailed Protocol (4 marks):**

The OVN, proposed by Hao, Zielonka, and Foy, is a self-tallying voting protocol with the following structure:

**Setup:** A pairing-friendly elliptic curve $E(\mathbb{F}_p)$ with generator $g$ and pairing $e : G_1 \times G_2 \to G_T$ is chosen. A hash function $H : \{0,1\}^* \to G_1$ is fixed. Each voter $v_i$ generates private key $sk_i \in_R \mathbb{Z}_q^*$ and public key $pk_i = g^{sk_i}$.

**Voting:** Voter $v_i$ encrypts their choice $v_i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ as:

$$
B_i = \left( g^{r_i}, \quad pk_i^{r_i} \cdot H(\text{electionID})^{v_i} \right)
$$

for a random $r_i \in_R \mathbb{Z}_q^*$. The voter signs $B_i$ with $sk_i$ and submits it on-chain.

**Tallying:** After deadline, the sum of the right components is:

$$
R_{\Sigma} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} pk_i^{r_i} \cdot H(\text{electionID})^{v_i} = \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} pk_i^{r_i}\right) \cdot H(\text{electionID})^V
$$

where $V = \sum v_i$. The product $\prod pk_i^{r_i}$ can be computed from publicly available information (each voter's $r_i$ is included in the ballot's left component). Therefore:

$$
H(\text{electionID})^V = R_{\Sigma} \cdot \left(\prod_{i=1}^{n} pk_i^{r_i}\right)^{-1}
$$

**Why discrete log assumption matters (1.5 marks):**
Recovering $V$ from $H(\text{electionID})^V$ requires solving the discrete log problem. However, $V$ is bounded in $[n, nk]$ (a small range for typical elections). Pollard's lambda algorithm runs in $O(\sqrt{nk})$, which is tractable.

**Why pairings matter (1.5 marks):**
Pairings allow independent verification of each voter's contribution:

$$
e(g^{r_i}, H(\text{electionID})^{v_i}) = e(g, H(\text{electionID}))^{r_i v_i}
$$

This lets any third party verify that the ballot was constructed correctly without revealing $v_i$. The bilinear property enables *individual verifiability* without *individual privacy leakage*.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe in detail the different types of blockchain-based auctions (English, Dutch, Sealed-Bid, Vickrey). Compare their suitability for on-chain implementation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the Dutch Auction mechanism used in token offerings. Derive the price-decay formula and explain how a smart contract enforces fairness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**English Auction (1.5 marks):**
- Open-outcry, ascending-price format.
- Bidders publicly outbid each other in real-time.
- Winner: highest bidder; Pays: highest bid (1st-price).
- Blockchain suitability: **High**. Each `bid()` call is a public transaction; the contract simply tracks `currentLeader` and `currentBid`. Gas-efficient. Used by OpenSea, eBay-style.

**Dutch Auction (1.5 marks):**
- Descending price starting from $P_{\max}$.
- First buyer to accept the current price wins.
- Winner: first taker; Pays: current price at moment of buy.
- Blockchain suitability: **High** for single-asset sales. Used in initial token offerings, Gnosis auction. Single transaction settlement.

**Sealed-Bid (1st-Price) Auction (1.5 marks):**
- Bids submitted secretly; all revealed simultaneously.
- Winner: highest bidder; Pays: own bid.
- Blockchain suitability: **Medium**. Requires commit-reveal scheme (two-phase) to ensure fairness. Without it, bidders could withhold high bids and copy winners. ZK-SNARKs or hash commitments are used.

**Vickrey (2nd-Price) Auction (1.5 marks):**
- Same as sealed-bid, but winner pays 2nd-highest bid.
- Blockchain suitability: **Medium**. Same commit-reveal complexity. **Incentive-compatible**: bidders submit true valuations. Used for keyword auctions (Google Ads style).

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Auction | On-Chain Complexity | Trust Required | Privacy |
|---------|---------------------|----------------|---------|
| English | Low | Minimal (smart contract) | Public bids |
| Dutch | Low | Minimal | Public price, hidden acceptance |
| Sealed-Bid | Medium | Commit-reveal + zk | Full |
| Vickrey | Medium | Commit-reveal + zk | Full |

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Dutch Auction for Token Offerings (3 marks):**

A Dutch auction is used to determine the **market-clearing price** of a token. The seller wants to sell a fixed quantity $Q$ of tokens. The price starts at $P_{\max}$ (above expected clearing) and decays linearly (or exponentially) until total demand $\geq Q$.

**Price Decay Derivation (2.5 marks):**

Let $P(t)$ be the price at time $t \geq t_0$, where $t_0$ is auction start. The decay is linear:

$$
P(t) = P_{\max} - \alpha(t - t_0)
$$

where:
- $P_{\max}$ is the starting price (set by seller).
- $\alpha > 0$ is the decay rate (tokens/time).
- The auction ends when $P(t) = P_{\min}$ (the floor price).

The total tokens sold if the auction runs for $T = (P_{\max} - P_{\min})/\alpha$ is $Q$. The clearing price $P_c$ is determined by total bidder demand:

$$
D(P) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \cdot \mathbb{1}[v_i \geq P]
$$

where $d_i$ is the number of tokens bidder $i$ wants at price $\leq v_i$. The smart contract sets:

$$
P_c = \min \{ P : D(P) \geq Q \}
$$

**Smart Contract Fairness Enforcement (1.5 marks):**

The auction contract $S_A$ enforces fairness through:
1. **Atomic settlement** — the contract collects all accepted bids at price $P_c$ and distributes tokens pro-rata to the supply $Q$.
2. **Pre-commitment** — bidders lock their bid amount in escrow (`deposit()` call). If $v_i < P_c$, the bid is fully refunded.
3. **No front-running** — the price is a function of block.timestamp, not of individual bid timing, preventing MEV bots from front-running the auction.
4. **Transparency** — all bids are public post-auction, allowing the community to verify the clearing price.

**Real-world example:** Gnosis Protocol Dutch Auctions, MakerDAO MKR auctions for collateral recovery, and Telegram's aborted GRAM token offering all used variants of the Dutch auction mechanism.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU ESE on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the OVN with a normal ElGamal scheme** — the OVN's key innovation is that the tally can be computed by *anyone*, not just the EA. Failing to state this loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the nullifier mechanism** — without it, the same voter can vote multiple times. Always include `n_i = H(sk_i || electionID)` in your answer.
> 3. **Mixing up Vickrey and English auctions** — Vickrey is *sealed-bid with 2nd-price*; English is *open-outcry with 1st-price*. Examiners deduct 1 mark per confusion.
> 4. **Writing the Dutch price formula as $P(t) = P_{\max} - \alpha t$** — must explicitly use $(t - t_0)$ for partial credit.
> 5. **Forgetting the Merkle root's role** — the root $R$ is what makes eligibility publicly verifiable; omitting it loses 1.5 marks.
> 6. **Not stating the discrete log assumption** — when explaining homomorphic tallying or OVN, the security of the scheme rests on the hardness of discrete log. Without this, the answer is incomplete.
> 7. **Ignoring commit-reveal in sealed-bid auctions** — examiners will deduct 2 marks if you don't mention the two-phase commit-reveal protocol to prevent bid-copying.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU ESE Prep):**

- **Voting is a 4-phase process:** Setup → Cast → Tally → Verify. Memorize this.
- **Self-tallying = no trusted authority** for counting. OVN is the canonical example.
- **Homomorphic encryption** allows encrypted votes to be summed without decryption. Paillier (additive) and ElGamal (multiplicative) are the two relevant schemes.
- **Merkle root $R$** of voter public keys is committed on-chain to enable $\mathcal{O}(\log n)$ eligibility proofs.
- **Nullifier** $n_i = H(sk_i \| \text{electionID})$ prevents double-voting without revealing identity.
- **Universal verifiability** = anyone can verify the final tally. **Eligibility verifiability** = only authorized voters cast ballots. **Privacy** = no link between voter and choice.
- **English auction** → 1st price, open-outcry, ascending. (OpenSea model)
- **Dutch auction** → $P(t) = P_{\max} - \alpha(t - t_0)$, first-taker wins. (Gnosis model)
- **Sealed-bid 1st-price** → own bid paid. **Sealed-bid 2nd-price (Vickrey)** → 2nd-highest paid, IC dominant strategy.
- **Commit-reveal scheme** is essential for on-chain sealed-bid auctions to prevent bid-copying.
- **Atomicity** of settlement: asset transfer and payment transfer must succeed together (Solidity `require` / Checks-Effects-Interactions).
- **Block finality** formula $P_{\text{final}} = 1 - \sum_{k=0}^{z} \binom{z}{k} q^k (1-q)^{z-k}$ — used when a vote is "confirmed" only after $z$ blocks.
- **Pairing-based cryptography** enables individual ballot verifiability in OVN: $e(g^{r_i}, H(\text{ID})^{v_i}) = e(g, H(\text{ID}))^{r_i v_i}$.
- **Real-world systems to mention:** MACI, Boardroom, Aragon, Open Vote Network, Gnosis, OpenSea, Uniswap V3 (CDA).
